Title: The Princess Knight
Author: Cait Jacobs
My Review:
This is the tale of a princess determined to prove she's more than what people assume she is—namely, that she's not weak and shallow like her parents, but rather capable of being strong, a warrior, and working for what she wants. Things may not turn out quite like she intended in the beginning, but she definitely proves that through the course of the story, and I was cheering her along the whole way.

This was such an easy, charming, engrossing read. It flew by in two sittings. Considering I've been clawing my way out of a hardcore reading slump the last couple of weeks, that's saying something. I've had some read disappointments in new releases lately, and it was such a relief that I adored this one like I did.

I loved the messiness of the romance that wasn't quite as messy as it could have been (in the best way). I love the rivalries turned friendships. I love the friendships turned acquaintances turned back to friendships. I loved the animal companion. I loved the use of sewing for both characterization and plot. I loved the lore and the build-up. I loved the found family.

My only semi-complaint is that there were so many names of people and places to keep straight, and I swear half of them started with C. I eventually just resigned myself to hoping the story would guide me enough that I wouldn't need to remember them, and I don't think my enjoyment suffered for it. I really hope to god I won't be expected to remember them for the sequel. (Because there's going to be a sequel, right? RIGHT????)

I received a free digital copy from Netgalley in exchange for an honest review.

Title: Zomromcom
Author: Olivia Dade
My Review:
This is a book that actually delivers what the cover and blurb say it's going to: a rom-com about a human and a vampire trying to stop a zombie-related disaster. It's very quirky, at times humorously vulgar, and full of jokes and one-liners worthy of highlighting in your kindle. If ongoing Disney-related jokes about a vampire who is unfortunately named Gaston are your jam, this might be for you.

Unfortunately, while there were good elements to this book, it ultimately didn't work great for me. Given the fast pace at which the plot unfolded, I just didn't buy the superspeed romance, especially when their interactions were 75% quips and sex vs 25% meaningful getting to know one another. Even when they had vulnerable personal moments, it didn't feel earned. But oddly, that wasn't even my biggest issue with the book.

That was the wiiiiild world building. Even with the map at the beginning and many words used up on descriptions of zones and gates and moats, it was extremely confusing. On the one hand, there are publicly known supernatural people and the danger of zombie attacks, both for the last 20 years. But then things like Etsy and OF and social media influencers still exist, and other than some (dumb) things like there being vampire blood available in the supermarket, the world still seems to function like ours, in a pop culture and social sense. I can't wrap my head around it. The book would bring up some fancy cheese they were eating, and all I could think was, "Why? HOW? They're fighting freaking zombies inside a walled city! Why was that a priority in this world?" (Not in the sense of the people eating it when it was there, but why there was a market for it in the first place? If I'm in a zombie apocalypse city, I don't care about bloody caviar and YouTube algorithms!) I'm pretty good about suspending disbelief and going with the flow when I'm enjoying a book, so the fact that I couldn't just smile and nod past it was a sign that I wasn't vibing with the story in general. Cutting in half the number of supernatural creatures, the gratuitous side characters, and the pop culture references would have been a great start to evening out what felt like a kitchen sink kind of story.

2.5 stars rounded up despite my annoyance, because I got a few good laughs at the Gaston renditions.

I received a free copy from Netgalley in exchange for an honest review.

Title: Ocean's Echo (Winter's Orbit, #2)
Author: Everina Maxwell
My Review:
I put off this book for what feels like forever, because I had certain expectations for it from [b:Winter’s Orbit|57693520|Winter’s Orbit|Everina Maxwell|https://i.gr-assets.com/images/S/compressed.photo.goodreads.com/books/1639424622l/57693520._SY75_.jpg|72867360], and I was really afraid of it not living up to them. I'm happy to report that, while it didn't quite hit all of the high points that the first book did, I really enjoyed this installment and am glad I've now read it.

I loved the setup for this story, with the two mains forced in an untenable situation and doing their best to survive it. I liked each of them for different reasons—Tennal for his restless energy and don't-give-a-fuck attitude that was tempered with a good heart and an inability to hurt the people he cared about, and Surit for his strong moral compass, care for his family, and ability to see when rules needed to be bent for the greater good. Their story covered a lot more ground than I expected, and I found myself truly tense and unsure how things were going to turn out for them and then truly impressed at how they resolved things. I found myself realizing some of the twists about half a page before they happened, and that was such a satisfying feeling.

I only had two real complaints, but they feel pretty nitpicky considering how much I otherwise enjoyed the story.

First, the way Tennal's reader abilities manifested while helping with navigation and, similarly, the way syncing worked in the end battle with Oma was confusing and, at times, longwinded in its beautiful and complex description. Not bad, but I found myself having to reread and still not following anything more than the gist, and that happening for multiple pages in a row.

Second, I didn't find the romance particularly satisfying. Don't get me wrong—I thought they had great chemistry and I was rooting for them, but it just didn't get enough page time. This felt much more like a hopeful ending than a happy one. I can still get behind it, but after being very satisfied with the romance in the previous book, it was a letdown.

4.5 stars, rounded up, because what else am I supposed to do with a great book that I read in one sitting?

Title: Hexmate (Widdershins Supernatural Academy #1)
Author: Blake R. Wolfe
My Review:
The Instagram algorithm followed by an internet rabbit hole got me to this book, and I'm not mad about it. Is it a new favorite? Nah. Did I enjoy my time with it? Yep.

I really liked the character dynamics, with Atlas being strong, sweet, and protective, but not overbearing, and Caden being shy and hesitant, but coming into his own as the story progressed. However, the plotline around prejudices between witches and other magical species was heavy handed, and the romance development was pretty shallow. They barely have small talk before they're in love, and the villains were of the mustache twirling sort. I was along for the pleasant ride, but it didn't rock my reading world.

3.5 stars rounded down, because it wasn't quite deep enough to get the four-star treatment.

Title: Roll for Romance
Author: Lenora Woods
My Review:
The story of an out-of-work corporate gal who moves in with her best friend, joins his D&D campaign, and then proceeds to fall in love with another newbie player is right up my alley. I'm an in-work corporate gal, but I met my husband over a game of D&D (though he's the artist, in our case), so I can hardly help it.

This story is sorry of a leveled up (pun intended) Hallmark tale. New York City girl in small town Texas who learns the meaning of following her dreams, all that. It was structured as a fairly even split (maybe 60/40) between real life and the narrated adventures of her cleric character, and that definitely added to its charm. I found myself sucked into both sides of the tale. It's all pretty low stakes, though, so there's no big twists, revelations, drama, out any of that. Not every story needs it, but I admit I prefer something that toes the line between this and angst. It was cute and fun while I was in it, and that's all I can ask. Probably not a reread candidate for me, but then you never know.

I've never been in game where the players of dead characters had to leave the table, but I suppose it's plausible some DMs might want that level of drama. I do regularly equate life happenings to dice rolls, so her likening of her job interview to D&D skill rolls was relatable. Overall, I enjoyed how the game was represented, and particularly how it grew friendships between the group members.

I would definitely read from this author again.

Title: The Final Girl Support Group
Author: Grady Hendrix
My Review:
Unreliable and unlikeable narrators make me anxious. I'm not sure if it's waiting for the other shoe to drop when I find out what secrets they're holding back or for the moment when they annoy me so much that I can't continue reading, but they keep me on the edge of my seat in a not entirely pleasant way. This one made up for it by being so darn readable that I couldn't stop. It helped that classic slasher films are a staple in my household, so I was able to really appreciate the references to their doubly-fictionalized counterparts. There were so many names and titles that I'm not entirely sure I got them all straight with one another, but I got the gist, and it was entertaining.

I did not like Lynnette, but I could empathize with the person she was. Following her train of thought was exhausting, but fascinating. I'm not sure that there were enough (or any) clues dropped that it would have made it possible to figure out the twist with tangible proof, but it was believable enough that I can let it fly. (It's also possible I'm rusty and just don't care to figure things out, lest it ruin my reading experience, so take that for what you will.)

This was my first Grady Hendrix, and it will not be my last. 4 stars.

Title: Morning Light
Author: Abigail Reynolds
My Review:
I finished this book and sat down to write a review that talked somewhat scathingly about how little this was "inspired" by Persuasion. And then I realized this is book 2 following [b:The Man Who Loved Pride & Prejudice: A Modern Love Story with a Jane Austen Twist|7283722|The Man Who Loved Pride & Prejudice A Modern Love Story with a Jane Austen Twist (The Woods Hole Quartet #1)|Abigail Reynolds|https://i.gr-assets.com/images/S/compressed.photo.goodreads.com/books/1302724785l/7283722._SY75_.jpg|34282731], and things made so much more sense. I wondered why the heck those characters were getting their own POV chapters! This is what I get for grabbing a physical book off my shelf on a whim without checking goodreads first.

I still think this has little-to-nothing to do with Persuasion. But that honestly wasn't even my biggest gripe. It was that the whole first half of this thing was built on one of the WORST uses of the miscommunication trope I've read in a long time. Easily a hundred pages would have been unnecessary or at least entirely different if Annie had said, like, 10 words to Jeremy at a crucial point early in the book. Anne Elliot, she is not.

2.5 stars, but I'm rounding up instead of down because coming into this after reading the other book probably would have helped my enjoyment a little. I did really like Calder and Cassie.

Title: Fortune and Fate (Twelve Houses, #5)
Author: Sharon Shinn
My Review:
As a story that exists outside the main continuity of the Twelve Houses series, I wasn't sure whether I cared about it at all. I put this book off for years, and I'm glad I did. I think if I'd come into this straight from the previous book, it would have been a let down. Wen is fine as a main character, but there wasn't a whole lot happening here. There are so many chapters of mundane happenings as characters to get to know one another and it was fine but lacked the suspense and other engaging qualities of the main series. Her story would have been a decent novella.

HOWEVER. A big chunk of chapters were from my beloved characters' points of view (mostly Senneth), serving as a sort of epilogue to the main series, and THAT was worth the price of admission. I will never be sad to spend a little more time with those six.

Title: Valkyrie Rising (Valkyrie #1)
Author: Ingrid  Paulson
My Review:
Really about a 4.5, but I rounded up out of love. :-)

I found this book sort of at random, and last night I was looking for something a little different to read, so I decided to give it a try. And BOY am I glad I did! I would have zipped through it all in one sitting if it hadn't been relatively late when I started it. So let's see...how to describe my love....

The story was fun, and I loved the concept. In a book market so saturated with vampires, werewolves, and angels, Valkyries were a nice change. Being set in Norway was a fun change, even if it made the names of places basically unpronounceable for me. But the best part about this book was the characters. I LOVED them. If they stay consistent the way they were here, I'd read any book the author wants to throw at me about them.

Ellie/Elsa/Ells was the kind of main character I like to read about. She has real insecurities, but she's not a doormat, and she doesn't make her decisions solely based on the boy she likes. She put actual thought into her actions, so even when she wasn't sure who to trust, or she was keeping secrets, it never dragged on and on where I was saying, "Just tell him already!!!" The romance was super believable, had a great build up, and Tucker was just an awesome character. Super swoon-worthy. It was obvious that he had his insecurities, too, which he tried to hide behind verbal sparring and teasing flirting...but ultimately he was honest with Ellie and he treated her well. Even Ellie's brother, Graham, was a great addition to the story, even though he wasn't present as much. He felt very believable, rather than just like a cookie-cutter character who was put there to annoy Ellie and not actually grow and change with the story.

I only had one small qualm, and it's the reason I deducted half a star. It only loses half, because by the time this came around in the story, I was already so addicted to the characters that it almost didn't matter. But it needs a mention, all the same. 3/4 through the story, we learn that Tucker also has some distant Valkyrie blood. Now, I could see a few spots where there was supposed build-up, so it wasn't that the author wasn't trying, but it still felt a little out of the blue to me. And a little too convenient. It didn't break the story for me, but I did notice it, and it's never a good thing when I get pulled out of a story like that.

All in all, though, I love love loved this book. I'm mostly just sad that it's brand new, which means any theoretical sequels won't be coming out for at least a year. Boo.

PS - Am I the only one who pictured Tom Hiddleston every. single. time. Loki made an appearance, regardless of what he was supposed to look like at the time? Hehe.

Title: Bluebells in the Mourning
Author: KaraLynne Mackrory
My Review:
Reread 10/15/16

I've read quite a bit more JAFF since the first time I read this book, and it occurs to me now that there are a ton of stories where Mr. Bennet dies, but not many where one of the sisters dies instead. It makes for a very different feel, where the family has to mourn, but they aren't in immediate danger of poverty. It's quieter with that lack of danger, but sometimes I'm really in the mood for that. The Wickham storyline is pretty satisfying, too.

Original read 10/11/15

I enjoyed this book a great deal. The writing was really good, and it probably would have been 4.5 or 5 stars if there was more new about it. A lot of material from the original was re-purposed to fit the new continuity. It was definitely used in creative ways, so bravo for that, but I would have liked it even better if the divergence explored more new territory.

Of the things that were new, though, my favorite was definitely Elizabeth's friendship with Georgiana. Georgie is consistently one of my favorite secondary characters, so anyone who can bring her into the story more and use her well is on my YES list.

I do have to admit that when Mr. and Mrs. Bennet reconciled, I was sort of hoping that they'd end up having a son. Glad the author did not disappoint. ;-)

Recommended as an enjoyable way to pass an afternoon.

Title: I Think I'm in Love with an Alien
Author: Ann Aguirre
My Review:
I saw this title and this cover, and what I really wanted was Garrus Vakarian in a rom-com with a side order of Roswell, New Mexico. What I got was...sort of that.

This book follows two characters, Jen and Tam, who met in forums about aliens and took their interactions into a group chat with four other members. They've never met in person until now, where they've all decided to attend a Roswell-esque alien-related convention together. (No idea if this is a real one or not, and I didn't care enough to research.) The obvious catch is that Tam is an alien who's gotten stuck on Earth and none of his friends know about it, not even his human crush, Jen.

The first ~50% of the book was decent, if a bit saccharine in its internal effusions of interest between the main pair. Getting to know the friend group was interesting, and I liked the interspersed group chat sections in idea, if not necessarily in practice. I've been in a lot of online chats with a wide variety of geeky people, and they just didn't feel like real people talking. "Stilted" is the best word I've got for it, though that's not quite it. And that's even when you remove the wooden formal chatter of the alien members. The little peeks of convention culture were fairly surface level, but fun. I enjoyed seeing Tam navigate life on Earth and interactions with humans.

Things started to fall apart for me once the action moved away from the convention. The only things happening were entirely mundane and largely uninteresting. Oh no, Jen's mother and sister are assholes, but in boring, bitchy ways. Oh look, this is what a picnic is. Oh look, Tam made friends with the cat. The parts I enjoyed most were insights into alien culture, planets, Tam's travels, that sort of thing. If the book had been more about that and less about the lackluster romance, I could probably have added a star.

But let's talk about the romance. I appreciate the ace rep here and that, while they existed in a fashion, sex scenes weren't the focus of this book. However. I didn't even believe their chemistry as friends, much less as people in love. Every conversation felt like they were tiptoeing around one another, having first date get-to-know you sessions (months into knowing one another), or being infuriatingly sappy and understanding. Like, sign me up for reasonable adults who have adult conversations and work things out rather than have misunderstandings (which, unfortunately factored into it, too), but it all felt so juvenile, like life problems can be solved every time by a, "They don't deserve you. You're wonderful."

The worst offender, by far, was the dumb storyline involving some guys from the convention who (correctly) claim to have seen real aliens in attendance. It was fine-ish during the convention segment of the story, but that ending?? What even was the point? It was a fitting ending that Tam decided to stay (or would have been, if I cared about those two at that point) and that Ravik got to go home in his place, but I could have done without the 11th hour wild goose chase for Jen after she wandered off into the woods. Again, why? It was such filler conflict.

I'm really sad I didn't like this one more, but I'll always be on the lookout for my Garrus fix. Until then, I'll stick with Mass Effect fan fiction.

I received a free review copy through netgalley.

Title: He's to Die For
Author: Erin                Dunn
My Review:
This book was not what I expected. In my case, that was a good thing, but if someone comes in expecting what that cartoon cover and those comedic comp titles are putting down, I can see them being disappointed. Me, I prefer it leaning more into its serious side, so I was happy. It's funny in places, but quietly so, and cute in others, but only in the sense that the characters are cute with each other. Mostly, this is a solid procedural mystery centered around a musician and his trauma, investigated by a detective who's drawn to said musician when he probably shouldn't be.

Rav Trivedi, the detective, is reasonable, witty, competent, personable, genuine, only occasionally snobbish. Jack Vale, the musician--and I use this term deliberately instead of "rock star" because the latter has a vibe to it that I think is inaccurate for this book--radiates sensitive artist, but not in a way that feels stereotypical, and I love the contrast of his competence against Rav's. The mystery and investigation are interesting and thoroughly intertwined with the way the two get to know one another and beyond. I appreciated that, even though there are some misunderstanding and challenges, they are ultimately both adults about it all. They felt like people I'd like to be around, and that made me root for them even more. Everything wraps up in a satisfying way with enough of a denouement bow that I was grinning as I turned off my ereader.

Probably more like 4.5 stars, but well worth rounding up.

I received an advanced reader copy in exchange for an honest review.

My Review: I've enjoyed everything I've read by Elizabeth Adams, so I can't say I'm surprised I liked this one. The concept was really interesting, and it was a rare book where I was still invested in and intrigued with Darcy's story despite it taking a while for Darcy and Elizabeth to actually meet. Colonel Fitzwilliam's viewpoint was prominent and added color to the story that might have been missing without him. I really enjoyed his relationship with Darcy. The small changes with Wickham were interesting. My only complaint (and my reason for dropping a star) is that I was expecting more conflict before Darcy and Elizabeth could settle things between them. Their storyline was good, but things were too smooth to truly get up to that five-star level. It was a very short book, though, so I can forgive it.

Title: Of Fortune's Reversal: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: Don   Jacobson
My Review: This was a series of narrative events with very little soul. The doctors had better chemistry with Kitty's head wound than Kitty had with Fitzwilliam. Such a shame, too, because I like the setup a lot. I would have DNFed if it wasn't so short.

Title: Bonded by Thorns (Beasts of the Briar, #1)
Author: Elizabeth Helen
My Review: This is hard for me to rate. I love a Beauty and the Beast retelling, so I was really enjoying things for a good chunk of the story. But as it went on, it started to feel far less like UST and far more like a hot-and-cold (pun somewhat intended) mess. Also I hated the ending. <spoiler>Nothing was resolved, and that's the worst kind of cliffhanger.</spoiler> There's still a tiny possibility I could continue and read the sequel, out of curiosity more than anything else, but I'm not sure. Right now I'm just glad I didn't buy all those pretty physical copies I've been eyeing. I guess it's about time I learned that lesson, huh?

Title: Secret Fire (Uniform Encounters #1)
Author: Morticia Knight
My Review: These characters had the emotional maturity of fifteen-year-olds, going hot and cold through awkward, clunky, heavy handed, and superficial interactions. I wish all the attempts at outside suspense through the fires they were investigating had been left out, because there was no actual mystery or suspense. Their personal issues were boiled down to one having a previous bad relationship and one previously not having any serious ones. The misunderstanding that fueled the quasi third-act breakup was just dumb and cliched. (I only finished this because I needed it for a challenge for The Amazing Readathon.)

Title: Six of Crows (Six of Crows, #1)
Author: Leigh Bardugo
My Review:
Considering how this book has been talked up over the years, I wasn't sure it could hold up. But everyone talks about how much better it is than the [b:Shadow and Bone|37834723|Shadow and Bone (Shadow and Bone, #1)|Leigh Bardugo|https://i.gr-assets.com/images/S/compressed.photo.goodreads.com/books/1519072763l/37834723._SY75_.jpg|15093325] trilogy, and I really liked that by the end, so I figured the hype couldn't be too far off. I also saw the first season of the S&B Netflix series, so I already had something of an introduction to the crew, however altered, and the only thing that really threw me was how young they were in contrast. I'm very much at that point in my life where YA protagonists don't feel as relatable, but most of the time I was picturing their aged-up selves from the show, and that worked in my favor.

I don't think I can provide much intelligent commentary, as is often the case with five-star books. I loved the characters and sharp edges mixed with depth. I was on the edge of my seat through the plot. This was both everything that I expected and everything that I didn't. I'm still reeling from that cliffhanger ending, and now I'm not sure what I want more: to immediately jump into the sequel or go watch season two of the show (RIP season three).

Title: A Charmed Christmas (The Charm Offensive, #1.5)
Author: Alison Cochrun
My Review: I'm aware that it's cheesy and stereotypical <spoiler>to end a romance novel with an engagement or wedding</spoiler>, but this was the exactly epilogue I needed for the main book.

Title: Dead Silence
Author: S.A. Barnes
My Review:
I've been craving some sci-fi horror lately, though I couldn't explain why, since I've experienced the genre only in movies. Maybe I've just seen a bunch of books around that look intriguing. Regardless, this was my first stop on that journey, and considering I had no idea what to expect from it, I'm pleased.

There were times the POV character got a little too trapped in her head, in a repetitive way, and the middle section dragged a bit, but nothing that killed my enjoyment of the story. I wasn't expecting things to go the way they did, but I enjoyed the characters, the plot, the big reveal, all of it. I'll definitely read another by this author.

Title: The Charm Offensive (The Charm Offensive, #1)
Author: Alison Cochrun
My Review:
I don't particularly enjoy reality TV or TV dating shows, and I'm really picky about contemporary romances with no hint of magic or other trappings, but damn if this book didn't latch it's claws in me tonight. It came highly recommended, so I assumed I'd enjoy it, but I didn't know I'd like it quite as much as I did. This evening was the very definition of being "lost in a book" because I barely noticed I was reading. I read it all in one sitting. I have some trouble suspending belief in that ending, knowing that world we live in, but also I wish I could have gotten more from it.

I understand why Dev had to leave the way he did, both for his character and for the plot, but it still felt jarring. Despite the lovely reunion, the big break lost some of the tension and emotional intensity, and I don't feel that it was quite recaptured. There weren't enough pages to really let them talk it out, even if the public nature of their confessions would have allowed it. They'd had such a lovely, meaningful connection before that I couldn't help but be a little disappointed with how it came together. It was good, but not quite great.

Title: Drowned Country (The Greenhollow Duology, #2)
Author: Emily  Tesh
My Review: Emily Tesh excels at making me feel things in very few words. This story had a happy ending for basically all characters, but 90% of it was steeped in so much melancholy and sadness that it almost didn't feel like a happy ending. Silver was just struggling so much with his existence and loneliness and reconciling his past to his future. It was so well done, but if I reread this, I will make sure I have plans to read something happier straight after it.

Title: The Brightness Between Us (The Darkness Outside Us, #2)
Author: Eliot Schrefer
My Review:
When I read it, I wrongly assumed [b:The Darkness Outside Us|55200663|The Darkness Outside Us (The Darkness Outside Us, #1)|Eliot Schrefer|https://i.gr-assets.com/images/S/compressed.photo.goodreads.com/books/1602485938l/55200663._SX50_.jpg|83111737] was a standalone. Upon learning about this sequel, I questioned how it could possible live up to the first book, which surprised, horrified, intrigued, and ultimately sucked me in. I'm trying to get better about reading sequels, though, so I pushed through and (for once) actually picked this up shortly after finishing the first. I read a few chapters from the new POV and put it down, not because it wasn't good, but because I missed the POV from book one.

I read a few other things, then I finally circled back here. (Still much quicker than usual, so good job me!) I got to just over the 50% mark, and that's when I realized I'd severely underestimated the power of this book to give the first one a run for its money. Much like the first book, I hadn't seen the pieces as they were being put into play, or at least hadn't understood their significance. I was suddenly hooked, enthralled--and it didn't hurt that it turns out we also get some Ambrose/Kodiak POV after the setup phase.

I blazed through the rest of this in a couple of reading sessions. I'm putting almost no information about the plot here, because I'm glad I went in blind. My advice: trust that the author knows what he's doing.

Overall, this one landed around a 4.5. Not quite as spectacular as the first, but that was a hard bar anyway. I still loved it and will be thinking about this series as a whole for a long time. I'm sure I'll reread it sometime after the plot starts to get murky in my head.

Title: Artificial Condition (The Murderbot Diaries, #2)
Author: Martha Wells
My Review: I didn't think it was possible for me to like Murderbot even more, but here we are. He can't help but try to do his job well, but all he wants is to be alone with his media. He's me. Except any murdering I do is entirely theoretical and imaginary.

Title: Silver in the Wood (The Greenhollow Duology, #1)
Author: Emily  Tesh
My Review: This was an odd, beautiful little book. The perfect palate cleanser. I love fairy tales or anything with those vibes, and this fit the bill. It did its job respectably in the page count it had, many times making me feel far deeper than I would expect for a novella. The fact that it could do that much in a hundred pages is the thing that makes me disappointed there weren't another hundred.

My Review:
This book was extremely well done. I enjoyed Emma just fine, despite feeling little connection and sympathy with the title character. That was not the case here. I loved George Knightley far more than I expected. His relationship with his brother, with Dr. Hughes and Spencer, with his tenants and steward--they all gave a picture of a man I'd very much like to know. I also really enjoyed seeing Emma from his perspective. Though I still can't say I loved her, I at least appreciated her a little more. His slow realization of his love for her was fabulous.

My only real complaint is that this was split into two books. It seems like an odd choice to me, since it's an incomplete story this way. But at least the second one is available through kindle unlimited. I'm definitely going to read it, though I might have to give myself a break from Emma in general for a bit.

Title: Generation Ship
Author: Michael Mammay
My Review: Well, that was a book. It was very readable, and chapters went by pretty quickly. That said, when I wasn't reading it I wasn't exactly looking forward to coming back to it. There were a bunch of POV characters, and even for the ones I somewhat liked, I didn't actually care about them. The stakes kept changing, so even if I cared what happened to them, I'm not sure it could have held me in suspense. I was curious about what was happening, but there was very little real tension. It was a lot of politicking and discussions, and it took forever to finally get to the planet and figure out what was happening. I felt very underwhelmed by the ending, though there wasn't anything objectively wrong with it. It was just boring.

Title: A Strange and Stubborn Endurance (The Tithenai Chronicles, #1)
Author: Foz Meadows
My Review:
This book has been tangentially on my radar for a bit, but after hearing it mentioned multiple times this week, I took it as a sign that I needed to read it. Boy, am I glad that I did. It's got political intrigue, social commentary, romance, and a pretty solid mystery plot, too. My only real complaint was the number of names and titles thrown around that were very difficult to keep straight, given that they were not made up of known words (or known to me, anyway). I stopped trying, after awhile, but I have a feeling I missed some of the mystery plot threads in there because of it.

However. Velasin and Caethari? Perfection. I know enemies-to-lovers is all the rage right now, but give me acquaintances-to-friends-to-lovers any day. They actually TALKED about things, never getting hung up on miscommunication, and yet the pining was still immaculate. Their relationship build was beautiful and subtle, and I loved it.

This is probably 4.5 stars, but I'm so charmed that I rounded up.

Note: There are big trigger warnings in this one. On page sexual assault. Two on-page suicide attempts. Death of an animal.

My Review:
I could see the fan fic all over this story, and I mean that in the absolute best way possible. I'm 100% this book's target audience. I liked the way Parker and Adam came together and got to know one another in the face of the zombie apocalypse. Some parts moved a bit too quickly and could have used some time to really solidify me in the world and with the situations they faced, but the fast pace was still pleasant to read. Their struggles never lasted long enough for me to really worry, and that overall hurt the depth of the story, but made it less angsty overall. Definitely not a cozy read, by any standards, but it leaned more into the action movie side than the horror movie side.

Very good, and I will be continuing the series.

Author: Sienna Sway
My Review:
I don't know how they managed to sell me on a romance that included kidnapping, forced marriage, and only a few days of screen time, but here we are. I enjoyed the hell out of this. I think some of it was the contrast of the subject matter with the characters. It's mostly a clash of cultures and a bunch of misunderstandings at the root of it, and Saar is just so sweet and and sincere and almost naive. I'm glad that he and Alex are going to feature in the sequels so their story can continue beyond those few days. It was insta-love, for sure, and I can't always jibe with that, but in this case it drew me in completely.

5 stars for the pure fun of it.

Glad to know it's supposed to be a series, too, because I will absolutely carry on.

Title: The Hurricane Wars (The Hurricane Wars, #1)
Author: Thea Guanzon
My Review:
Why is it so difficult to rate books that you enjoy despite many flaws? 3 stars feels too low and 4 stars feels too high, but I'm not even sure having half stars would solve my inner conflict. It's really just that I liked some things a lot and some things annoyed me, and they feel like very separate things that can't be blended into a rating. But here we are.

There are parts of this book that were a slog. I went along willingly, because I really wanted to like it. The narration was far too wordy and overdone for my taste, everything from needing half a dozen adjectives in each sentence to SO many named secondary and sideline characters, many of them with titles that made them too similar to other characters. I can appreciate wanting to delve into cultures in a meaningful way, but there was simply too much for the page count to be enjoyable. I found myself really annoyed at every new name or descriptor or term. Some things leaned into development of the world, but at a point it felt excessive--a place where "kill your darlings" could have really come in handy.

I also have to admit that I am not an enemies-to-lovers girl. I don't hate the trope. I really want to like it, even. I like slow burns and tension, but for some reason the enemy element of it gets under my skin. I often find it difficult to be patient with the characters as they circle one another, and this book was no exception. It probably didn't help that very little was resolved between them by the end. It wasn't the kind of cliffhanger that I hate (thank GOD), because enough was resolved with the plot itself to feel like an ending was justified, but they were right back at each others' throats, and I find that annoying. I know the cracks have started to form, but I just read 400+ pages, and I want more than that! I can recognize that this is outside my usual wheelhouse and give it some grace. The push and pull of tension was well done, even when it was playing into the trope's conventions.

I really like the magic system and am intrigued to see more in the next book. It doesn't hurt that I can see the Star Wars influences all over it. I could definitely go for more of a Luke Skywalker and Mara Jade enemies-to-lovers kind of storyline, but I won't hold my breath. I enjoyed the court politics and the fact that, while it was mentioned, it didn't get bogged down in the "getting trained to be a princess" parts. I'm curious to see how things may play out later with certain key individuals who could shape up to be allies but could also go the other way.

I haven't read the blurb for the next book to know what it's focusing on, but I have some assumptions (going back to the continent for the Night Empress coronation) that give me anxiety. Alaric's father is terrifying. I'm definitely going to read it at some point (currently annoyed that I can't get a paperback until January 2026).

Also - question only to myself, so please do not spoil me: Is Vela Alaric's mother, or am I reading too much Star Wars into this?

Title: Quantum Cage
Author: Davis Bunn
My Review:
Quantum Cage is the story of a man joining a top secret team partially made up of old friends to mind meld with aliens while navigating dangers on Earth and on that other planet. In the relatively small count of pages it had, it accomplished a complete story arc with a satisfying conclusion. There was a twist I didn't quite see coming that was really well done. I really enjoyed the aliens and the transits and everything about that storyline. Weirdly, it was the human things that detracted from it. As a whole package, though, it wasn't bad, and I'm not mad I read it.

3.5 stars, rounded down for reasons I'll detail below.

I had some issues with it, though. For a short novel, there were parts that were unnecessarily repetitive (far, far too many scenes wasting sentences on descriptions of meals, for one). I found the narrative dragging in places, and I chalk that up to delaying information for the mystery of it, which ultimately just made things more confusing. I'm no physicist, certainly, but there were places where description of physics (or dreams, or alien society) felt bulked up with fluff, like a student might do when trying to hit the word count on an essay. Descriptions were long, but vague, sometimes nearly incomprehensible. Early on, I assumed it was to draw out the big reveals, but it wasn't something that lessened as the story progressed.

I started out really wanting to know more about Darren's family and his strained relationship with these old friends, but ultimately they didn't really matter much. Despite being told about his grief for his wife, I didn't feel it. I actually kind of hated her for forcing his separation from his friends for what felt like a really stupid reason. But unfortunately, the friend storyline didn't really give me what I wanted, either. They each had their own little story and/or backstory (Barry and his hotel sale, Tanaka and his good deeds), but they didn't really matter. Despite the story opening up with Darren and Barry (for faaar too long leading up to the alien reveal, which I knew from the blurb), Neil ended up being the most consequential to the plot. It all left me feeling like the friends could have been mashed into one old best friend to make for a much tighter story. For a little while, I thought there being four of them would be consequential, matching to the four aliens, but instead we got the three scientists filling that role. Such a missed opportunity.

I received an advanced reader copy in exchange for an honest review.

Title: Tigana
Author: Guy Gavriel Kay
My Review:
I've been wanting to finish a book by this author for a very long time, after multiple aborted attempts to read [b:The Summer Tree|104086|The Summer Tree (The Fionavar Tapestry, #1)|Guy Gavriel Kay|https://i.gr-assets.com/images/S/compressed.photo.goodreads.com/books/1412531529l/104086._SY75_.jpg|3238632]. I can't explain why I haven't been able to get through it, except perhaps it just wasn't the time or the mood. I'm hoping now that I've finally popped my GGK cherry, I can go back to it and persevere.

Overall? Really enjoyed this. It was well crafted but imperfect. Definitely dated, but I expected that. The plot flowed smoothly. There were a LOT of characters that I sometimes had trouble keeping straight, but after awhile I just stopped trying, and it was fine.

There were some plot elements that made me uncomfortable, particularly the prince's binding of a wizard, but the author called this out in an afterward that was on the audiobook as kind of the point--and I do think that the text at least attempted to deal with the moral quandary of it. It was similar for the fight to bring back Tigana in general. A few characters latched onto the cause with perhaps too-sudden fierceness, but I was able to go with it.

The "romance" was pretty insta-lovey and kind of bland, to the point that I almost wish it wasn't there. But attempts were made, so *shrug*. I could have done without the incest backstory, though. Yes, it added some tension, but considering how it impacted the ending (i.e., not really at all), tension could have been accomplished without it.

My favorite part was all the maneuvering and how the pieces fell together in the end. A little too convenient at times? Sure. But really satisfying all the same. There were a couple of secrets there at the end that were and weren't shared, and I think the author made the right choice with them. I was really wondering how those elements would wrap up, and I wasn't disappointed.

I listened to the audiobook, and Simon Vance is a king among narrators. Highly recommend him regardless of the book, and this was no exception.

I liked this author's writing style, and the story itself was decent enough. I did have a bit of trouble keeping the "nice" alphas straight, while West stood out simply for his different attitude, but it wasn't too bad. BUT. This book was really short, and it ultimately felt more like a tease of a story than a complete narrative. There are other books in this series, but I'm not fond of books where the plot feels chopped off. The only real resolution in here was more of a character thing than a plot thing. Title: Sacrifice (Bloodline Vampires, #1)
Author: Katee Robert
My Review:
This book was...fine? Like, I definitely give it some kudos for not ending on a cliffhanger (something I loathe in general that seems to be common among poly books intending to push the reader to a sequel). The story was interesting enough, but it was pretty surface level. It was basically just enough to string together the sex scenes without it being substance-less smut, so I have to give it credit for that. The sex scenes were...fine? Nothing to write home about, nothing to cringe over (assuming graphic sexytimes doesn't make you cringe in general). The character development is minimal at best, which again, not the worst for this sort of book. I think my biggest gripe is that there was hardly any tension at all. Conflicts were ignored for the sake of sex until the ignoring turned into accepting or resolving—therefore they might as well not have existed in the first place.

If it had been any longer, I might not have made it through the book, but at this length it was enjoyable enough, and I'm not mad I read it.

Also, there were vampires, which helped.

Solid 3 stars.

Title: Void Dancer (Tales from the Gemstone Kingdoms #4)
Author: Amanda Meuwissen
My Review:
I am happy to report that I enjoyed this way more than book 3, but, sadly, not as much as book 2. The first two books had the advantage of being set in areas with a level of forced proximity, which worked to their advantage. Opening the stories up to the greater kingdoms made it more difficult for me to suspend disbelief. In this case, that was me rolling my eyes when Enzo said, "SURE, random guy with amnesia I just found in a tunnel, come stay with me (because you're hot)!" It's a little easier for me to roll with it when they're in a curse castle/city and don't have much choice but to be around one another.

Maybe I'm less in the mood for this style of story than when I started this series, but wow, were some of the romantic scenes cheesy. Also, if I had to read Enzo calling Cullen "fair" one more time, I was going to scream. The whole rebellion/royalty plotline was a bit too deep for a story this short and fluffy, so some bits made me roll my eyes. It was the most cliched version of that plot that it could have been, but I'm not sure it would have had the space to be anything else.

It wasn't bad, though, as much as it sounds like I'm complaining a lot. I really did enjoy reading this. The characters from the previous books are still there, but they didn't feel quite as intrusive as they did in book 3. It also made far more sense to have them around because of their prior connection to Cullen, and I did really like the through-line on his plot. It was a nice pay-off from where things started in book 2. Enzo was a good character who I liked quite a lot (though could have done without so many references to his height - I get it, he's half-dwarven, really I do) and particularly his inventions. There was just enough page time to delve into his history and his feelings on it in a satisfying way, which I really liked.

If I reread this series, I'll probably stick to the first two books...but I'm not mad I read this.

3.5 stars rounded up.

Title: Fourth Wing (The Empyrean, #1)
Author: Rebecca Yarros
My Review:
I went into this out of curiosity about the hype, ready to drop it after a few dozen pages when I found it annoying. Obviously, that is not what happened. Nope. Instead, I read it all in one sitting. Thank goodness for New Year's Day off from work to compensate for the late night.

First, let me state for my own sanity that the deathtrap they call a school is completely stupid and illogical. Culling the "weak" ones with death makes no sense. Even taking the humanity of it out of the equation--just fail them and send them to do the menial jobs no one else wants or something! At least then you aren't wasting valuable human resources. I knew just from hearing the description that it was stupid, and that didn't change in reading it.

That said: disbelief suspended and moving on.

I guessed all of the major plot twists, but I'm not mad about it. The journey was entertaining, even if I already mostly knew the destination. This thing had flaws that I can't deny, but it was such an entertaining ride that I can't even deduct stars for it. Also, I'm a sucker for a good rebellion plot. What can I say?

This gets thrown around under the label of a romantasy, but I really think it's just a fantasy with a romance subplot. The plot didn't hinge on the romance. It gave me serious [b:Shadow and Bone|37834723|Shadow and Bone (Shadow and Bone, #1)|Leigh Bardugo|https://i.gr-assets.com/images/S/compressed.photo.goodreads.com/books/1519072763l/37834723._SY75_.jpg|15093325] vibes, but with an adult edge.

And dragons. Did I mention dragons? I freakin' love dragons. I'm pretty sure this series is going to put me in the mood to revisit [b:His Majesty's Dragon|28876|His Majesty's Dragon (Temeraire, #1)|Naomi Novik|https://i.gr-assets.com/images/S/compressed.photo.goodreads.com/books/1376392909l/28876._SY75_.jpg|726205], and I'm not mad about it.

5 stars. I can't even be ashamed that the hype got me. I only wish I'd caved sooner.

Title: Knife Children (The Sharing Knife #4.5)
Author: Lois McMaster Bujold
My Review:
I was a little afraid it had been too long since I read the main books in this series for me to enjoy this one, but I shouldn't have been. I fell back in with the world, cultures, and magic system very easily. It helped a lot that Barr needed to explain everything to Lily. That repetition might have been annoying if I'd come straight into this from the series, but I'm my case it was a welcome refresher.

This was a much quieter story, with very little of the fear and excitement from the main books, but there was just enough of a taste of patrolling and malices to spice it up a little. Mostly, it was father and daughter learning about one another and finding different levels of acceptance. I wouldn't want a whole series of it, but it was good for a novella.

My only disappointment was not getting to see Dag and Fawn, despite the tease of it.

This whole series was a solid 4-star one for me, of the sort that I recognize the flaws but still very much enjoy revisiting the world. This novella fit right in perfectly.

Title: Somewhere Beyond the Sea (Cerulean Chronicles, #2)
Author: T.J. Klune
My Review:
This book really played with my emotions. It lulled me into a false sense of security by revisiting beloved characters and then sank solidly into a middle that was tense and anxiety inducing while still allowing those characters to shine in their unique ways. Seeing the kids a little more grown up was heartwarming, and I was just as proud of them as Arthur and Linus were.

Did I have a few quibbles with this tale? Sure. The kids were maybe a little too insightful at times, and the inspector was a bit of a cookie-cutter villain of a social worker...though I haven't entirely decided if the latter was intentional or not. Either way, while I noticed these things, they didn't stop me from enjoying the hell out of this book. You could give me a dozen short stories detailing the kids' Saturday adventures and nothing else, and I'd read them. I was here for the family, and I'm not disappointed.

Probably more like 4.5 stars to respect the superiority of the first book (Arthur's journey was more heart-wrenching, but less about the kids and more about himself--hence my preference for Linus's), but it's getting rounded up. Obviously. This is my third TJ Klune book and my third time rating one five stars. Am I surprised? Not really. I've come to learn over the 1300+ pages of his I've read that I really jibe with his writing style. Considering I've now collected his entire backlist, I hope it stays that way!

Title: Sorcery and Small Magics (The Wildersongs Trilogy, #1)
Author: Maiga Doocy
My Review:
AAAAAAH I had no idea this was the first in a trilogy, and now I'm SO sad that I'm done and can't read any farther. This is why I never read series when they're new. This was SO GOOD. I loved the characters, the magic system, the plot, everything. It scratched a fantasy itch I didn't even know I had. I need the next two books RIGHT NOW.

I listened to the audiobook, which was very well done. (All in one sitting, I might add.) I'm not always a fan of books in first person, but Leo's POV was the perfect blend of sassy, unreliable, and earnest. He was a troublemaker without being cruel, and it made his rivalry with Grimm very satisfying, both in where it starts and how it changed as the story progressed.

I would like to give this 6 stars, but I guess I'll stick with the usual 5.

PLOT SUMMARY SPOILERS (for when I inevitably forget everything before book 2):

Leo is someone who creates/scribes spells, not casts them, but he can't do "grand" spells. Only basic ones. (He can do some magic through playing music, but it's not an accepted magic form, so he downplays it.) He's is on probation at the academy, both feeling like him being there is pointless but that he has to make it through so that he can get his inheritance, which includes his dead mother's estate where he spent much of his childhood. He gets paired up with his enemy/rival, Grimm, in a class and antagonizes him for fun, giving him spells to do that he knows Grimm will hate. Between classes one day, he literally runs into Grimm's former scribe partner and accidentally picks up a spell the other dropped. Grimm inadvertently chooses this spell in class and casts it on Leo. They think it failed at first (thinking it's a different spell), but eventually realize it's a compulsion spell of some kind, forcing Leo to do anything Grimm says and also making him want to be in proximity to Grimm all the time. They work with the other scribe to try to break the very illegal spell, but no dice.

Everyone leaves for a holiday break, and the effects intensify, leading to Leo becoming very ill and acting out at a party his father is hosting. He confides in his BFF, and she helps him travel to where Grimm is helping his family with harvest out in the country. After some shenanigans (including healing an infected field with a magic song that Grimm sings, unexpectedly) and convincing, they go to these dangerous woods to pursue a sorcerer hiding in the woods that they think may be the author of a lot of countercharms. It's very dangerous because of monsters and poisonous things, so they get a guide. She turns out to be a member of a splinter group of sorts, and they barely escape with their lives.

They manage to get to the witch in the woods and spend several days with her. She reveals that the curse is not compulsion, but a love spell. Leo hadn't realized, but the hints were there. The curse intensifies a lot while they're working with her, while he also gets closer to Grimm but can't tell what's friendship and what's the curse. She digs into his past, too, eventually prodding until he reveals that he doesn't do grand magic because the last time he made a mistake and it killed his mother (really failed to save her, but he believes he killed her). They try her counterspell, but it doesn't work and nearly kills him, so Grimm refuses to try again. Leo has the idea to "convince" the curse to move into another object, much like they did with the field. Their proof of concept works, but they need a strong object, which means they need to find the splinter group again, as they had a griffin feather.

They find and help the splinter group, learning that they aren't really bad people, except for the leader's brother, who is very hostile toward them. (The group stole the only remaining healing spell to save their leader, as healing spells have been found impossible to replicate, so they're rare and precious.) They promise to write up helpful and benign spells for them in exchange for the feather. Leo sympathizes with them, and even Grimm starts to come around. The leader is so pleased she gives them two feathers. They leave but then are attacked in the night by the hostile brother, who believes they will not keep the group's secrets, despite the magical contract they signed. He takes the feather from Leo, not knowing Grimm has a second, and leaves them half-paralyzed for dead in a monster den. Leo sings and charms the monster, but the brother returns and tries to shoot them, ending up shooting and mortally wounding Grimm instead.

Leo, in a moment of final despair, weaves the grand magic of a healing spell into a song. The monster and others join in and lend him their magic, allowing him to save Grimm's life. It still takes a lot out of him, but the assistance means he doesn't die. He wakes after many dies unconscious to find Grimm still at his side, but that the curse has been removed. He's surprised to learn this, because his feelings are the same. Grimm is obviously in love with him, and I suspect actually broke the curse that way instead of with the feather, as he's cagey about it all. They can't communicate for shit, but at least they leave together to go back to the academy and are friendly-ish. They are able to do another, smaller, grand magic song, proving it's not a fluke. Leo declares his desire to make something of himself after all and there's a tentative plan between them that they'll still be casting together in the future.

THE END (AAAAAH)

Title: Heart's Blood
Author: Juliet Marillier
My Review:
Well. THAT just put me through the ringer. I can't remember the last time a book made me cry, but this one had my heartstrings in its hands for the last 20%. Did I read this straight through in one sitting? You betcha! (Thank goodness for Labor Day, otherwise I'd really be regretting this come time to sign into work in a few hours.)

This is, at the core, a Beauty and the Beast retelling, but it's truly so much more than that. If not for a few key elements, I might not have even noticed the fairy tale origins. This does exactly what I love out of a "retelling," which is to take the structure and best bits and then turn them into something new. This is a cast full of character who all have distinct personalities and with whom I easily fell in love. The "beast" has plenty of rough edges without the reprehensible behavior that can sometimes plague this tale. I loved that his scars were a part of him and not something that needed curing. The curse was creative and utterly chilling, especially as the details unfolded.

If I had any complaints (and I don't, but speaking hypothetically), it would be that I figured out some of the twists pretty early on. One twist in particular, really. But I'm not mad about it. It only made me feel proud for being such an observant reader.

This couldn't be anything but 5 stars, and I'm so pleased. I needed a 5-star read. It also brings serious consideration to the thought that I should revisit Juliet Marillier's more famous work, [b:Daughter of the Forest|13928|Daughter of the Forest (Sevenwaters, #1)|Juliet Marillier|https://i.gr-assets.com/images/S/compressed.photo.goodreads.com/books/1343589988l/13928._SY75_.jpg|1897725]. I DNFed it previously, but I always strongly suspected I simply wasn't in the mood for it at the time. Is this a new favorite author in the making???

Trigger warnings: Domestic abuse and resultant PTSD; some character death, but particularly animal death.

Title: Silver Borne (Mercy Thompson, #5)
Author: Patricia Briggs
My Review:
This is the first book of the series where I would actually rate it down a bit...though it's a 4.5 rounded up, so it hardly counts as rating down. Mercy was still lovely, and I enjoyed the plot. I enjoyed the progression of Mercy's relationship with Adam and all of the pack dynamics and drama related to that. And I loved the Samuel plotline at the start, and I thought it created a depth of emotion that contrasted nicely against the pack stuff. BUT...the way it resolved was a bit too deus ex machina for me. I mean, the fae who created the artifact Mercy has just happens to be one that Samuel has been in love with for ages? I didn't feel like I'd gotten the lead up to that revelation that I've come to expect from this series. I ignored my sense of "wtf?" and moved on, but it did bug me. Which sucks, because I adore Samuel, so I had high hopes for the resolution of his issues.

But overall, it was just as addictive and fun as all the previous books. I've already started on the next.

Title: Yield Under Great Persuasion
Author: Alexandra Rowland
My Review:
Another winner by this author! I read [b:A Taste of Gold and Iron|58724599|A Taste of Gold and Iron (Mahisti Dynasty, #1)|Alexandra Rowland|https://i.gr-assets.com/images/S/compressed.photo.goodreads.com/books/1635774115l/58724599._SY75_.jpg|87027106] back in August and loved it, so I was really hopeful for this one, even though it sounded pretty different. It was, but in delightful ways. The opening had me laughing, because the Tam's internal monologue was so distinct and wonderfully grouchy. What I didn't expect was just how sweet and emotional the book was, too. There were some real lessons for the main character to learn, but his personality also didn't do a 180, and I really appreciated that. At the end of the day, Lyford loved him for who he was, flaws and all, but just wanted to be treated well--and I'm so happy he got it.

I devoured this book in one day. It deserves these 5 probably-could-have-immediately-reread-it stars.

Title: The Frozen Crown (Warrior Witch, #1)
Author: Greta Kelly
My Review:
Oh hell no. I'm too pissed off at this book to write a coherent review. Maybe tomorrow.

**

Update (tomorrow)

This book follows Princess Askia as she travels from her war-torn Northern kingdom to the empire in the south to seek aid in the form of an army. Once there, she finds that the methods she'll need to use to gain support fall outside her comfort zone, and she makes many blunders along the way, but ultimately she'll stop at nothing to achieve her goal.

There's really only one thing I liked about this book, and that's the fact that the focus/goal of the main character remains clear throughout. Everything Askia does is through the lens of helping her people by gaining an army.

And that's pretty much where the praise stops.

This book was such a letdown. It started strong, and I had really high hopes. Generally speaking, it wasn't difficult to read, and I found that I got through chapters pretty quickly. However, what started as an intense story about war devolved into a story about court politics--not a bad thing on its own, but the story was fixated on the parts of it that revolved around "proper" behavior of a woman in society and the need for Askia to get married (or not fall for a guy, as the case may be). The world itself was very interesting, so it really annoyed me to see it fall into the same tired handling of women in fantasy. It could have been far more nuanced than it was. Instead, what I found was a story that promised one thing and delivered another. Half the male cast was interested in Askia, and the two cases where she was interested were so poorly developed that I just didn't care. Given that I'm usually a sucker for a good romance subplot, this was also disappointing.

The setting shift was annoying, because with that setting came an entirely different story than what this could have been. There were too many characters with not enough attention to developing them. There was instalove/lust. The females characters flip-flopped between being friendly/nurturing to being cold and bitchy with very little provocation. The male characters were either flirty, creepy, or fatherly. The (albeit brief) sexual content was entirely unnecessary.

And god, the ending. Let's talk about that for a second. Strong, intense, war-focused opening. Bland, "let me be a proper woman, but not too proper because I'm a rebel" middle...BUT NOW let's get back to the action at the 90% mark and throw in the kitchen sink! I'm gonna spoil the whole thing under the tag, because I need to remember why I hated this once I forget this plot, just in case I'm ever insane enough to think this needs another try. Askia gets butt hurt that guard Illya doesn't stick around while she's getting wined and dined (and fondled) by the emperor, and also because the prince didn't like it either and was equally butt hurt. She's all sullen and just wants to be alone once she gets back to her room, so much so that she ignores her ghost traitor friend (whose name started with a V) until the last second when someone bursts into her room. She uses magic to escape, ending up falling from the sky in the great hall. The Emperor is concerned, immediately proclaims to the gathered crowd that they're getting married. Evil Count guy protests and presumably takes info back to his master (who was the one who tried to magically kidnap her, also wanted to marry the chick because idek). Askia signs the marriage contract, which makes Illya sad and Prince Iskander turn into an immature punk. Finds out the queen is dying, so Askia is gonna be the Big Bitch queen now. She marries the Emperor just in time for the Evil Count to murder the other queen. Askia immediately takes charge of the country because the all-powerful Emperor is Too Sad to do it himself. Later that night, Emperor proclaims to his two sons that he's making Askia Empress instead of putting one of them in charge while he goes to war. Understandably angry (even if they're both punks), they protest right up until the Big Bad magically swoops in, murders the Emperor, and kidnaps Askia. She wakes up and is somewhere else, held prisoner. THE END. No joke, it ends on a ridiculous cliffhanger. Also no joke, all of that happened between 83% and 97% (where the book ended) in the kindle version after about 75% of the book where NOTHING HAPPENS but social blunders.

The magic system was interesting, and that's the thing that saved this from a 1-star rating.

Title: Just Stab Me Now
Author: Jill Bearup
My Review:
A couple of months ago, I stumbled across the author's compiled versions on YouTube of the video shorts that inspired this book, and I was so entertained that I immediately ordered the novel. Life being what it is, I've only just gotten around to reading it. It could have gone either way, considering its origins, but I am so pleased to say that I loved this book. I found that having seen the videos first only enriched the experience and made me eager to see how they translated. I was sucked in from the first chapter and probably would have read it all in one sitting if my schedule had permitted. As it was, I started it last night and then made time today to finish the rest out all in one go. It was so much fun, and I can see myself rereading it in the future to break a reading slump.

Is this a life-changing or genre-busting fantasy plot? No. But it knows it, leans into it, and is highly entertaining as a result. You know what you are, book, and I see you and appreciate you for it.

5 stars of pure enjoyment.

Title: The Dragon and the Emerald King (Tales of the Gemstone Kingdoms #5)
Author: Amanda Meuwissen
My Review:
I really wish I'd read the books in this series with no gap between them, because I think I could have enjoyed this one more if I had. (I couldn't, because they weren't all released when I started, but still.) This book suffered from some of the same issues of the last two, where there was so much time spent on callbacks to earlier books that they became excessive. In this case, there were previous characters appearing left and right, and I remembered basically no one who wasn't a POV character from a previous book. Even those were a little fuzzy.

Setting that aside, the plot of this one wasn't bad. Perhaps a bit simplistic, when you cut right to it, but it's a short book, so I can forgive some of that. It failed to recapture the magic of the first two books, but it was fine for what it was. Definitely better than book 3. I'm looking forward to trying some more books by the author, now that I've knocked this series out. When they're good, they're great, so I hope to find that again.

3.5 stars rounded down.

Title: The Magician King
Author: Lev Grossman
My Review:
This book is proof that sometimes it's worth it to give the second in a series a chance, even if you didn't love the first. Despite my love of the TV series, [b:The Magicians|6101718|The Magicians (The Magicians, #1)|Lev Grossman|https://i.gr-assets.com/images/S/compressed.photo.goodreads.com/books/1313772941l/6101718._SY75_.jpg|6278977] was just a 3-star read for me, and I debated whether to bother continuing. I'm SO GLAD I did. It had me hooked from the first chapter. It took me a little while to finish, but that had more to do with me relearning how to flex my reading muscles than any fault of the book. I alternated between a paper copy and the audiobook, as the situation merited, and both experiences were excellent.

Where book one made me wonder what the point of it all was, this book made me feel things. Surprisingly, my feelings weren't even all for Julia (though, thankfully the show forewarned me about the rape scene and answered the age-old question of whether that plotline was canon or invented for the show). Quentin has far sharper corners in the book than in the show, making him my favorite in one place and my least favorite in the other, as of book 1. So color me surprised when I really empathized with him here. There was a mix of the mundane and the adventurous in his chapters, and I was really engaged with both. I won't say that Julia's chapters dragged, because I was definitely interested to see what was happening with her, but there was a repetitiveness to them that was noticeable in comparison. Also, I'm really glad that her pretentiousness was toned down for the show. She and her friends were the epitome of the insufferable genius.

Super happy I read this, finally. 4.5 stars, really only docked for some Julia quibbles, but happily rounded up to 5 in the end.

My Review:
Read #2 - Still loved it.

*****

Hoooooly crap this book was good. Intense. Powerful. Sad. Terrifying. Lovely. I really can't find enough adjectives to describe it. I was really afraid I had gotten so sucked into it only to be disappointed in the end, like with a lame cliffhanger or some such, but NO, and my goodness was it worth the torment.

I'm a sucker for fairy tale retellings, and despite the fact that I somehow had NO IDEA going in what this was, I was hooked about three seconds in when I realized. I loved the fairy world, the grimness of the human world Feyre inhabited, the brutality softened by the beauty...yeah, there I go with adjectives again. It's really difficult to describe in detail what I liked. I have trouble with books I love, and this one is no exception.

It was just amazing. Full stop.

Title: Mansfield Park
Author: Jane Austen
Bookshelves: austen-and-austenesque, classics, austenesque-challenge-2016, library-audible, library-paper, library-kindle
Bookshelves with positions: austen-and-austenesque (#15), classics (#29), austenesque-challenge-2016 (#83), library-audible (#55), library-paper (#1319), library-kindle (#973)
My Review:
I tried reading this book previously and struggled with it a lot. I think partially it's that the movie versions have always been among my least favorite Jane Austen adaptations (though I still like them), and because I'd heard some unfavorable things about this book. I'm glad I gave it another try, though, because ultimately I did really enjoy it. If you're like me and struggling, go for the Juliet Stevenson audio version. Seriously, that woman is worth her weight in gold.

Fanny is fairly inactive as a main character, and Edmund is at times frustratingly blind to Mary Crawford's faults, but I couldn't help becoming wrapped up in their little family drama. Fanny is sweet, shy, innocent, fearful, indecisive...basically the total opposite of Elizabeth Bennet. I could never love her as much as Elizabeth, but I think I related to her more. I imagine that if I were in the shoes of either of those characters, I would probably be more like Fanny, even if I would prefer to be Elizabeth.

I was glad as I was reading that I'd seen the movie versions (the 1999 Jonny Lee Miller version being my favorite, though it's less faithful), because it allowed me get more absorbed in some of the longer and more tedious speeches without losing the thread of the story. It was really interesting to see that some of the things I'd assumed were written for the movie (Mary talking about Edmund's "pretty lecture" comes to mind first, from the 2007 version) were actually taken almost directly from the text. And though the 1999 version strayed in many ways, particularly in Fanny's passiveness, I could better understand why those changes were made now that I've read the book.

I really didn't intend to talk about the movies so much--ha--but I find that I have little in the way of particular critique for the story beyond what I've already said. In the grand scheme of Jane Austen, I liked this better than Emma and Lady Susan, but not as much as Northanger Abbey or Sense and Sensibility. (And do I even need to put Persuasion or Pride and Prejudice on that list? They're so far above, for me, that I'd be shocked if anything else compared.) Definitely worth the read, even if it's not going to end up on my audible repeat list like my faves.

Title: Beautiful Beast (Beautiful Queens, #1)
Author: E.J. Hill
My Review:
I received a free copy of this book through BookSirens in exchange for an honest review.

At its core, this book is a Beauty and the Beast retelling, wherein the main character, Kalista, is cursed by a bitter sorceress to be a prisoner in her own home with only invisible servants for company. The story kicks off when a man, Arawn, arrives to murder her ("the beast") in revenge for his brother's death. He learns that things are not as they seem and has to work together with Kalista to try to break the curse before it's too late.

I'm a sucker for a Beauty and the Beast-based story, so picking this one to read and review was a no-brainer. Unfortunately, this one really didn't do it for me. Goodreads lists the paperback as 334 pages, and it felt a lot longer to me, despite being filled with short scenes. The prose-level writing was good, and while there were a handful of very minor editing issues, it was overall a clean, well-constructed ebook. The things I disliked were not so egregious that I wanted to throw it across the room, but rather left me feeling bored, vaguely annoyed, and like I wanted to just put it down and not bother. I did not, because I wanted to be able to offer thoughts on the full manuscript for this review.

1) There are weird callbacks to the Disney Beauty and the Beast that seemed derivative rather than inspired by. Two of the primary servants are symbolized by a pocketwatch and candleabra. There are wolves in woods who attack when Arawn makes a run for it. The west wing is forbidden. There's a magic mirror and a rose under a dome that is losing petals. Probably there were more, but those were the ones that stuck out the most.

2) Despite finishing the book, I don't really feel like I know the characters. We're told some background information on them (though very little), but at no point did I get drawn into their feelings, emotions, wants, needs, etc. beyond the very little that was necessary to advance the plot (and that was told to me, not shown). For instance, Arawn is there to avenge his brother's death. We learn a couple of sentences worth of information about what happened to him, and there's one throwaway line where Kalista thinks Arawn regrets having to be heir to the throne more than his brother's loss, but that is it. This is so key to Arawn's motivations that this strikes me as a vast desert of missed opportunity.

3) There are many repeated mundane events throughout the story that only advance the plot in the tiniest ways and the characters hardly at all. They dress for dinner, eat, go horse riding, spend time researching in the library...and all of the scenes are very short with almost no information conveyed in them. Every time Kalista reveals an important piece of information, there's an artificial reason that the whole story can't come out right then (like, she's too tired to keep talking, when it's only been a few sentences). It dragged the pace unnecessarily and annoyed me. It was almost the equivalent of those novels where the only conflict is the fact that the characters won't talk to each other, and if they did the conflict would be over.

4) Kalista isn't really a beast. She isn't even a bad person. Supposedly her family was conceited, and that's why the evil sorceress cursed them, but Kalista herself has no apparent flaws that she needs to overcome. And since this is a Beauty and the Beast retelling, that makes me ask: What's the point????

5) Because of some of the things above, I really didn't feel any chemistry between the main characters. I barely knew them, and they barely knew each other, and that is not what I'd call an epic love story worthy of Beauty and the Beast.

Unfortunately, this is 2 stars for me. I can't rate it any higher, even with the decent prose.

Title: Threads of Magic: A Pride and Prejudice Fantasy Variation (Mr. Darcy's Magic Book 2)
Author: Monica Fairview
My Review: I enjoyed book 1 enough that I immediately downloaded this one. I had an equally good time with it, and I even did not guess the "twist" this time. My only complaint was that some of the conflict between Elizabeth and Darcy felt repetitive. I get that he's still growing as a person, but sometimes he was borderline too obtuse for my taste. I can forgive it here, but I'll be annoyed if that continues into book 3. I did predict (somewhat) the <spoiler>Jane and Bingley</spoiler> storyline, but that was more from a realization that it would make sense based on the source material, rather than any fault of this book. It made me giggle when I was (again, somewhat) right. Still having a good time with this series and will continue.

Title: Dangerous Magic: A Pride and Prejudice Variation (Mr. Darcy's Magic #1)
Author: Monica Fairview
My Review: I was really in the mood for a strong JAFF, but also feeling like some fantasy, and this book delivered. I love a good forced marriage trope, and this one was pretty good. I enjoyed the way Elizabeth and Darcy had to work together and got to know one another in the process. The twist (if we can even call it that) was pretty predictable, but I still had a good time all the way to the end, particularly with some A-level pining. Definitely recommend this one to anyone for whom the premise is appealing.

Title: Truth or Dare (Dare to Love, #1)
Author: Mira Lyn Kelly
My Review:
I found this book because it's featured in the Chapters: Interactive Stories app by Crazy Maple Studios. I was impatient for the whole thing to be released--hence my excitement when I realized it was available on kindle.

This is a pretty short book that I was able to finish in a few hours, but that was one of its selling points for me. I don't read a lot of contemporary romance, because it seems the barriers to two characters being together, in a modern world, are often angsty, overdrawn, and implausible...or just make the characters look dumb. This book not only had a solid concept/male lead motivation, but it never left the characters lingering for too long in that annoying "JUST TALK ALREADY" zone.

I really enjoyed both characters, their backstories, and how they interacted with each other. I loved Tyler in particular. Totally my catnip.

Solid 4 stars. I'd read this again if I wanted something quick and satisfying.
