Title: Sway
Author: Melanie Stanford
My Review:
I'm in the middle of my yearly (bi-yearly? tri-yearly? who knows!) re-listen of Persuasion, and it put me in the mind to read one of the few adaptations that I've had in my queue for a while. I mostly read P&P stuff, but it's really a shame there isn't more variety of variations for this particular Austen work. This one came highly recommended, and I managed to catch it on sale recently, so it was a good candidate for this particular mood.

I was not disappointed and greatly enjoyed this story. Contemporary is not my favorite. That's not to say there aren't good ones, but I tend to enjoy fewer of the as a percentage of my reading list. For Austen adaptations, I think it takes some clever work to make the storylines translate, and this story did a pretty good job. There were a few things that bugged me, like the more "official" nature of the relationships of Eric/Lacey and Ava/Gage, but it would have been nearly impossible to avoid in a modern setting. I didn't love the way Eric acted early on, and I found it harder to buy the Lacey/Sam relationship. I don't count that against this story, per se, but it does illustrate one of the reasons I tend to prefer historicals.

I approved of the modern day translations of all of the characters, except perhaps that Shelby (Mrs. Clay) didn't seem to have the same level of purpose/motivation for existing. The story itself was at times a bit too closely translated, scene for scene, but there was enough that was unique to keep it from turning boring. My favorite parts, though, were the glimpses of Ava and Eric's relationship growing in the past, done through flashbacks. That, obviously, was not part of the source material, so it was partially the newness of it and partially how endearing they were. I love friends-to-lovers stories, and their friendship was adorable. (On a side note, I also loved that Ava and Charlie were best friends, too. It was a nice touch.)

Solid 4 stars, and I will likely re-read this one.

Title: Spindrift
Author: Amy Rae Durreson
My Review:
I'm now two for two with this author. I loved the characters. I loved the plot. The ghost story was creepy, but not scary. The love story had some well-handled angst and plenty of sweetness. I stayed up WAY too late reading it on a work night, because I just couldn't help myself.

5 stars, no doubt. Give me all the books like this.

Title: In Heaven or Earth
Author: Amy Rae Durreson
My Review:
I really enjoyed this story, but it's definitely one that would have benefited from another 100+ pages to allow for slower dispersion of information and a slower build to the love story. All the science and tech stuff was a lot to absorb in such a rapid-fire delivery of information. At a certain point, I stopped trying, and I found that I didn't really need to understand it all to enjoy the story. The romantic development could definitely have used the extra time, as it came about pretty suddenly. I still liked it, because the characters themselves were likeable, and they had good chemistry, so it worked--but it could have been even better. I found myself picking this up during every free moment I had today, though, so obviously my complaints are pretty minor.

4 stars and something I would absolutely reread.

Title: The Court of Lightning
Author: Amy Rae Durreson
My Review:
This book could have used a good 100+ more pages to really live up to its potential. There was a ton of worldbuilding and a ton of characters, but very little page time for them to shine. The bones were good, sometimes excellent, but they were the sort that would have fitted a 500-page epic fantasy better than this 124-page novella. I want to see more of the courts and how the war started and see Shan inventing things--and so much else.

The romance was also nice, but could have benefited from more page time, too. The first half was full of lovely pining, but once the UST resolved, there wasn't much more development. A little, but not a ton. I still liked the relationship, and they had nice chemistry, but I just wanted more!

3.5 stars rounded up, because I enjoyed it despite its flaws.

Title: The Bard and the Fairy Prince (Tales of the Gemstone Kingdoms #3)
Author: Amanda Meuwissen
My Review:
Given how much I enjoyed the first two books in this series, I'm sad to say that this one really fell flat for me. I never really felt like I got to know the two main characters, and I never reached a point that I was rooting for their romance. Soooo much time in this book was spent reintroducing and featuring characters from the first two books. It might have worked in a longer-format story, but these books are barely long enough to be considered full novels (not a judgement, just a fact), and that meant this character distraction ultimately detracted from the story and my ability to empathize with the main characters. There just wasn't enough time on the page to allow them to get to know one another. I didn't buy Nemirac's emotional transition between the climax and the end. I didn't buy these two being in love. That said, I still plan to read book 4, particularly because of the build up and insight we've already got about one of the mains.

2.5 stars rounded up, because it was ultimately fine...just disappointing.

Title: Stitches (Tales of the Gemstone Kingdoms #2)
Author: Amanda Meuwissen
My Review:
Much like the first book, [b:The Prince and the Ice King|58409067|The Prince and the Ice King (Gemstone Kingdoms, #1)|Amanda Meuwissen|https://i.gr-assets.com/images/S/compressed.photo.goodreads.com/books/1624464997l/58409067._SY75_.jpg|91624567], this one featured a super sweet main character that basically everyone likes and who has no real flaws. Unlike the first book, this one threw me for a complete loop around 80%, and for that I'm giving it an extra half star over its predecessor. Maybe I was just dumb, because I can see the clues in hindsight, but it really surprised me, and I appreciate when a book can do that. Even with the conflicts getting cleared up pretty quickly, there was some genuine tension near the climax, and that made the ending all the more rewarding. I also enjoyed how it circled back around to the first book's ending.

Speaking of the first book, I did actually figure out that Levi was the sacrifice Reardon replaced before it was revealed, so give me at least some credit for being perceptive!

4.5 stars, rounded up.

Title: A Taste of Gold and Iron (Mahisti Dynasty, #1)
Author: Alexandra Rowland
My Review:
It's been awhile since I read a full-length fantasy novel, so it was a bit of an adjustment to let myself roll with all the world building and plot and characters. I was tempted to put it down early (did, the first time I tried reading it), not because of any flaw with the book, but because I seem to be afraid of reading a dud. In this case, I shouldn't have been. I adored all the main players in this story, and the plot was entertaining, if not particularly twisty or surprising. The romance was super slow burn, but not high angst, which I really like. The way it tackled panic attacks without ever giving it a name or diagnosis was really interesting, too, and I like that there was no magical cure for it.

Totally 5 stars, can't be anything else.

Title: The Alien's Defiant Omega (The Alien's Omega, #3)
Author: Sienna Sway
My Review:
This book was good, but not my favorite of the series. I'm not sure yet whether that's due to the formula getting old, general series fatigue, or simply because I didn't find these characters as interesting. I did really enjoy the twist at the auction, and that made the whole build-up worth it, but the path getting there wasn't as fun as in the other stories. Still fun, though.

Probably 3.5-ish rounded up, if I'm pressed, but I could be convinced to go a solid 4 stars just for the auction and getting to see Alex and Saar's baby.

Title: The Alien's Pregnant Omega (The Alien's Omega)

My Review:
This novella was pretty pointless. At least half of it was repeated from book 2, to the point that I started just flipping past all those bits. I enjoyed the added scenes for more context with Alex and Saar, but I was disappointed there wasn't way more of it.

This gets 3 stars only because it's not bad--I just wish there was more original material.

Title: The Alien's Runaway Omega (The Alien's Omega #2)
Author: Sienna Sway
My Review:
I enjoyed this one almost as much as the first. It was nice that the build was a bit different, with the timeline of getting to know one another and mating, and the UST and pining was nice. Some of my favorite bits were the scenes with Alex and Saar, not surprisingly. I liked Devin's backstory and motivations, and while Eisa is still not my favorite, he grew on me well enough. This was an interesting progression of the worldbuilding, and I'm looking forward to seeing where it goes.

4.5 stars, rounded up, mostly because I just don't find Eisa as charming as Saar.

Title: Malik (Kif Warriors #1)
Author: Kiki Burrelli
My Review:
I haven't read a lot of alien romance, so this was mostly new to me. I liked the culture clashing and working that out, particularly as the primary setting was so foreign. There were parts where the plot moved too fast, skipping over sections that might have had more fleshing out in a longer novel, but overall I didn't mind the speed. There was just enough detail to feel immersed in the world without it being too much for the length, which I appreciated. The romance was pretty insta-love, but I thought it worked in this scenario. They were very sappy with one another, but apparently I'm in a phase where I'm enjoying low angst, because it hit just right.

Probably more like 3.5 stars rounded up, but had a good time with it and probably would reread it if the mood struck.

Title: A Frost of Cares
Author: Amy Rae Durreson
My Review:
This book was amazing. I'm honestly shocked by how sucked in I got and how much I loved it, considering it was a random book from a random list that seemed mildly interesting and had decent ratings. I didn't have any particular expectations, other than being allowed to put it down if I started it and didn't enjoy it. I was already hooked on page 2, because Luke was just such a charming narrator. Jay was gruff and a little standoffish, but not actually an asshole. Their love story was beautiful. The ghost story was creepy, but not so overwhelmingly that I'm worried I'll have nightmares. How the author packed so much into a short book, I don't know. Probably arcane magic.

5 very enthusiastic stars. So enthusiastic that, even though I had this book on KU, I immediately ordered the paperback for my collection.

My Review:
I am such a sucker for portal fantasies. Despite the description, I did not expect that to be what I was going to get when I started this series, but here we are. I'm having way too much fun with it all, enough that this book gets an upgrade to 5 stars. It's not that it's better than the previous two, per se, but just that I'm less annoyed at the episodic nature of it now. Still don't love that aspect, but I'm basically pretending like it's all one book.

Though I don't think this series is finished, so I'm going to be peeved if I get through these and don't have an ending. Just sayin'.

Title: Bro and the Beast (The Wolf's Mate, #1)
Author: L.C.  Davis
My Review:
Edited 12/12/2023 after finishing # 5

I can't help but raise my rating for every book in this series to 5 stars based on my overall enjoyment of it and the fact that I only graded this one lower because of the episodic nature of it. Just plan to read books 1-5 all at once like it's a standard novel, and the problem is solved. Still not my structural preference, but this one gets a pass because I had such a good time with it.

-

Original review 12/10/2023

I really expected this book to be dumb. I expected to read a chapter, laugh at the low quality crap that ends up in KU, and send the book back.

That did not happen.

I read the whole thing. I laughed aloud at the places the author intended. I liked the characters. I liked the schtick of the world.

In short, I enjoyed the hell out of it.

It loses one star because this is not a complete story, and I hate that. It's not quite as egregious as some chopped-off endings I've read, but this is definitely one of those "you must read the sequels to get a full story" type of books that really should have just been one longer book.

But, fine. I concede. You got me.

*grumbles and goes to download book 2*

Title: Darcy's Pursuit
Author: M.A. Sandiford
My Review:
What a wild ride. This is pure crack treated seriously. It's like [b:Mr. Darcy's Journey: A Pride & Prejudice Variation|30303244|Mr. Darcy's Journey A Pride & Prejudice Variation|Abigail Reynolds|https://i.gr-assets.com/images/S/compressed.photo.goodreads.com/books/1464381730l/30303244._SX50_.jpg|50785771] and [b:The Journey|13454484|The Journey|Jan Hahn|https://i.gr-assets.com/images/S/compressed.photo.goodreads.com/books/1328488341l/13454484._SY75_.jpg|18981669] had quintuplets, and I mean that in both the best and worst ways. Like, you're happy they exist but man are they probably a lot of work. This story was sometimes riveting, sometimes ridiculous, sometimes both. Mostly, it was quite repetitive in the middle, and I was skimming a lot by the time they were at their 17th toll collector or inn to ask for information (or their millionth kidnapper, for that matter). Still, I enjoyed it and stayed up way too late finishing it, so that wins it back some points.

Going with 4 stars, though I haven't entirely decided if that's it or if it's more like 3.5 rounded up. That will probably be judged if I ever reread this.

Title: Mr. Darcy's Journey: A Pride & Prejudice Variation
Author: Abigail Reynolds
My Review:
Another winner from Abigail Reynolds! There was less angst than some of her others, and a healthy dose of adventure to replace it, which I absolutely adored. I'm always a sucker for having an involved Fitzwilliam family, and every member portrayed here was delightful. I loved the way their travels played out, the ways that Darcy and Elizabeth became closer almost by accident (the alley scene when they had to escape the miscreants was rather swoon-inducing), and all of the wacky antics and touching moments. I'm no history scholar, but the bits that were injected here were really interesting and I loved how it impacted the characters.

The story was great. The audiobook narration was great. I will definitely be listening to this one again.

Title: On Equal Ground: A Pride and Prejudice Vagary (The Dramas)
Author: Elizabeth     Adams
My Review:
Re-read 10/22/23

I grabbed this almost at random because I needed a book for an emergency trip, and this was absolutely perfect to pass the time with a long drive. I enjoyed it just as much as the first time. (I had actually intended to grab [b:Ardently: A Pride and Prejudice Variation|25656343|Ardently A Pride and Prejudice Variation|Caitlin Williams|https://i.gr-assets.com/images/S/compressed.photo.goodreads.com/books/1438008845l/25656343._SY75_.jpg|45478003], but I was tired and couldn't remember the title. All I could remember was that Elizabeth had a higher standing, so in that way I did a good job choosing something similar. This is what happens when you've read a lot of these, ha! Turns out I have Ardently on kindle, not audible, which explains why I couldn't find it....)

*

I bought this book on a whim, because it was recommended through my audible app after I finished [b:The Houseguest: A Pride and Prejudice Vagary|21768878|The Houseguest A Pride and Prejudice Vagary|Elizabeth Adams|https://i.gr-assets.com/images/S/compressed.photo.goodreads.com/books/1420512315l/21768878._SX50_.jpg|24740249] for the third time. I didn't read the description beforehand, and started listening immediately. When I realized what was going on, I wasn't sure I would like it. I usually find it hard to see Elizabeth with others, and prefer for that to be off-screen if it's a story element. However, what I found here was that I actually LOVED her first husband, so by the time that portion of the story ended, I was strangely afraid that I might not like Darcy!

The transition was well done, though. I liked that they already knew one another, and thus the transition of their friendship to something more was gradual and realistic. This is one of the few stories with very little conflict and a high level of detail of various familial relationships and epilogue items that I actually really liked. These are not the same characters from the original, but I could see how they would get there, and that made them acceptable. It was sort of fun to see characters such as Lydia getting to grow without it being a sequel.

4.5 stars rounded up.

Title: In Sickness and in Health (Austenesque Vagaries)
Author: Frances Reynolds
My Review:
I loved this book. This is a great example of a variation that does not change the characters in their essentials, but that diverges so much that it's a very different story. The quarantine backdrop allowed us to see characters in a setting entirely unlike the formal drawing room, and those quiet snatches of conversation between various characters were really the strength of this tale.

It's often hard for me to talk in details about books I loved, and that's the case here. Too many good things to comment on only a few.

5 enthusiastic stars.

Title: Obsession (The Mate Games: War #1)
Author: K. Loraine
My Review:
I have little idea what to say about this book. It was such a wild ride that I flew through it in two days, and there was a certain enjoyment in just how ridiculous these characters are, but I can't say I liked it, exactly. I'm starting to think maybe this sub-genre of romance (or its most commonly used tropes, anyway) are really not my thing. The near-insta-love, the obsession, the constant horniness - I'm not offended, it's just a bit much.

What I will give this over other RH/why-choose stories is that: a) it didn't end on a completely stupid cliffhanger, and b) there is an actual plot beyond simple horniness.

I'm calling this a 3.5 rounded down, but with the consideration that I'm not fully the target audience.

Title: Speechless
Author: Jessie  Lewis
My Review:
While I have a few miscellaneous complaints about this book (the inconsistent formatting of Darcy's dialog/thoughts, the tendency toward overly-wordy narrative segments, some repetitiveness), they are greatly outweighed by the things I loved. I generally love a forced-proximity tale, and this is both no exception and one that is pretty different from others I've read in other P&P-based stories. I particularly enjoyed how Darcy and Elizabeth were able to get to know one another, and the quirk of Darcy being without his voice added that little level of interest and complication.

Really more like 4.5 stars, rounded up, which is consistent with how I felt about [b:Mistaken|43910839|Mistaken|Jessie Lewis|https://i.gr-assets.com/images/S/compressed.photo.goodreads.com/books/1549840394l/43910839._SY75_.jpg|57898025].

Title: The Beast's Ruined Bride (Rogues Gone Dirty, #1)
Author: Bella Moxie
My Review:
Like the previous book in this series, things happened really fast in this short book. However, I found them more believable (if no less expectedly cheesy) because the hero and heroine knew each other previously and already had feelings for one another. It felt more like the last quarter of a novel rather than a full story itself, but I enjoyed it nonetheless.

3.5 stars, rounded up for decent writing.

Title: Missing Jane
Author: Bronwen Chisholm
My Review:
This was an atypical Pride and Prejudice variation, in that its page time revolved far more around Jane than around Elizabeth. Elizabeth was only in a couple of scenes, but Darcy's thoughts about her kept her represented when she wasn't present. I wouldn't say I preferred this allocation of the cast, but it was such a short story that it worked pretty well. Anything longer and I think Elizabeth's absence would have been too great. This felt almost like the coda to a longer story, where many things had happened to the characters already, and we just got to see the last adventure and culmination of it all. I wouldn't want every story to be like this, but I enjoyed this one.

4 stars because I'm feeling novellas today!

Title: A Spindle Splintered (Fractured Fables, #1)
Author: Alix E. Harrow
My Review:
Ugh, that was SO GOOD. It made me cry multiple times, which a) is difficult (particularly from a story that's barely more than 100 pages), and b) isn't something I'd normally say was a good thing, BUT this story was beautiful. I loved Zinnia, Charm, Primrose, and even the handful of minor characters who had very little screen time. The narration was breezy and yet endearing, and tugged at my heartstrings in just the right way while not being overly sentimental or angsty. I previously adored [b:The Ten Thousand Doors of January|43521657|The Ten Thousand Doors of January|Alix E. Harrow|https://i.gr-assets.com/images/S/compressed.photo.goodreads.com/books/1548174710l/43521657._SY75_.jpg|63516505], so this latest success has firmly rooted Alix E. Harrow in my trusted authors list.

5 glowing stars. Excuse me while I go read everything else she's written.

Title: The Viscount Who Loved Me (Bridgertons, #2)
Author: Julia Quinn
My Review:
I officially understand now why people are obsessed with this series. After my very meh feelings about book #1, I had my doubts, but this one turned it all around. Antony was a bit insufferable at times, but when I rolled my eyes about it, I felt more like one of his siblings than an annoyed reader. The family relationships in here are the BEST, and Colin regularly made me laugh aloud as I listened. I can't wait to read more of this family.

I listed to the audiobook and the narrator, Rosalyn Landor, was excellent.

5 relieved stars. I feel like I'm in the club now.

Title: Final Girls
Author: Riley Sager
Bookshelves: suspense-thriller, library-kindle, tbr-2021, library-paper, advanced-reader-copy, readathon-2023-romance
Bookshelves with positions: suspense-thriller (#56), library-kindle (#786), tbr-2021 (#8), library-paper (#1109), advanced-reader-copy (#10), readathon-2023-romance (#34)
My Review:
This is my first Riley Sager, and I was not disappointed. I was tense in my seat for the entirety of the novel, which I read all in one go. I naturally suspected everyone as I went along, but I can genuinely say that I didn't predict how it came out. Bits and pieces, maybe, but not the whole truth. There were moments when I was sure I was going to hate the ending, but I'm thankful to say that wasn't the case. There were definitely some bits in the middle that I didn't care for, mostly because it negatively impacted my empathy for the main character, but I can retroactively understand why things went the way they did. If I ever reach a point that I've forgotten this story, I would read it again. Maybe even if I do remember some bits. It might be interesting to watch the through line as it progresses.

4.5 stars that I'm rounding down because there were bits that I didn't love, even if I get them.

Title: All Night with Darcy: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: Jane Grix
My Review:
A fun, quick read. My only real complaint is there was a lot of lingering on unnecessary internal monologue and repetition of story beats from the source material. Would have been better with that trimmed down, but I still enjoyed it.

4 stars.

Title: Less Proud and More Persuasive
Author: Sophie Turner
My Review: I enjoyed this novella. It was perhaps a little wordy in the introspection, but otherwise I liked the path it took. My favorite part was their discussion about <spoiler>Darcy's tenants, as it perfectly illustrated her growing insight into his character and his growing appreciation for her intelligence.</spoiler> My only real regret is that the ending was very abrupt. I would have liked to get a little more of a denouement.

Title: Finally Mrs. Darcy: A Pride and Prejudice Novella (Dash of Darcy and Companions Collection Book 1)
Author: Leenie Brown
My Review: The length of this story necessitated that parts were more abrupt than is really my preference, but I liked the path the story took. It wasn't what I was expecting, as most of the conflict did not revolve around Darcy and Elizabeth coming together. It was refreshing in that way. There were some intense moments mixed in that I really appreciated, and I think showed off this author's ability to tug at the reader's heartstrings. This is my second by her, and I will definitely be reading others.

Title: The Fallen Earl's Sinful Salvation (Rogues Gone Dirty, #0.5)
Author: Bella Moxie
My Review:
The romance moved super fast. Mostly expected due to the length, but it also happened over a very short time period. It was enjoyable, though. The writing flowed well, and I liked the characters.

Probably more like 3.5 stars, rounded up.

Title: Darcy’s Change of Heart: A Pride and Prejudice Regency Variation 2nd Edition
Author: Harriet Knowles
My Review: This is a rare case of a very short novel feeling very long. It was a lot of words for very little happening, and I found myself skimming whole swathes of inner monologue. It was fine, but not a lot happened, and it could easily have been trimmed in half and been more enjoyable.

Title: Lord of Eternal Night (Darkmourn Universe, #1)
Author: Ben Alderson
My Review:
This story started slow, in the sense that there was a lot of nearly incomprehensible information thrown out immediately, and that made it difficult to get through. I was tempted to DNF it early. Then Jak got to the castle and I realized what I had apparently managed to miss in the blurb--this is a Beauty and the Beast retelling. Therefore, slow start or not, I knew I would be finishing it after all. The short page count means that it didn't have as much time to develop the romance as I would probably prefer, but it was good for what time it had. I enjoyed the things that made this version of the tale unique, and I wouldn't be opposed to continuing to read in this world.

4 stars because you have to do something really wrong for me to rate a Beauty and the Beast tale any lower than that.

Title: Psycho Shifters (Cruel Shifterverse, #1)
Author: Jasmine Mas
My Review:
This was a quick read despite some confusing worldbuilding and unnecessary repetition. There was a big focus around the M/M relationships in this book, and for the most part that was well done. I liked the Sadie was not focused on immediately jumping into bed with every possible character, as it made a lot of sense with her backstory. There's some lack of communication that I found grating, far too many references to vomit for my taste (not in an ED context), and a disconnect between the male Alphas' thoughts and actions, but the single biggest issue I had was the freakin' cliffhanger ending. It was warned in the content warnings at the beginning, but that's really no excuse. Just give me something wrapped up by the end (not everything, just something!), not a story completely chopped off. It's not that complicated. Considering the heavy focus in this book on the way this world works and Sadie's confusion/distrust about it, there was really space to have some good payoff, and the lack is disappointing.

3 stars that honestly could have been higher with some tweaks, which makes me sad. So close and yet so far.

Title: Red, White & Royal Blue
Author: Casey McQuiston
My Review:
Holy crap. Why can't I give this 6 stars?

This is the third night in a row I've stayed up too late reading. This thing sucked me in and wouldn't let me go. It's the kind of book I find myself needing to pick up throughout the day "just to read a few more paragraphs"—and then end up reading a whole chapter. I seriously loved everything about this book. The characters are so real, distinct, heartfelt. The romance was exactly the sort I like: two intelligent people who sometimes make bad decisions but ultimately communicate like the adults that they are and figure things out. I loved the twist. Hell, I even loved the all the little bits of history and politics, and I hate both history and politics. Also, I'm ashamed to say that I teared up when they won the election at the end. Obviously this book has brainwashed me.

I can't even talk about it. There's too much to list all the good parts. Imagine me tossing a copy at your feet and saying, "There. Those are the good parts. Read them."

Title: Spells and Shadows: A Fantasy Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: Victoria Kincaid
My Review:
This is the P&P variation I've been waiting for. It's the perfect mix of Austen characters/situations with the fantastical elements. I loved everything about this book, from the way the characters were portrayed (including their necessary alterations for the magical bits) to the progression of the plot and all in between. If not for work, I would have read straight through after starting it, and once I picked it back up I couldn't stop until I was done.

5 stars and then some! I will definitely reread this in the future.

Title: Indigo
Author: Beverly Jenkins
My Review:
Beverly Jenkins has been on my "I need to read this author" list for a very long time, and I'm really glad I finally got around to one of hers, because this was a lovely book. I don't gravitate toward historical romances set in America, so I was unsure whether the setting would grab me, but the element of the Underground Railroad made it so interesting I couldn't resist. It added a nice level of suspense that I wasn't expecting, though not too much. I loved the progression of Hester and Galen's relationship. The relationship itself was fairly low conflict, and I found I didn't mind it with the setting supplying just enough conflict to keep things interesting. I found myself admiring Hester's determination to set boundaries (even when she ultimately lost the battle) but also her ability to admit she wanted and accept luxuries Galen could offer on occasion. She felt very realistic to me. Also the ending nearly made me cry, which doesn't happen often. Bravo, Ms. Jenkins.

4.5 stars

Title: Bone Crier's Moon (Bone Grace #1)
Author: Kathryn Purdie
My Review:
I'm genuinely shocked by how much I did not enjoy this book. I love the premise, and the characters were fine, but I didn't find the way it developed at all satisfying. The magic system was interesting, but every new development/twist seemed convenient rather than foreshadowed. Sometimes the world seems to hold to its own rules, and sometimes it doesn't. The romance was only one step shy of insta-love. The antagonist was wishy-washy, because while their motives were theoretically clear, the execution of steps to achieve those motives made very little sense. The twists made me roll my eyes, and there were enough things left to be resolved in a sequel that I found the ending deeply unsatisfying, too. Though, honestly, by that point I didn't really care how it ended. I was just ready to be done reading.

2.5 stars, rounded up to 3 for an interesting concept and generally decent writing.

Title: Prevailed Upon to Marry (In Love with Mr Darcy)
Author: Mary Smythe
My Review:
There were some things I enjoyed about this story and others that I did not, overall adding up to a novel that was entertaining but is not taking a place among my favorites. I liked the premise and the way that Jane ushered it along, and the way that Darcy and Elizabeth worked out their differences was pretty satisfying, if a little rushed for the sake of plot needs. I did not like the overlapping repetition of story beats between POVs, nor did I like having so many POVs in general. No one likes Mr. Collins, right, but I didn't need his insipid POV to convince me of that or attempt to inflate the tension. Lady Catherine was similarly annoying and useless, and even the Jane and Mr. Bennet POVs could have been left out without really sacrificing much of the story. I think this could have been a novella focused on Darcy and Elizabeth with the same story and better effect. (Honestly, there were parts I skimmed, and I didn't miss anything necessary.)

But I did have a good couple of hours reading it, and I wouldn't hesitate to recommend this to someone interested in the premise. Solid 3 stars.

Title: A Vow So Bold and Deadly (Cursebreakers, #3)
Author: Brigid Kemmerer
My Review:
I was really afraid going in that I wouldn't end up liking this book. Book 2 really threw me, both because of the change in POV characters and some of the actions of familiar characters. I was so afraid that I bought this book on release day and then proceeded to wait 2.5 years to actually read it.

Silly, silly me.

While I can't say I adored this installment quite as much as I did book 1 as a standalone, I ultimately had a great time with it and blew through it in an afternoon. I had no reason to worry, as the things I didn't like were mostly addressed here, and some of them in a very satisfying way. The only thing that detracted from my enjoyment was was the split POV. I like Lia Mara fine, but she never quite wormed her way into my heart like the others. Her POV pulled me away from the characters I really cared about, so even though her POV was necessary for plot purposes, I could've done without her.

This was a satisfying conclusion, though. Parts I really loved and appreciated, especially at the end when Rhen and Grey got to just be brothers. I liked the progression of the plot, and all of the worries I had about lack of communication driving things were unfounded. Even when those plot points existed, it was only temporarily. Rhen told Harper about Lilith at 15% and I was so relieved.

4.5 stars rounded down, because 4 stars is a fitting rating for the series as a whole. Now I'm really curious to read other reviews, because I thought I heard this book wasn't particularly well received, and I'd really like to see the criticism, even if I had a good time.

Added after reading some of those reviews:

I liked the ending for Rhen. He got what he deserved, and by that I mean he now gets to rest. For so long, he's had the weight of the kingdom on his shoulders along with all the trauma of the curse, and he needs to focus on himself so he can heal. Grey wants him around to help and you know he'll treat his brother well, but ultimately Rhen doesn't have to make the hard decisions anymore. Personally, I think it would be a relief..

Title: A Court of Mist and Fury (A Court of Thorns and Roses, #2)
Author: Sarah J. Maas
My Review:
Well. I didn't see that ending coming. Though, knowing that there's a sequel series I'm amused at how Feyre's sister becoming immortal is supposedly a bad thing.

It took me so long to read this book (because I'm in a reading slump - not because it's at all bad) that my thoughts on it are rather scattered and fuzzy. BUT. I'm so glad I got spoiled on the character shift that happens in this book compared to the first. I had to sort of reset my brain, but it was worth it. This book was glorious. Loved everything about it.

Especially the found family. I'm straight up a sucker for those.

An unreserved 5 stars from me.

Title: Longbourn's Unexpected Matchmaker
Author: Emma  Hox
My Review:
​I liked the general storyline of this book, where Mr. Bennet plays a sort of father figure to Darcy and guides him along the path to winning Elizabeth's affections. However, the whole thing was marred by tedious, wooden, unnatural dialogue. All hellos and goodbyes are unnecessarily detailed, and the dialogue often repeated what was just mentioned in the narrative. (Elizabeth decided to tell Jane the story. "Jane, I'm going to tell you the story." That's not a quote, but you get the idea.) The conflicts all resolve very quickly and mostly through coincidental occurrences--people happen to hear/find things or be in the right place at the right time, and we're never really given time to fear for Darcy and Elizabeth's happiness. I don't think either of them are ever directly impacted at all. Also, the last 1/3 of the book pretty much revolved around the day-to-day of wedding planning and prep and had no conflict at all.

The audiobook was well done, and the narrator did the best she could at making the dialogue she had sound good. Reading in that format definitely did highlight the dialogue issues, and I might have been able to ignore them a little better if I'd been able to visually skim instead.

Title: Ruin and Rising (The Shadow and Bone Trilogy, #3)
Author: Leigh Bardugo
My Review:
Okay, THIS is the book I was waiting for this whole series. By chapter two, I was sucked in hard and couldn't put it down. (I finished it in a day.) I genuinely had no idea where it was going, and it continued to surprise me. I could see where little seeds had been planted, but those moments still sneaked up on me. It's not the ending to the trilogy I would have envisioned, but the more I think about it, the more I think it couldn't have ended any other way that would have been more satisfying.

Though I'll admit I kind of wanted Alina to figure out a way to have Mal be the last amplifier without needing to kill him. It would have been satisfying to have her promise to kill him and then refuse to do it at the last moment, just like he did for her in book 2. Nikolai is still my favorite, though. I got so excited when I saw him called "King of Scars" because I remembered that was one of the duologies in this world. Really looking forward to reading more of him.

Six of Crows is already sitting next to me, waiting for me to start it.

4.5 stars rounded up.

Title: Siege and Storm (Shadow and Bone, #2)
Author: Leigh Bardugo
My Review:
Did I like this book? Yes. Was it infuriating? SAINTS, yes. I really wanted to strangle Alina for half the book. Her lack of communication with Mal was absolutely ridiculous, and it's a storytelling trope I hate. That said, I liked the way the book continued to build the war with the Darkling, I liked the new characters (Nikolai especially), and the ending sequence was particularly riveting. There were some excellent reveals in this book, and that really saved it for me.

3 stars, because Alina was annoying, but ultimately the story worked.

Title: Shadow and Bone (Shadow and Bone, #1)
Author: Leigh Bardugo
My Review:
*5/8/2023*

Finally got around to really reading this book (on audio). I enjoyed it. I can't say I was engrossed or that it's a favorite read, but it did a really good job setting up the world, and it made me want to continue with the series. It's a bit too heavy on the teen angst for my taste, but I sort of expected it for the territory, so it was fine. Though, honestly? I liked the Netflix series better.

3.5 stars rounded up.

*12/12/2021*

Technically speaking, I've finished this book. But I skimmed it because I was too late trying to finish it for a book club, and that's really not sufficient for me. I want to start this one over at some point and blast through the trilogy.

Title: A Night to Surrender (Spindle Cove, #1)
Author: Tessa Dare
My Review:
Really enjoyable read. I love the world, and Susanna and Bram were both great characters. Their secrets were reasonable and shared when it felt appropriate, rather than held for overly long. I hate it when conflicts feel artificial, and withholding of personal information seems to be a common one--so it was nice to see things handled well here. I really felt their emotional connection, and I could believe they were falling in love. I admit I was a little confused at the end when Susanna ended up with broken ribs and a collapsed lung, but I suspect I might have been reading too fast and just skimmed past an important paragraph that would have made it make sense.

The secondary characters shine, too. I know some of them are sequel bait, but even those that aren't (yet?) made the story very colorful. I especially liked the twins...and Colin (who, yay, is hero of book 2, so.). I'm glad I listened to all the praise for these and bought books 1-3 at various times when amazon had them on sale, because now I have them to devour!

Title: Dark Moon Defender (Twelve Houses, #3)
Author: Sharon Shinn
My Review:
This one is more like 4.5 stars instead of a solid 5, but I'm rounding up out of general love for the series and the characters. My biggest quasi-complaint is that out of the core 6 characters, only Justin gets any solid screen time. It was nice that Senneth and Tayse had some scenes of their own, but I would have liked to have the others around for a larger chunk of the book. I liked Ellynor well enough, but her POV was not nearly as enthralling as the rest of the gang, and that's my reason for deducting that whopping half of a star.

Despite my lesser love for Ellynor, my absolute favorite scene of the whole book is the one near the end where she finds Justin almost dead. Oh MAN the feels. Though, to be fair, that might largely be because of how much I love Justin.

I like how we got to see the carry-through from book 2, with Kirra proclaiming that Justin is her brother. I felt that about their relationship long before either acknowledged it, which just made Justin's realization about it that much stronger. I also liked that Kirra got to talk to Senneth a bit about how things were going with her and Donnal. And though I love a super happy ending, her proclamation that she would probably never get married and her explanation for it made me appreciate Kirra more, particularly after her bad life decisions in book 2. I really appreciate that their relationship is unique to them, and that it doesn't try to be anything that it's not.

"I think I own him, Senneth. I think there is nothing I could do to drive him away. That moves and frightens me and makes me want to be very, very careful with his heart."

And? Tayse is THE MAN. I loved his marriage proposal. That took some serious guts of a very different kind than the guts he usually displays. Oh! And how about the sneaky revelation about Queen Valri? Not that I hadn't already guessed that she was Lirren, because duh, but I was really amused at how off-handedly that was thrown in.

I still LOVE LOVE LOVE these books. I'm taking a short break before I read book 4, partially to let a friend who's on book 1 catch up, and partially because I don't want them to end!

A side note: I listened to about 80% of this book on audio, like I did entirely with the first 2 books, and I was not impressed. It switched to a male narrator, since Justin is the main POV character in this book, and he was not good at doing the female voices. Or keeping the accents straight. Donnal had an Irish accent, even though he didn't have one in the first 2 books. (Different narrator, yeah, but I would have liked some consistency.) I got used to that eventually, but then Cammon's accent started fluctuating toward Irish, too, despite not being at all Irish at the beginning of the book. So yeah. While I highly recommend the audio for books 1 and 2, I think I'm going to stick with the kindle version for book 4.

Title: Troubled Waters (Elemental Blessings, #1)
Author: Sharon Shinn
My Review:
I've officially decided that I need to read everything Sharon Shinn has written. I can't say that I love this as much as [b:Mystic and Rider|97967|Mystic and Rider (Twelve Houses, #1)|Sharon Shinn|https://d.gr-assets.com/books/1388338711s/97967.jpg|94425], but that's because I really loved that one, and it's hard to compare. I came into this one a little wary and afraid of disappointment, but the audiobook was done by the same narrator as the previously-mentioned favorite (Jennifer Van Dyck), so I couldn't resist at least trying it. (It's amazing how much of a difference a good narrator makes...see my review of [b:Dark Moon Defender|97960|Dark Moon Defender (Twelve Houses, #3)|Sharon Shinn|https://d.gr-assets.com/books/1404479543s/97960.jpg|2727] for more on that.)

I honestly wasn't quite sure where this book was going at first. I didn't go in knowing much other than the book description, and I didn't understand what it even meant (coru prime? huh?). Zoe zipped from one event in her life to another, picking up friends and enemies along the way. You could really feel the "coru" in her (which you won't understand till you read it) by the way the book flowed. She's impulsive, doesn't always make the best snap judgement calls, and yet she's so genuine that you can't help but like her.

There's just enough political intrigue in here to satisfy me without getting annoying, probably because Zoe cares about as much about politics as I do, heh. There are tons of characters and nearly all of them were vibrant...even people like Foley, who aren't in it that much compared to others. My only really complaint--and my reason for deducting a star--is that Zoe judges people based on appearance ("he looked mean and she didn't like him"...that sort of thing) and was almost always right about that perception. I think there was some vague mention of it being one of her coru powers, but it was a little too on the nose for me. At least it was consistent.

I gather that the reviews of book two aren't stellar...but then, they weren't stellar for [b:The Thirteenth House|97962|The Thirteenth House (Twelve Houses, #2)|Sharon Shinn|https://d.gr-assets.com/books/1388350576s/97962.jpg|94421] either, and I liked that one just fine. :-) Already used my lovely audible subscription to grab [b:Royal Airs|13578466|Royal Airs (Elemental Blessings, #2)|Sharon Shinn|https://d.gr-assets.com/books/1364887334s/13578466.jpg|19161968] and hope to enjoy it very soon.

Title: The Prince and the Ice King (Tales of the Gemstone Kingdoms #1)
Author: Amanda Meuwissen
My Review:
I debated giving this book 5 stars, because I liked it a lot. Upon further reflection, I can't quite do that, though take this with a grain of salt. Depending on your mood or what you're hoping to get out of a book, this one still might check all your boxes.

There are two (related) reasons for knocking off a star:

- Reardon has no flaws. He's the sweetest golden retriever of a character, but does not learn or change.
- The book gives the appearance of having high conflict while resolving most of it very quickly through Reardon's pluck and sweetness.

I'm not mad about either of these things—especially the second, as I can have a low tolerance for overly angsty books with interpersonal conflicts that linger unnecessarily. I would rather fall on this side of the fence than the other. However, it does mean that this book is less likely to linger in my head as the ultimate in satisfactory resolutions, because I was never really tense or afraid it wouldn't be resolved the way I liked. That's a weird line to walk in a romance, because you basically expect an HEA, but I need to feel like they worked for it. And they did, in this case...just not very hard.

Reardon was adorable, and I liked him as a person, but everyone was quick to fall prey to his puppy eyes and optimism. If not for the explicit sex scenes, this would have been a super light read.

So yeah. Very enjoyable read with some forgivable flaws. 4 stars.

Title: I'm Glad My Mom Died
Author: Jennette McCurdy
My Review:
My comparative age means that, while I was aware of its existence, I never watched iCarly and was only somewhat aware of it and its stars. So when buzz started circulating about this book, I was intrigued by the title but otherwise didn't give it much thought. Fast forward a couple of weeks, and the more I saw and heard about it, the more and more curious I became—which says a lot, because I really don't read much non-fiction like this. I just need to state for the record that I actually paid full price on kindle for this (something I do maybe once every five years), so I was prepared to be royally pissed off if I found it an irritating read.

What I found was an interesting and difficult read, but one that kept me glued to the page with its engaging flow and construction. It's hard to call a tale full of narcissistic abuse and eating disorders "entertaining," but I read it straight through in an afternoon and do not regret it. I won't comment on the author's life or story more than that, but I can say that this book makes me look forward to things she might write in the future. If she can do justice to the intense and often disturbing topics here, I can only imagine what she can do with things she finds entertaining.

Title: Winter's Orbit
Author: Everina Maxwell
My Review:
Holy WOW this book was spectacular! I have a feeling it's going to fall into that category where I loved it so much that I will find it difficult to say why, but I'll do my best.

Prince Kiem has to marry Count Jainan for speedy political reasons tied to the fate of their treaty with big galactic powers, despite the fact that Jainan's previous partner only died a month prior. What follows is one of the best (if not the best) arranged marriage stories I've ever read. There are misunderstandings, but they are logical ones, and often wrapped around information that you as the reader don't have yet (and that is withheld in realistic ways). The romance is central, but for a large chunk of the book it is not "romance" in the way that romance novels often go. (Don't get me wrong, because I also read romance novels, but the romantic interest is typically very clear in the way characters think about/see each other. And while it's clear that there is attraction, it is far more subtle and secondary to everything else that is happening. They have better things to do than constantly think about how hot the other one is. It's different, but still very satisfying.) These two are getting to know one another, learning about each other, stumbling and making mistakes—all while trying to solve serious diplomatic/ legal issues and unravel several mysteries. This had the perfect balance for me between the sci-fi and the romance, and I didn't feel that either was overshadowed by the other. Neither could have been removed without affecting the story, because they were both essential to the plot and character arcs.

There's a lot I don't want to say because of spoilers, but I will only add that if you have any triggers, you may want to review content warnings for this book, because it definitely deals with some things. Just be aware that it's a pretty big spoiler (although one that I figured out—or was pretty sure I had figured out, then was eventually confirmed—about 1/3 into it).

5 stars. Definitely a favorite of the year so far.

Title: His Uncle's Favorite
Author: Lory Lilian
My Review:
Reread 10/12/16

Enjoyed it just as much the second time around, though I did manage to force myself to spread it over a few days rather than binge-reading it.

Original read 2/11/16

I stayed up way too late last night reading this book. I blame it for the fact that I'm still droopy-eyed this evening, even after much caffeine. I often find it difficult to write reviews for books I loved beyond AJLJKLFEDJALJFDALDAOMGOMGOMGOMG <3 <3 <3, and especially when I've had too little sleep, but I will do my best.

One of my frequent complaints with variations is that Elizabeth and Darcy resolve their differences too quickly, and especially that Elizabeth decides she's in love with Darcy too easily. This book is a great example of the relationship progression done well. Darcy is still desperately resisting her for the first chunk of the book, and there's none of the gushy, sappy stuff even once they realize their feelings. They both realized their faults and sought to address them, and I felt they truly deserved one another by the time things were resolved between them.

As well as Elizabeth and Darcy were done, I think the secondary characters really make this book shine. Lord Matlock and Mrs. Gardiner, in particular, were fabulous. I found myself laughing aloud at something the earl said on more than one occasion, and I liked that there was an explanation for why he was ready to accept Elizabeth and Jane into his social circle despite their different spheres. Somehow it didn't occur to me until about 50% that them both being widowed was the perfect opportunity for the two of them to get married, but that made it all the more rewarding when it happened. I loved the interaction between Darcy and Mr. Bennet. I loved that Lydia was kept home from Brighton and still managed to elope with Wickham. I loved how lines from the original ended up (brilliantly) spoken by completely different characters.

In short, I loved it all.

Title: To Conquer Mr. Darcy
Author: Abigail Reynolds
My Review:
One thing I'll say for Abigail Reynolds is that she excels at producing strong emotional reactions. Even when I was unsure how to feel about this story, the quality of the prose and how it made me feel was never in question.

I was hesitant to even attempt this story, because I knew that there was premarital sex. I'm not opposed to that in general, nor am I offended by sex in JAFF stories, but I do have a difficult time seeing Elizabeth and Darcy acting in that manner. There's a lot at stake for them, and their characters as established by the source material don't really allow for that sort of behavior. In this story, Elizabeth and Darcy are very liberal with their physical affection even before they finally do the deed. I did appreciate that there was significant buildup to that moment, and it did feel like a natural progression. However, I basically had to force myself to consider their physicality a specific point of the variation - i.e., that it was intentionally not supposed to be the Elizabeth and Darcy that I know. Once I did that, I was able to enjoy the story for what it was rather than what my preconceived notions expected it to be.

This is a very sexy story. I listened to the audio book, and that tends to make any emotional highs and lows that much more extreme, and this was no exception. I think it's partially because it keeps me from reading through it as fast and partially because a good narrator is worth her weight in gold. I do have to admit that while the sensuality of the private moments was appealing, there were times when I wanted it to ease up so Elizabeth and Darcy could just TALK. Their misunderstandings and disagreements didn't drag on forever, as is sometimes a problem in really angsty books, but I would have liked for them to have at least a couple of conversations where they kept their hands (and other parts) to themselves but weren't arguing.

There were times during their courtship that Elizabeth grated on my nerves and I felt really bad for Darcy. I know she was supposed to be conflicted about her feelings for him but she held the real power in the relationship--I honestly think one word from her could have stopped his attentions at any time--and poor Darcy just got strung along. I spent a good part of the first half wishing that I wasn't losing respect for her. It did get better, though. By the time they ended up at Pemberley, I no longer had issues with the story and quite happily finished listening.

The plot of this story was nothing revolutionary, but it did serve as a suitable backdrop for the real tale, which was the emotional journey they faced. I can't say this is my favorite P&P variation, nor even my favorite Abigail Reynolds, but it was enjoyable even with its faults (and my dislike of the circumstances), and I will probably read it again sometime.
Private Notes: pre-marital sex

Title: What Would Mr Darcy Do?: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: Abigail Reynolds
My Review:
Reread (audio version) 2/19/16

Yes, it's been less than a month since I read this, but I needed something that I knew I would enjoy that was available on audio. My general impressions were largely the same--though somehow the kisses that Elizabeth and Darcy shared prior to their marriage seemed far more sensual when read aloud. Probably due to my tendency to speed read.

One thing I will say about this, though, is that I was not fond of the narrator. Pearl Hewitt had a pleasant voice and did a good job with all of the females, but I hated her male voices. I've encountered that problem with male narrators doing female voices, but this is the first time I've had it with a female narrator. She made Darcy sound smarmy and irritating (seriously, I expected to get used to it by the end, but I really didn't), and Mr. Gardiner was even worse. I far preferred Darcy as done by Rachel Hurley in [b:The Last Man in The World|1588597|The Last Man in The World|Abigail Reynolds|https://d.gr-assets.com/books/1185539970s/1588597.jpg|34265490] and Kate Reading in [b:Pride and Prejudice|1885|Pride and Prejudice|Jane Austen|https://d.gr-assets.com/books/1320399351s/1885.jpg|3060926].

First read 1/24/16

This was a very low conflict story, and the variation actually changed very little about how events unfolded (mostly timing), and yet it still managed to be interesting and entertaining. I kept expecting to get bored, but I didn't. One of the biggest changes from the original, and consequently my favorite thing about this book, was that Georgiana and Elizabeth had a lot of correspondence and Georgiana spent time at Longbourn. I love Georgiana when she's done well, and so it was nice to get to see more of her.

Don't go into this one expecting anything revolutionary, but it's a nice place to get your P&P fix for a few hours.

Title: By Force of Instinct: A Pride & Prejudice Variation
Author: Abigail Reynolds
My Review:
This book wasn't awful, but I couldn't give it 3 stars simply because I consider 3-star books to be good enough to recommend to someone who likes the genre, and this isn't one I'd particularly recommend. That said, I wouldn't warn someone away, either. It's a benign 2.5 stars for me. I skimmed a lot of it and rolled my eyes through other bits, but I did finish it, and at least 50% of it was fine, if nothing special.

Hiding the rest of this review, as I'm not going to hold back on spoilers. I had a whole list of things immediately after I read it, but I've read something else since then, and I don't care enough to try and remember everything. Still, here are a few specific things I liked and disliked:

1) I liked Darcy joining in on gatherings and discussions with his "lessers" and learning things from them, to his benefit and growth. I really liked the idea that Elizabeth had introduced him to people who could expand his social circle and introduce him to opinions and ideas he wouldn't necessarily encounter in his accustomed society.

2) I did not like the premarital sex. I can buy it in a romance novel about people from the same social classes as these characters, but this is Elizabeth and Darcy, for crying out loud. I felt they were very out of character here. I mean, come on. The Darcy I know would never encourage Elizabeth to sneak out of her house in the middle of the night to meet him at an abandoned house to have sex. And the Elizabeth I know would never risk disgracing her family with that sort of behavior, even if she was tempted. The whole thing was just ridiculous.

3) I hated the way Mr. Bennet and Georgiana were used in out of character ways just to fuel the misunderstandings and artificial conflict. Georgiana was just a brat, and I don't think that the departure point for this variation was a justification for so completely changing her character. I could have believed Mr. Bennet's behavior if he'd relented when Lizzy had a frank conversation with him, but his obstinate attitude was silly.

Title: Mr. Darcy's Noble Connections
Author: Abigail Reynolds
My Review:
Re-read 3/9/17

Raising rating from 2 stars to 3 stars. My original annoyances with this book still stand; however, I think I have a better appreciation now for the Abigail Reynolds style. She does have a way with characters that I appreciate, as they're vibrant even when they're inconsistent like they sometimes are here. It's not my favorite of her books, but some of the plot points bothered me less for having read more JAFF and more AR.

Original read 7/8/15

This is a weird one for me to rate. The story was an easy read, and I read part of it through listening to the audiobook, which was narrated fabulously. However, there were things that bothered me about it.

1) It annoyed me that Darcy not only had two other close friends, but that Bingley ranked third among them. Edward Carlisle is his oldest friend, and then Paxton was someone they met at school, and lastly Paxton introduced him to Bingley. I know Bingley can't be his only friend in the world, but for a reserved person like Darcy who's not overly social, having all three of them annoyed me. And Bingley didn't appear at all, so it made him seem completely unimportant.

2) Charles Carlisle is pretty ridiculous. One minute he's honest and charming. Then he's making bets about ruining Elizabeth. Then he's blackmailing Darcy to marry his sister by threatening to ruin Georgiana. Then he's being honest and charming again. Then he's asking Elizabeth to marry him. Then he's physically fighting over her. Then he's a loving and welcoming younger brother and nice guy again. I'm assuming the author was trying emulate the "is he or isn't he a good guy?" thing that was going on with Wickham, but it just felt inconsistent to me.

3) It bugs me a bit that Elizabeth and Darcy were actively supporting and helping their friends have a clandestine and very improper relationship. I can maybe see Elizabeth going for it on some levels, but definitely not Darcy. And also, the way Elizabeth and Darcy get together is basically because of improper physical things they do that convince them that they are in love. This feels like taking the easy way out, and I would have much rather seen them reach this conclusion through witty and heartfelt conversations.

4) Most importantly, why the hell does Darcy need to have migraines to explain his antisocial and taciturn tendencies???? He's allowed to have actual faults and is a better character because he does.

So yeah. 3 stars for the writing itself, which was fine. 2 stars for the plot and 2 stars for the characterization.

Title: The Man Who Loved Pride and Prejudice: A modern love story with a Jane Austen twist
Author: Abigail Reynolds

Title: Mr. Darcy's Refuge: A Pride & Prejudice Variation
Author: Abigail Reynolds
My Review:
Not my favorite by this author, but entertaining. I stayed up way too late reading it all in one sitting (and now regret my life choices), so that gives you an idea of my enjoyment.

This book contained two things that, if done even halfway decently, will always be a win for me. First, it starts as a forced proximity romance. It's no surprise that I enjoyed this element, considering [b:Alone with Mr. Darcy: A Pride & Prejudice Variation|25379609|Alone with Mr. Darcy A Pride & Prejudice Variation|Abigail Reynolds|https://i.gr-assets.com/images/S/compressed.photo.goodreads.com/books/1429321551l/25379609._SY75_.jpg|45005740] is one of my favorites. Second, Jane ends up with Colonel Fitzwilliam, and I was so excited when I realized the plot was going this direction.

Darcy was pretty obnoxious in the beginning here, an impression probably exacerbated by starting the book in his POV. I enjoyed the shift in his manners and thinking as the book went on, but sometimes his thoughts felt oddly expressed in the text. It's hard to explain what I mean here, but basically I think it comes down to a lack of subtlety. Maybe this is an impression marred by getting sucked into the story, but I think the author put a lot more focus on his bad attributes through hearing his thoughts, but covered his good attributes through his actions and spoken words. Maybe that was necessary, given that Darcy is keeping his intentions close at the beginning, but the sheer amount of internal monologue feels imbalanced as a result. The proposal itself is probably sufficient to show he needs some self-reflection. This is my primary reason for deducting a star.

I didn't love Mr. Bennet (how could I?), and while the history provided for his actions helps some, I didn't like how the information was dropped into the middle of the story without any prior hints about it. The saving grace is that Mr. Bennet did eventually relent and watch and listen...sort of. (Also, it's better than Mr. Bennet in [b:By Force of Instinct|1620450|By Force of Instinct|Abigail Reynolds|https://i.gr-assets.com/images/S/compressed.photo.goodreads.com/books/1266848870l/1620450._SY75_.jpg|34277222].) I did love his relationship with Colonel Fitzwilliam, and their interactions often made me laugh.

The best bits of this are, obviously, the interactions between Darcy and Elizabeth as they get to know one another better. I liked that the middle section is not drawn out for dramatics and angst, and found that it was just the right amount of both. Elizabeth's sass and willingness to stand up for herself and others was center stage and I loved it. Mary's role in the story is relatively small but also nice for a change. Lydia and Kitty are off stage for 99% of the story, and I'm not mad about it. Mrs. Bennet is at maybe 95%. Also not mad.

Overall very good, and I'm happy to add this to the list of Abigail Reynolds books I've enjoyed. She has written many of my top JAFF stories, and while this isn't one of them, I'm sure it will go on the reread list all the same. Solid 4 stars.

Title: The Last Man in the World
Author: Abigail Reynolds
Bookshelves: austen-and-austenesque, austenesque-challenge-2016, favorites, library-audible, library-kindle, library-paper
Bookshelves with positions: austen-and-austenesque (#80), austenesque-challenge-2016 (#14), favorites (#16), library-audible (#130), library-kindle (#1007), library-paper (#2609)
My Review:
This is one of my favorite variations so far (and I've read quite a lot of them!). I think I'm drawn to the ones where they're forced to marry early because it takes away many of the restrictions and allows them to spend a much larger portion of the story in each other's company. It's very compelling to have them learning to love one another in close quarters.

I liked that the misunderstandings between Darcy and Elizabeth were not silly things--there were potentially serious consequences that caused each to worry. I thought Darcy's reaction to learn the truth about Elizabeth's feelings was very characteristic Darcy, and the ferocity of that reaction made parts of the story very intense. I felt bad for both Elizabeth and Georgiana that they didn't have the same closeness early on, but that was also interesting to read. They had to work a little harder to understand one another.

I liked this one enough that I'm almost tempted to read it again immediately, mostly because I blazed through that first reading so fast! Highly recommended.

Title: Alone with Mr. Darcy: A Pride & Prejudice Variation
Author: Abigail Reynolds
My Review:
I loved this book! I've read a couple of books that used the device of one of them being injured or the weather causing them to be stuck together and thus Elizabeth being compromised, but this is probably my favorite. I adored Darcy throughout this whole book, and Elizabeth was pretty well done, too. I appreciated that the things that kept them apart were something other than one or the other of them being completely unreasonable. Certainly, their decisions affected the situations, but a lot of it came from external sources, and it was entertaining (and often agonizing) to see how they dealt with it all.

I think one of my favorites elements, outside of Elizabeth and Darcy themselves, was Colonel Fitzwilliam's PTSD. It wasn't a huge part of the story and didn't in any way detract from the main plot, but it also wasn't just tacked on. It felt like an important part of his character and also influenced Darcy to a certain extent. I absolutely loved it during the wedding breakfast when Darcy insisted that Richard sell his commission and come live at Pemberley, without even a second of hesitation. And while I'm on the subject of Richard...him plotting with Charlotte was adorable, and I was totally wishing that they could get married. Abigail Reynolds is totally missing some sequel bait here.

There were some other things that I found interesting and/or refreshing, even if they didn't qualify as favorite things, per se. For one, Anne had a strong personality, and I was shocked to find out that she was married--I had been expecting that she wanted to marry the doctor, but not that she already had. For another, making Georgiana a half sister and adding the subplot with her mother was an interesting choice. Lastly, I was surprised that the Jane/Bingley storyline didn't come into play. I kept expecting that to be the thing to once again separate Elizabeth and Darcy, and I was pleasantly surprised when it didn't. Even if it was a logical place to go, it was kind of nice for a story to not for once.

Title: Knight of Ghosts and Shadows (Bedlam's Bard, #1)
Author: Mercedes Lackey
My Review:
I read this book for the first time a long time ago. I don't remember exactly when, but I think in the early 2000s range. Really the only thing I remembered about it was a Renaissance festival, a hero who played the flute, elves, and a vague sort of poly-amorous thing.

As it turns out, that's basically the whole book, ha!

This is a relatively short book with a relatively basic plot: bad guy wants magic for himself, good guy has to learn his abilities and save magic for the elves. It was actually nice after reading a couple of longer books before this to find something that was not quite as involved but was still a satisfying novel length. It's a very easy and enjoyable read with one very big exception - and that's the incredibly overwhelming amount of italicized internal thoughts from whatever POV character we're with in a given scene. Sometimes they take up half a page, I swear. I think it's just an older writing style (god, the '90s is "older" now...*shudder*) that has largely fallen out of favor, but MAN does it feel egregious even in that.

Oh and, okay, the "romance" is a bit insta-lovey, but not terrible. For a short book like this, I thought it was fine, especially since it's a sub plot. But I'm very curious to see how things develop in later books, as I don't remember if I read past book one the first time I read this.

Probably actually 3.5 stars rounded up, but I could be talked into 4 stars simply because I enjoy the idea of elves in L.A.

Title: Bitten (Women of the Otherworld, #1)
Author: Kelley Armstrong
My Review:
After hearing people talk about it for years, I really expected to like this book more than I did. That's not to say I disliked it, but there was something about the writing style and method of storytelling that rankled. Given that this was her first novel, I'm hopeful that things evolve for the better, but for now there were things I didn't care for. The biggest was how so many things were thrown in as semi-flashback afterthoughts, like, oh by the way here's this info you need to know that I forgot to mention. Let me take two pages to tell you about it now. Similarly, there are a few times where the narrator drops the story to speak to the reader directly in sort of conversational tone. I realize this is a thing that can happen in first person, but it felt out of place compared to the other 95% of the time. The "action" was also rather anti-climactic, with most of the narrative focusing on in-between than on the bad things happening. I never really felt that the main character was truly in danger, because the story never dwelled in those dangerous moments for long enough.

In short, I think it could have used some work.

The story itself was fine. Elena was a little annoying in her obstinance, but I was able to roll with it for the most part. Clay was equally annoying at times, but Phillip was just so blah boring that I was ready to throw the book if she didn't ditch him at the end. Mostly I enjoyed the secondary members of the pack and their relationships with one another. The actual world drawn around this group seemed strange to me, with so few werewolves in the world, but I'll save my judgement on that for reading later books, since I know the series gets into other supernaturals.

I finished the whole thing, so obviously it wasn't terrible, but I was sort of scratching my head the whole time and wondering why people loved it so much. More like 3.5 stars but rounded down out of disappointment.

Title: Shards of Honor (Vorkosigan Saga, #1)
Author: Lois McMaster Bujold
My Review:
This is the story of Cordelia Naismith who ends up entangled with Aral Vorkosigan when things go bad on their respective missions to an uninhabited planet. About the first third of the book follows that part of the story. There are two time jumps after that, each with their own continuation of the tale, allowing the characters time and distance to work through everything that has happened to them.

I appreciate Bujold's writing style, which is direct, with few frills, and doesn't handhold or info dump you. I've read her Sharing Knife series (beginning with [b:Beguilement|61898|Beguilement (The Sharing Knife, #1)|Lois McMaster Bujold|https://i.gr-assets.com/images/S/compressed.photo.goodreads.com/books/1278374915l/61898._SY75_.jpg|1520541]), so I was already familiar with this aspect. It was a little jarring, coming from books where that was not the case, but I fell quickly into sync with it. It's not always perfect, because there were definitely places (particularly after time jumps) that I had a hard time figuring out what had happened in the interim and what was currently happening. That was particularly the case in the second third of the book, as I both felt like I lacked context and was thrown in the middle of some pretty rough stuff. (Content warning for threat of rape and mentions of prior rapes to mostly unseen characters. I didn't find it overly graphic, but YMMV.) It doesn't help that there are roughly 6 billion characters, a lot of strange names (some that are very similar), and they are difficult to keep apart sometimes. I did eventually find my feet, though, and really enjoyed it overall.

The build of the romance subplot is very subtle, almost undescribed. It's not bad just because it's also not full-on romance novel to the point of almost over describing. (I read romance novels, so this is not really a criticism. More a genre characteristic that can be jarring when it's applied outside of novels intended for a romance reader.) I was able to accept it because these are not overly-dramatic teenagers we're following, and to a point it made sense that they weren't overly...showy with their feelings. I did appreciate the passage of time in the story and thus the gradual nature of their relationship. It made it infinitely more believable and was probably the main reason I could accept it despite its quietness. Also, I just liked both of them, so I really wanted them with each other.

The military maneuverings, war, political power plays, etc. are almost incidental to Cordelia and Vorkosigan coming together. That's not to say that the book is overly focused on their romance (it's not), but they find mutual respect and friendship, learn about each other's cultures, defend and protect one another, go through some seriously dangerous situations together, and then there's a romance. In that way, it's just the kind of story I like.

4 stars, because it isn't a perfect book, but it's definitely the sort I'd reread, and I will continue the series. From reading the notes at the end, the author recommends [b:Barrayar|61905|Barrayar (Vorkosigan Saga, #7)|Lois McMaster Bujold|https://i.gr-assets.com/images/S/compressed.photo.goodreads.com/books/1397151220l/61905._SY75_.jpg|3036422] next....

Title: Rhapsodic (The Bargainer, #1)
Author: Laura Thalassa
My Review:
This is a very odd book for me to rate. First, it was not at all what I expected from hearing people talk about it on YouTube. There was sort of this dark, mysterious air around it, but that's not at all the experience I got from reading it. It's basically the story of a supernaturally gifted girl (siren) who makes a deal with "the devil" (or, in this case, a fae king) that turns into something of a forbidden romance, but with the resolution of said romantic tension happening seven years later, told in alternating timeline from the perspectives of her now and her younger self. Everything else is mostly set dressing. That's not to say it's bad—it's not—but I was expecting something far more revolutionary than this. It's also a bit of a cliffhanger, because the non-romance part of the plot is not resolved in this book.

The romance is one I've read before. Again, not bad, but I wanted something more from Desmond than I got. He's every bad boy from new adult lit, but with a fancy magical title. Callie is annoyingly oblivious. I put things together way before she did. I can see some of the reasoning for it, but that doesn't make it less annoying to read. Her human-world associates are just caricatures of people. She doesn't really grow or change. The only thing that changes is her understanding of Des. Which, fine, but it's a less satisfying narrative for it.

I'm going with 3.5 stars rounded down, not because I didn't enjoy reading this, but because it's one I can't see as worth revisiting in the future. It was fine, but nothing special.

Title: A Timely Elopement: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: Joana Starnes
My Review: This was one of the best paced and plotted short novels I've ever read. So often they don't seem to have sufficient pages to accomplish what the author sets out, but this one was very well done. I greatly enjoyed the way the plot unfolded. There is one thing that dropped my rating a whole star, and that is something that happens in the plot almost at the very end. <spoiler>Darcy shows up at Netherfield looking for Elizabeth. He sees two people kissing, assumes in jealousy that it's Elizabeth and his rival (an OC for this book), and angrily accuses her of it. She clears up the misunderstanding, he apologizes, and they immediately get engaged. I really, really didn't like that jealous rage. If it was going to happen, I would have preferred it earlier in the story. I had trouble accepting that it was something she could easily forgive, because it was SO extreme. If his reaction had been calmer (or sadder), I would have been fine with it.</spoiler>

Title: Locked at the Netherfield Ball: A Pride and Prejudice variation
Author: Layla Johnson
My Review: This story took an interesting approach to the locked in a room scenario, and the execution was fairly successful, though I think it lacked in some areas. Mostly, I thought Darcy's realization of his own faults was too quick to really be believable. There were also sections with excessive character navel-gazing that I ended up skimming once I caught on to them. The characters spoke a little freer than I've come to expect, and while I enjoy what that adds to the level of communication, it also hurts the realism for me. I did enjoy that the different choices he made brings things to a head faster, but in a way that's logical based on said choices. Overall, it was a quick, fun story. Nothing I'd plan on rereading, but I passed an enjoyable couple of hours with it.
