Title: Radiance (Wraith Kings, #1)
Author: Grace Draven
My Review:
I was mostly drawn to this story because the setup reminded me of one of my favorite Mass Effect fan fics. Perhaps sad, but true! The actual story bears no resemblance, but I like the idea of two people from disparate, nearly alien cultures going into an arranged marriage with a reasonable attitude about it. I enjoyed watching these two get to know one another, and the build of their relationship was very well done. It was nice watching the subtle changes in the ways they viewed one another, waiting for them to realize it themselves. There were a few little things that lowered my rating a little, such as the constant repetition of how ugly they thought each other was (yeah, I get it, really), and somewhat convenient plot contrivances throughout (since the relationship itself had, like, 1% conflict), but overall I really enjoyed it.

Solid 4 stars, and I can see myself reading this again in the future.

Title: A Better Understanding: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: Jennifer Kay
My Review:
This book may just have saved my reading month. After a couple of weeks of mostly lackluster reads, this was a breath of fresh air.

The point of variation for this book comes at the Netherfield ball, where Darcy and Elizabeth's conversation during their dance takes a different turn—one that is far more frank and informative than canon. It's a theme that continues through the book. Characters communicate far more frequently and clearly, often intuiting information from situations that they might not have before. It isn't entirely realistic (and my rating reflects that), but MAN was it refreshing to read. Sometimes it's just really, really nice when conflict can come from something other than misunderstanding and miscommunication. This novel is very much about the protagonists learning their lessons through softer, less harrowing means. The one except to that is the super quick and almost handwaving way that the Wickham storyline plays out. I didn't love it, but it was nice to get it out of the way, because the heavy struggles weren't really the point. These are reasonable people learning about each other in reasonable ways. (As you can imagine, Caroline Bingley is an exception here, but isn't she [almost] always?)

I don't always enjoy a low conflict story (or low intensity conflict), but I chose to read this one from my wish list because a) it had a 4+ star rating on goodreads, b) it was short, and c) it wasn't super expensive. My expectation was a nice palate cleanser after some crappy reads, and I was not disappointed. The writing is very good, so even the convenient plot contrivances and quick resolutions didn't bother me. I loved the characters, particularly Colonel Fitzwilliam and Lady Matlock (who are great favorites with me when handled well), and thought Elizabeth and Darcy were handled well. They had actual time to get to know one another better, and for their feelings to gradually shift/be realized, and I really thought they deserved their happy ending.

A very solid 4 stars. Definitely want to read more by this author.

Title: A Wizard of Earthsea (Earthsea Cycle, #1)
Author: Ursula K. Le Guin
My Review:
This book follows the wizard Sparrowhawk/Ged from his humble and curious beginnings, through his prideful youth, and into the hard years of his late teens as he handles the problems he's created for himself. Along the way he interacts with a number of characters, but the most notable are: Vetch, his only real friend, and Ogion, his wizard master. He learns magic, uses magic both dark and mundane, fights dragons, sails solo between distant islands, and has many other adventures.

I was already somewhat familiar with what I'd find here, because many years ago I watched the TV mini-series. (We're leaving discussions of that on the sidelines, as I don't care to spend enough words to really get into it. Suffice it to say that I enjoyed it for the cheese that it was, but I had no idea how unfaithful—in so many ways—it was to the source material.) I enjoyed the story that Le Guin spun here. Ged's early life is interesting, and he does a lot of interesting things. The magic is fascinating, particular in its use of true names, especially given when this story was written. Where I really struggled with the book was in the writing style, which was that of a narrator telling us a tale, much like a fairy tale. I felt very little connection to Ged or other characters, and I suspect that any I did feel was because of the inevitable insertion of my miniseries memories. There's a natural distance there, and it kept my emotions from really engaging in anything. The one exception was the death of his otak, but I can't help that I'm a big ole softy for pets in stories.

I'm glad that I read this, because now I know what it's all about. I haven't decided yet whether I want to continue the series. I do own the next two books, so I'm leaning toward probably. I enjoyed this as much as is possible for a story written in this style, it wasn't a difficult read, and I'm curious to see where things go next. All the same, I'm not sure when I'll prioritize them, because this wasn't anywhere close to a new favorite. It makes me sad, because I know how foundational this book was, but it is what it is.

Solid 3 stars. Nothing wrong with it, but not my preferred writing style.

Title: Annihilation (Southern Reach, #1)
Author: Jeff Vandermeer
My Review:
Annihilation is the story of a scientific research expedition into a mysterious, unknown region with goals that are fuzzy even to the people embarking on it. It is atmospheric, moody, deceptive, and vague, and I spent the entire book wondering if the narrator was reliable—which I think is intended, since it opens with discussion of previous researchers being delusional or otherwise acting strangely. It raises a lot of questions and answers almost none of them. It was creepy and often disturbing, but in ways that I found interesting and enjoyable ("enjoyable"—you know what I mean!). While I wouldn't call the ending dissatisfying within the context of what I believe was the story's intent, it was ultimately not the type of story I prefer.

I don't have too much else to say about it. I probably wouldn't continue on with the series, mostly due to my reading preferences than any particular faults of the story, EXCEPT that I poked at reviews for the next two books, and it appears that there may be some payoff for sticking with it that I would find appealing. The next two installments are significantly longer than this one, but still not so long that I wouldn't be willing to give them a try. I'm very tempted.

3.5 stars rounded down, but if you like atmospheric sci-fi/horror that doesn't seek to provide answers to the universe's oddities, I think you'd probably like this.

Title: Dune (Dune #1)
Author: Frank Patrick Herbert
My Review:
I'm going to attempt to be coherent with this review, but I have a feeling it's going to be the mental equivalent of keyboard smashing.

This book was SO GOOD, I LOVED it...and I'm struggling to put into words why. It's really, really different from anything I've ever read. I'm glad I was prepared for the head-hopping narration and that, honestly, my expectations were pretty low. I was hoping I was going to enjoy it, but I was expecting it to end up being Not My Thing. If I could describe the writing and storytelling style of things I typically enjoy, this isn't it. AND YET. This really worked for me. I'm glad I didn't know much going in, but because I knew the general direction of things (just read the blurb on the back of this edition), I had an expectation that the story would go a direction I wanted—which meant I felt safe continuing despite its strangeness.

If you're at all interested in this based on the blurb, or if you saw the trailer for the upcoming movie and were intrigued (me), or if you just feel like you need to read this because it's a classic in the genre—DO. It grabbed me pretty much immediately, but I'd say give it 100 pages to get used to the style and sink into the story before giving up on it. It's a long ride, but totally worth.

No half stars here. Full five. Definitely gonna read the sequel.

My Review:
This book was alright. I liked both of the main characters, and Simon in particular, but the transition between being friends (I loved their friendship) and lovers was kind of abrupt for me. The first half of the novel went along at a proper pace, and then it seemed to speed up for the sake of fitting in all of the plot, and that's where it lost me somewhat. It wasn't bad, but I didn't feel like it had the same draw as the earlier parts. And the scene where Daphne takes advantage of Simon when he's drunk and too slow to keep from possibly getting her pregnant left a bad taste in my mouth. Even though I think she was right in the things that she said to Simon about not letting his father rule his life, I didn't like how she essentially bullied and guilted him into her way of thinking. It wasn't enough to make me completely dislike the story, but it did taint it.

So 4 stars for the first half and 2 stars for the second half, averaging out to a 3-star rating for the book as a whole.

Author: Kristen Britain
My Review:
I enjoyed this book so much that I immediately picked up the next one and completely forgot to record finishing it here. Oops!

This is book three in the series, and it's been about a year and a half since I read the first two, so it was quite a learning curve to remember what had happened previously. There are still a few things that I don't remember very well. But honestly? It didn't matter. It was really easy to get sucked into the story and the characters again. I like that the characters continue to grow and change, both for personal reasons and because of the plot that surrounds them.

I especially liked Alton's side story. There was almost no connection between his story and Karigan's (except for a few mentions) and no actual interaction between them, so I was surprised that I stayed equally engaged in both stories. Usually one or the other starts to become my favorite, but I really wanted to get to both of them. I loved seeing how Alton was altered because of the events of book two, and then his slow climb back to himself. The magic of the wall was fascinating.

Of all the plotlines, I'd say Lady Estora's was my least favorite. Not to say it was bad, but the first half of it did drag a little. The most interesting bits were her interactions with Lord Amberhill, and then the second half of the story after the kidnapping. I especially liked her interaction with Karigan during the rescue. It was a really nice contrast to their estrangement at the beginning of the novel.

This book was a solid 4.5 stars, with that half star deducted for the one storyline that was a little slow. Overall, it was lovely.

Side note: This is the first one of the series that I read through the audiobook, and I loved it. Ellen Archer is a very talented narrator, particularly with making all of the characters sound distinctive.

Title: A Heart So Fierce and Broken (Cursebreakers, #2)
Author: Brigid Kemmerer
My Review:
Hrm, okay. This book has me torn. I didn't love the POV swap to new characters (even if I like Grey in general), but it grew on me, as did Lia Mara. I liked the way her story went, because it fulfilled some of my expectations and guesses and broke others. I'm not sure I really felt the chemistry between her and Grey, but I'm not sure how much of that was the POV swap and how much was the characters themselves. They were fine, but they didn't have the same level of tension that Rhen and Harper did. I also liked Grey's story and growth, even if there were moments that stretched my disbelief a little. I'm still not entirely convinced that him keeping his secret so fiercely wasn't just a plot conceit. Ultimately, it was a story that was easy to read and that did manage to suck me in a couple of times, but it wasn't so effortless as the first installment.

My real indecision comes from the ending. I'm honestly not sure how I feel about Lilith popping back up in the last chapter, Rhen being blackmailed with Harper's safety to work with her, and him insisting that it stay secret. It feels a bit too much like reverting to the first book. If the "secret" gets revealed early on, then I could go for it, but if it drags on for much of book three...I'm gonna be highly annoyed. A lot hinges on how book three carries on from here. It has the potential to wrap things up into a highly satisfying and entertaining trilogy, or to really beat a horse until it's dead and then keep going. We shall see.

4 stars. Good, but mostly riding on the coattails of the first and sending me uncertainly into the last.

Title: The Ten Thousand Doors of January
Author: Alix E. Harrow
My Review:
Wow. Just wow. This book was chock full of things I don't usually like in stories—particularly the protagonist spending a big chunk of the book as a child, a near-excessive amount of story-within story, and unreliable narration—and yet somehow it surpasses those things and is straight up one of the best things I've read this year. The POV is super engrossing, the prose gorgeous, the characters vivid, and the plot twisty in ways that completely make sense by the end. Also, it's a portal fantasy, and those are almost always a hit with me. (And the audiobook is great. Highly recommend it.)

No question - 5 stars.

My Review:
I'm not sure why, at this point, I'm still surprised when I fall into this world like diving into cool, blue water, but somehow, I was. It didn't even matter that it has been a couple of years since I read #6. Sure, there were some things I didn't quite remember about what had come before, but there were just enough quick reminders sprinkled in that I got the gist. I love Mercy, Adam, Stefan, all of them. I enjoyed the bit of character crossover with the Alpha & Omega series. I liked that so many good characters got to feature for at least a scene or two. It all feels like a reward for sticking around with the series for so many books.

I always find it difficult to explain what I love about certain 5-star reads, and this one is no different. I don't think the plot of this one was quite as intriguing as some others, but I enjoyed it well enough. It's really all about the characters, and the rest is only icing on the cake. (Also, the audiobook narrator, Lorelai King, is a queen in her field, and I really can't make myself read these on paper when I could listen to her instead. So good.)

Title: Cursor's Fury (Codex Alera, #3)
Author: Jim  Butcher
My Review:
This book took me a lot longer to finish than usual, but not because of the book itself. Life simply ate into my listening time. I think if it had been an option, I would have devoured it in one sitting.

When expressing my preferences for fantasy novels, I've always said that I best liked books that weren't about politics or war. This book? ALL politics and war. This is a great example of how it's entirely possible to keep those two topics from getting dry and boring, and instead use them to show engaging characters doing exciting things. I love the characters, primary and secondary, and I look forward to jumping back to every storyline (even while I hate leaving them to jump!). I love the progression from book to book, and how there's full resolution of the current plot while leaving tons open for the sequels (no cliffhangers, yay!).

I like how we know stuff that Tavi doesn't. I mean, Jim Butcher, you sneaky thing! Tavi = Octavian. I can't believe I never saw it. I like thinking forward to what's coming and wondering how things are gonna develop.

Kate Reading = fabulous narrator, as always.

Basically, I enjoyed everything about this book. Already bought the next one.

Title: The Rook (The Checquy Files, #1)
Author: Daniel O'Malley
My Review:
This book was such fun. I was afraid for the first couple of chapters that I might not like it, because there was a huge amount of backstory info dumping. Given that I recently struggled with [b:A Deadly Education|50548197|A Deadly Education (The Scholomance, #1)|Naomi Novik|https://i.gr-assets.com/images/S/compressed.photo.goodreads.com/books/1596909044l/50548197._SY75_.jpg|75543174] for its extreme amounts of info dumping, I wasn't in the mood for another one. However, I was pleasantly surprised. The method of feeding in backstory (letters) combined with the distinctive voice conveyed through it was really well done and didn't bother me at all once I got accustomed to how it was being used. The MC (whose name I won't even attempt to spell) had spunk and was extraordinarily competent despite her disadvantages. Given that the plot is built around the fact that she has complete amnesia, it would have been really easy for her to be a passive character—but she's anything but.

The world of this story is unique, exciting, funny, and engrossing. It wasn't a perfect story; the mystery of who was behind everything didn't seem particularly telegraphed to me. Probably it was there, hidden in all of the backstory, but I really felt like it could have been anyone and I could have bought it. Even so, it didn't lessen my enjoyment of it, and I'm looking forward to reading the sequel.

4 stars.

Title: The Burning Sky (The Elemental Trilogy, #1)
Author: Sherry Thomas
My Review:
In a lot of ways, this felt like Harry Potter fan fiction. Or maybe just a re-imagining of Harry Potter elements. That's not a negative in my book, because it wasn't like this was a direct retelling or anything. However, the characters use wands, they perform spells with latin-like incantations, they have instant travel (vaulting, i.e. apparition), and they're fighting against a seemingly all-powerful scary bad guy. Oh yeah, and they go to a boarding school. Beyond that, the comparison starts to break down, but it was enough that I raised an eyebrow at times.

I really enjoyed this book. Iolanthe and Titus were great characters. Titus had more of an arc than Iolanthe, but both had some decent growth. They both had convictions, stood up for them, and yet learned where they'd gone or been led astray. The prophecy/prediction element was very heavy, and I don't always love that in books, but it was well done here. The Inquisitor was suitably scary. The different settings were vivid.

My only complaint is that this trilogy isn't all published yet, so book 3 has another year-ish before it'll be released.

Note: I listened to the audiobook, and it was fabulous. I often don't like male narrators, because they can sound ridiculous doing female voices...but that was not the case here. I highly recommend the audio version.

My Review:
4.5 stars rounded up to 5.

I'm also reading (by audiobook) [b:Kushiel's Dart|153008|Kushiel's Dart (Phèdre's Trilogy, #1)|Jacqueline Carey|https://images.gr-assets.com/books/1328168291s/153008.jpg|2990010] right now, which oddly made this book hard to read. Go figure, because this is definitely the "easier" of the two books. Despite being written in a style that feels modern and does not linger on too many details, the world of this story felt vivid, if small in size--at least of the parts we get to explore in this first book. My only real complaint is that the introduction of magic through Yelena's broken memories/flashbacks was jarring and pretty confusing early on. (And, okay, some of the concepts, such as the Code of Behavior, felt a bit too much like the YA dystopian novels I've read.) And the prose may be light of pretension or poetry, but it's pretty damn deep and dark in places. I wasn't expecting that.

I loved Ari and Janco. I had my suspicions about Valek and his plotting and motivation, and I feel gratified that many of them were valid in the end. By the end, the plot ended up being pretty straightforward, but it was still exciting to see it all unravel. I liked watching Yelena and Valek slowly fall for one another. But, then again, I'm a sucker for a romance plot thread, so that's no surprise.

So despite some minor complaints, I greatly enjoyed this. In fact, it's 1:30 am when I have to be up at 6:00 am, and I just finished reading it after binge-ing it from 20% onward. Le sigh.

Title: Thornyhold
Author: Mary  Stewart
My Review:
This is a quiet, leisurely-paced sort of book that I probably wouldn't have continued if I'd had reason to put it down in the middle. As it is, I had blocked an afternoon for reading, and this is short enough that I was able to finish it before I lost my sunlight for it. I can't say that I'm widely read in gothics, and particularly the classics of the genre, but this one didn't quite hit the right notes for me. It wasn't bad; the build and the pay-off were done well enough, and I'm not at all annoyed for having read it. It simply wasn't gothic enough for me. The hints of those tones were there, but the menace never quite reached the right level of tension. I was never left to wonder much about character motivations and have those turned on their heads. This was largely a "what you see is what you get" story with a straightforward, almost coming-of-age sort of plot and a romance that was so barely-there (and barely developed) that it's shocking that anyone would apply that label to it.

Three stars for a generally pleasant read that I wouldn't say you shouldn't try, but that I wouldn't go out of my way to recommend, either.

My Review:
Second read 5/20/16:

I'm so sad that I'm done with this series again. The audio versions were fabulous, and it only added to how much I enjoy these characters. It had been just long enough since I read it that I had mostly forgotten the plot, but still remembered the feel of it. I will definitely be reading these again in a couple of years.

First read 6/22/13:

This was an enjoyable conclusion to the series. Like the rest, it was really part of one long story, rather than being its own book. It added some more interesting characters--enough that, at one point, there were a good twenty secondary characters floating around. Many of the old favorites were there, too, which was nice. Despite his shaky beginnings, I really came to like Whit, and naturally Berry remained a favorite, too. Even Barr grew on me, which I completely didn't expect.

I like how you can feel the progress in these books. The characters are constantly learning things, testing and changing their perception of the world, making mistakes and then correcting for them. Or not correcting for mistakes, and thus making for some nice antagonistic characters to balance things out.

I recommend this series, but I definitely recommend reading all four in close succession. I think they would have lost something if I spaced them out more.

Title: Search the Shadows
Author: Barbara Michaels
My Review:
Very, very dated in its treatment of gender roles and dynamics—lots of the sassy, "feminist" character being bossed around and coddled by men. I'm not surprised, since this was originally published more than 30 years ago, and because I've read quite a few others by this author. It's been a long time since I've read one that I could rate higher than this, though, and I'm starting to wonder whether that has more to do with my changing reading tastes than it does the books themselves. I think maybe I have less patience for how dated it is than I did when I was in my twenties.

That said, in the grand scheme of trying to get through the entire Barbara Michaels catalogue, this was a pretty good read. It had a bit of a slow start—not uninteresting, but a little meandering—but sometime after page 50 I really got into it. The bits of the mystery were fun to follow, and I was very interested in the resolution of it. I can't say that I guessed it, but I wasn't surprised at how it ended up. It's one of those books that leaves so many options open that you could hardly be surprised at any of them. Probably, I would have rated it 4 stars if not for a little bit tacked on at the end, re: romance. Spoilers below, if you care.

Why on earth was it necessary for her to suddenly have a thing for Dave, her mother's old friend, at the end??? I'm all for an age-gap romance when it's done well, but there really wasn't any build for this. She liked him, respected him, but to me there wasn't any hint that it was more than that. I wasn't rooting for anything in particular to happen with Carl, either, despite the fact that they'd kissed. I'm all for a good romance in just about any novel, but there didn't seem to be much of a foundation for it in this, and it was really just unnecessary. Soured the ending for me for splitting focus onto that entirely useless note. It makes me wonder if the age-gap thing was a trope Barbara Michaels particularly liked, though. I'm pretty sure I read another one that had a similar conceit.

I'm not mad I read this (unlike some others of hers). 3 stars that I could probably be persuaded to bump to 3.5 if I was feeling generous.

Title: The Mating (Law of the Lycans, #3)
Author: Nicky Charles
My Review:
Wow. That was the most generic paranormal romance I've ever read, in all the worst ways. It's like there was a playbook of the most irritating plot points from the genre and checked them off in a neat little row. I only finished it because a friend recommended it, so I wanted to give it a fair shot. I partially skimmed it for the first 30% and then started heavily skimming just to get through it as fast as I possibly could. I could see 90% of the plot points coming 6 miles away, because a) they were so stereotypical, and b) they were not at all subtly telegraphed. Everything fell back on the characters not talking to one another or not listening when they did talk, which is my Absolute. Least. Favorite. Thing.

1 star. I'm so glad to be done with this.
Private Notes: Jules recommended

Title: The Crown of Embers (Fire and Thorns, #2)
Author: Rae Carson
My Review:
Evil evil evil evil evil evil evil evil!!

After starting this book, I read somewhere that this was a cliffhanger. And it is, of a sort. The main plot of the book resolves, but then an additional element gets introduced at the end, and THAT is where the cliffhanger comes in.

More acceptable, but still EVIL.

I'm very happy to say, though, that I loved this book just as much as (possibly even more than) the first one. I didn't want it to end, and I'm still sad that I'm done with it. The third book will come far too slowly for my liking, that's for sure.

The world and the story are well done, but it's really all about the characters for me. Elisa may be a princess-turned-rebel-turned-queen, but she feels so REAL. Her worries and fears and joys and triumphs are in a different world and on a different scale--she has a whole country to worry about, after all--but she's so freakin' relatable that it's hard to believe it's just a novel. I've read modern day books about people much like me who weren't nearly so easy to grasp.

Elisa would be enough on her own, but the secondary characters really round things out perfectly. Hector, Mara, Tristan, and even Ximena (though I nearly cheered when Elisa chose Hector for the party instead of her, and at the end when Elisa sent her away) pretty much kick butt.

Why isn't book three out NOW?!?

Title: Walk on Earth a Stranger (The Gold Seer Trilogy #1)
Author: Rae Carson

Title: A Deadly Education (The Scholomance, #1)
Author: Naomi Novik
My Review:
This book almost put me into a reading slump. Considering that almost happened with an earlier October read ([b:The Haunting of Hill House|89717|The Haunting of Hill House|Shirley Jackson|https://i.gr-assets.com/images/S/compressed.photo.goodreads.com/books/1327871336l/89717._SY75_.jpg|3627]), it was extraordinarily unfortunate timing for it. However, I've read other Naomi Novik books I've enjoyed—notably the first few of the Temeraire series (which is phenomenal) and [b:Uprooted|22544764|Uprooted|Naomi Novik|https://i.gr-assets.com/images/S/compressed.photo.goodreads.com/books/1550135418l/22544764._SX50_.jpg|41876730]. Uprooted seems to be rather divisive, but I'm on the "I really liked it" side of things. It's only because of my success with her other works that I pushed through to the end of this book and did not DNF it. Even so, it took me 7 days in a month when I was already behind in what I wanted to read, mostly because some days I couldn't make myself pick it up.

Do I regret it? Well, mostly no.

Let's get the bad out of the way. I strongly disliked the writing style. It was highly conversational, with the POV character, El, dumping heap after heap of backstory and world building. The first who knows how long was almost nothing BUT backstory. It's worst in the first quarter, but it continues throughout the book, piling in dozens of characters and facts about the world as if there was a quota that had to be met. You know whole myth about Charles Dickens being paid by the word to write some of his doorstoppers? It's like Naomi Novik was paid per paragraph of exposition. The super sad part of this is that it means a 336 page book—a suuuuper reasonable, readable length—feels like it's twice as long. If you stripped that all away, you'd probably have a novella.

I did enjoy the world when I wasn't being beaten over the head with facts about it. It's rather nonsensical at times, with reasons piled upon reasons piled upon reasons to try to explain away what is really a pretty idiotic setup, wherein grownups willingly send their children off to be slaughtered at this ridiculous school, because you're telling me that no one has come up with a better solution over the last two hundred years???????????? But if you can accept the premise, then the rest becomes more enjoyable. The idea of them constantly being on their toes, never knowing when a mal might attack, did suck me in. (Did anyone else think of the video game, Prey? Anyone??)

El was fine as a main character. Her attitude was grating at times, and I could only somewhat accept her reasons for it, but mostly I didn't mind her. Orion was a bit of a dumb oaf, and I think lacked a little something in characterization, but I didn't mind him, either. There were a few side characters with some color to them, but most of the vibrancy was left for El, and that was fine. If we'd had to diverge to learn more about side characters, it might have added another hundred pages of exposition.

Writing style = 2 stars. Characters = 3 stars. Story/world = 4 stars. So I'm averaging out to 3 stars and calling it a day. Mostly, I'm just glad to be done.

And will I read any sequels? Hmmm. Jury's still out.

PS - I've read quite a few reviews that talk about racial and ethnic representation and diversity in this book. I don't want to linger on that too long, because I think others have said it far better; however, I want to make two quick points. 1) I agree that there were some things that were poor choices of description and wording that were insensitive at best and harmful at worst. I'm specifically referring to the dreadlocks reference and the Arabic text with the man about to mow down pedestrians in the illustration. 2) Outside of those couple of egregious examples, I found that the general depth of exploration (i.e., lack of) into the other cultures mentioned was a symptom of the world itself. El wasn't learning other languages to learn other cultures—it was all about getting a leg up to survive. It would be nice to see more in the sequel(s), though. As I mentioned before, the whole thing was so exposition heavy already that I can't see how she could cram anything else in the waterlogged beast of a manuscript.

Title: Furies of Calderon (Codex Alera, #1)
Author: Jim  Butcher
My Review:
I listened to the audiobook, which was narrated by Kate Reading, and it was fabulous--but then, Kate Reading always is. I loved this book so much that I finished it and immediately started the next one. It's been on my list for a while (like so many things), but I'd never gotten around to it. However, now I'm faced with a summer of workday commutes and half-marathon training wherein I need solid distraction, so this series seemed a good bet. Not that there should have been any doubt, as I previously read a couple of the Dresden books (back when there only were a couple) and loved them.

I think what did surprise me, though, was the quick pace of this story. There was almost non-stop action from start to finish, in a way that should have been exhausting. However, the characters were so brilliantly crafted that getting to know them absorbed some of the shock of zipping from catastrophe to catastrophe. There were a few moments when things felt a little too convenient, like when Fidelius and entourage just happened to show up at Bernard's just after Amara, but it was pretty easy to ignore those things for the sheer fun of it all.

On a side note: I was a little weirded out at first at the relationship between Amara and Bernard, but I think that was due to a distorted perception of their respective ages. I'm still not sure exactly how old they are, but the more I think about it, the more I suspect it's like mid-twenties and mid-thirties (or early forties). For some reason, I was imagining Amara as 17-ish, at first.

I can't wait to see how this series turns out. I have at least one guess.

Title: The Backup Girlfriend (Grove Valley High, #2)
Author: Emma Doherty
My Review:
I received a free copy of this book for an honest review. Trigger warnings for descriptions of physical abuse (not on page) and dieting/unhealthy relationships with food.

I don't read a ton of YA contemporary, but the premise grabbed my attention enough that I decided to give it a go. What I did not realize until more than halfway through the book was that this was book 2 in a series. Oops. Guess I wasn't paying enough attention. If I'd known, I probably would have skipped it, but it was readable enough even without getting the context of the first book. In the back of the book, it mentions they can be read as standalones, and I agree with that to a point. I found myself confused and a little annoyed, during the first half, with the fact that Abigail kept referring to her bitchy, mean girl attitude and how she'd treated people. I thought it was a case of telling instead of showing—but turns out it's just a case of getting that out of book 1. I only say this as a reader beware if you decide to skip around. It was ultimately fine, but it took me awhile to get past it.

If you want a YA contemporary centering around a former mean girl figuring out what she wants for the last few months of her high school and working to get that, this is a book for you. I thought I was coming into it for a romance, but that was ultimately a much smaller part of the plot than I expected. In the first half, it was almost nonexistent. I enjoyed the way it played out when it was there, but it wasn't the focus until the last quarter of the book. The rest focused on Abigail's screwed up life, both in ways it was inherently sucky and the ways she'd made it sucky through her behavior. I don't love lingering in those days of high school (I'm far aged past it), so without the romance being a bigger focus, it wasn't really a story that interested me much. However, I did ultimately appreciate how the story went. I liked how Abigail's true friends rallied around her and the things she learned about herself.

There were a few things that I didn't love. The pacing of the first half was slow and erratic, and the way Abigail acted during that section also seemed inconsistent at times. I think the intention was to show just how messed up she'd gotten (emotionally speaking), but I had trouble buying some of her actions. There were a lot of characters—probably at least partially a result of this being a series—and I think that was part of what bogged down the first half. I was constantly getting people confused and wondering why I was supposed to care about this specific student, and I think it could have benefited from some character streamlining. There were far too many for most of them to be memorable.

3.5 stars rounded up, because I think that someone who's a bigger fan of YA contemporary would enjoy this even more.

Title: The Haunting of Hill House
Author: Shirley Jackson
My Review: I can see what this book did and appreciate it, but I think I've consumed far too much horror media created in the years since this book was originally published to really love it. The whole book is slow, and the first half is boring in a way that is entirely necessary to the build of it, but that isn't all that enjoyable to read. It was very sneaky in how it <spoiler>showed Eleanor's descent into house-induced madness</spoiler> but the farther along it went, the more confusing it was to read. And again, it was necessary for the job it was doing, but I wasn't dying to see what happened. Mostly, I was a casual, curious observer who didn't actually care how things turned out. If this wasn't a classic work of literature, I probably would have DNFed it. I'm glad I read it, because now I know...but I think I will probably stick to the screen adaptations from now on.

Title: Star Wars: The Last Command (The Thrawn Trilogy, #3)
Author: Timothy Zahn
My Review:
Aaaaaaaaaaaaah! I can't even. I freakin' love this book. Reading it the second time ~15 years after the first time was such a strange experience, because while I didn't remember what was going to happen, specifically, I would get to certain parts and have these flashes of memory. Just enough to get really excited about what was about to happen next. I'd totally forgotten about Luke's clone, but as soon as he appeared, I realized that Mara would kill him. And at some point during one of the Thrawn scenes, I halfway remembered that Rukh was going to be the one to take him out. I also remembered that the ysalamiri were somehow counteracted, but I couldn't remember how. Rather than ruin it for me, it made me anticipate everything with more excitement--sort of like riding a roller coaster and knowing the big drop is coming.

I love how Zahn flips between characters with such ease that you barely feel like you've left your favorites before you're right back with them...mostly because they're all your favorites! Even characters who get less screen time like Wedge and Aves are vibrant, and I enjoyed their scenes just as much as the rest. Zahn has a talent for turning all those seemingly disparate plotlines into one big magic story.

Luke has always been my favorite, with Leia being a close second. There was something about the whole twin revelation that really got me in the original trilogy. This book trilogy only strengthened that love, and it also extended my Jedi-love to Mara Jade. I loved how Mara tolerated him and resisted liking him while still helping his sister's family and ultimately learning Jedi lessons from him. The way the two of them worked together in the final fight sent chills up my spine.

I could write a novel-length review praising what I loved about this, but I'll sum up by saying that, in short, I loved everything about this book.

PS - I'd be neglectful if I didn't mention that I listened to the audiobook, which is a fantastic production and was voiced with superior skill by Marc Thompson. I will listen to anything that man decides to narrate. (And, thankfully, he's done a lot of Star Wars stuff!)

Title: Star Wars: Specter of the Past (The Hand of Thrawn Duology, #1)
Author: Timothy Zahn
My Review:
I KNEW IT. Freakin' cliffhanger. This is one of those books that's obviously designed to not really be a complete story, and it really leads right into the next book, but for whatever reason the publisher decided it needed to be two books. I suspect, in this case, that it simply would have been too long. Ugh. But I hate cliffhangers.

That said, this book was still enjoyable. Not quite to the level of the Thrawn trilogy, but still fun. Marc Thompson is still the best narrator EVER. Probably at least one star of my enjoyment of this book came solely from his marvelous presentation. The rest came from two particular storylines. I love all of these characters, obviously, but I found myself getting a little bored with Leia and Hans parts. Probably because theirs are largely political and less adventurous. My favorites this time around are: Talon Karrde and Luke/Mara. This book leaves Mara in the precarious situation, and that's honestly the thing that makes me want to read the next one the most. I'm a little surprised at how much I'm enjoying General Pellaeon, too. He wasn't someone I cared about much before, one way or the other, but he's very interesting here.

I'm assuming that this book plays off continuity established by other books in the Star Wars universe in the ten years between the Thrawn Trilogy and this, because there are loads of references to things characters have done. I was able to mostly just ignore them and move on, but it did make me wish I could have read the interim stories. Granted, if I wanted to read all of the Star Wars Expanded Universe ("Legends" - whatever), I would read nothing else for the next five years.... Still. I need to find all the good Luke/Mara books. And is Talon Karrde in other books? Must find out. *mental note*

Taking a break before the next one, mostly because I know I won't have a lot of commute time to listen for a bit--and because the next one is 26 hours, compared to 14 for this one. Yikes.

Title: Heir to the Empire (Star Wars: The Thrawn Trilogy, #1)
Author: Timothy Zahn
My Review:
Okay, first of all, I have to gush about how awesome Marc Thompson is. Hands down my favorite male narrator now. I mean, he makes me believe I'm listening to the Star Wars characters, and that's a pretty tall order.

As for the book itself...this is one of those cases where I like a book so much that it's difficult for me to comment in detail. I loved The Force Awakens, but this book is still what I would have loved to see in an Episode VII, if it could have been made 20 years ago. Beloved characters are just how I envision them, new characters are just as vivid, and the story has the right blend of adventure, danger, romance, and magic.

I read this trilogy once before, 10+ years ago, and while I have no idea why I waited so long to come back to them, I'm glad I did. It was just long enough for me to forget all of the details and get to enjoy them all over again. I can't recommend these enough. (If I had my way, everyone I know would be reading them right now so we could gush together!)

Author: Helen  Hoang
My Review: This book was such a quick and easy read that I'm still sort of surprised that I'm done already. It's the sort of book where you can't read just a page or two, and find yourself two chapters farther than you'd planned and half an hour missing on the clock. Stella and Michael were unique, loveable, relatable, interesting characters, and I greatly enjoyed their journey of falling in love. This is a lovely contemporary romance for anyone who appreciates grown-ups with problems who still act like grown-ups and anyone who enjoys reading about people whose thought processes are completely different from their own (as I do). There was nothing about this that I didn't like. 5 stars all the way.

Title: Mistborn: The Final Empire (Mistborn, #1)
Author: Brandon Sanderson
My Review:
Oh man, what to say about this book? It took me so long to read it that I can hardly remember all the impressions I had of it. (Okay, to be fair, it only took "so long" because I took a break of about 5-6 weeks at around 80-something percent. Not because I wasn't enjoying it, but because life got in the way and I didn't have the luxury of the multiple hour reading sessions that got me through the first 80%. So yeah. Not a lot of days to read it, but a lot of hours.)

The writing was great, the world was great, and the characters were great. It's only getting 4 stars instead of 5 because there were a couple of things that I found annoying, but I would caution anyone who might use this review to judge whether he should read this book that the things I found annoying may just be personal things. We'll start with those:

1) The "love story" was annoying. I realize it wasn't the main point of this book, but the relationship between Vin and Elend did become rather crucial near the end. I just had to sort of sigh and accept their affection as fact to enjoy the ending, but I never really bought it.

Also, am I am the only one who, prior to the above pairing becoming evident, wanted Vin and Kelsior to hook up? I never really felt the whole father-daughter thing that I think we were supposed to see. Maybe it just wasn't ever clear to me how old he was, and he seemed young enough that such a relationship wouldn't be weird. Maybe I'm just weird. :-)

2) There were a lot of descriptions of fight scenes where you had to pay close attention to every word to understand what was happening. I'll be honest...I usually skim fight scenes. I look for the ways the characters are affected and the way the characters feel about what's happening, but otherwise I don't care what cool moves they use to jump over obstacles and cut off heads. Unfortunately, the magic system (which, granted, was super cool) had very detailed mechanics, and all those mechanics came into play whenever there was a fight. It almost felt like the tutorial level of every video game that has complex controls (Witcher 2, anyone?), where you're so busy trying to memorize what buttons to mash that it seems like you're never going to get to freakin' play.

But really, that's it. Otherwise, I loved it. I'll see if I can make a list of some of the things I loved, though I'm not going to try to remember everything. That's a lot of pages to critique, so I'll just hit some high points. This will be in no particular order.

1) The magic systems. I will admit that the explanations of them did feel a bit textbook sometimes. I know Vin was learning them, so Sanderson could get away with using that to explain the complexities to the reader. I accepted this and went with it. The sheer concept of swallowing metals and using different types for different powers was sooo super cool. I liked that not everyone had the powers, and that having all of the powers was even more rare. I could go on for paragraphs just trying to describe all of the cool uses and different people using both types of magic, but suffice it to say that it's a lot of fun.

2) I'll admit that at first I was only skimming the headers of each chapter, where the former Hero's journal described his journeys and trials. That was, until I realized from the text of the chapters just how important that journal was going to be. Paying attention paid off in the end.

3) Vin's journey was very well done. Street urchin to revolutionary is a pretty big journey, and I truly believed every step that she took on it, for better and for worse. With the exception of her relationship with Elend, anyway. That was a little too "love at first sight" for me, but I've already gone there. I did especially love how she kept going back to what her brother said to her about everyone betraying her in the end, and then the revelation that he didn't leave her like she thought, and instead died protecting her. So poignant.

4) The heist within the fantasy novel. This brought a full cast and a really exciting series of events that made for a story that was very different from anything I'd ever read before.

5) The Inquisitors were super scary. I know they were supposed to be, and it totally worked. I was immediately tense at the mere mention of them.

I'm sure there were other things, but that's what I've got off the top of my head. Excellent read, and I will definitely read the sequel when I have some huge blocks of time available to me.

Title: Legend (Legend, #1)
Author: Marie Lu
My Review:
Even though it took me 5 days to finish this book, I flew through it, because I wasn't reading more than an hour or two per day. This thing is a love song to narrative structure, with all of the right things hitting at the right beats, and even recognizing that and coming to expect it did not dim my enjoyment of this book. The two POV are distinctive from one another, and I loved their little quirks (particular June's list of observations in every situation). There were some things that I saw coming, but it was so darn entertaining that I didn't care. At the same time, it didn't shy away from dark subject matter (handled in a YA-appropriate fashion) and characters deaths, both of which I appreciated.

The only reason I can't give this another star is the romance. While I liked both of the characters and can see them getting involved eventually, it simply happened way too quickly. June was on the street only a few days before getting Day captured. It was fine, but not my favorite.

4 stars, definitely recommend.

Title: The Scarlet Pimpernel
Author: Emmuska Orczy
My Review:
The first time I read this book was for school required reading, in either 8th or 9th grade. Up to this point, I remembered it as one of the few things I was forced to read that I had actually enjoyed, but this is the first time I've read it in about 25 years. It's an odd book, stylistically, in the way that it jumps between various POVs (particularly in the first few chapters) to build up the backstory. I definitely connected with it far more once it stuck with Marguerite, and while I still didn't love the narrative voice, it was an entertaining story. I think the thing that surprised me the most is how simplistic the plot is, at the core. If you lay out all of the plot elements, it's really a pretty small list.

I'm giving it some leeway as a classic in my rating, since there's no way in Hades a book could be written like this today. I enjoyed it, and it was a fairly easy read, wordy prose and all. Probably more like a 3.5, but rounded up because why not?

Title: Fitzwilliam Darcy, Rock Star
Author: Heather Lynn Rigaud
My Review:
This book started off strong. I loved the concept, and I loved the prologue. I couldn't stop giggling at the idea of my favorite characters in these new roles, and I'm a sucker for a musician anyway, so this was a great combo.

Unfortunately, that giggly feeling didn't stick around. I got to 63% and finally just had to put it down. At that point, I had been skimming for easily 20%.

The POV hops between characters with dizzying frequency, and even if it hadn't, all of the perspectives other than Elizabeth and Darcy were sort of irritating. Jane and Bingley are cute for about 2 pages, and then it's sickening and sappy. I found Richard and his troubles interesting and sympathetic until it got super repetitive.

The dialogue tags are littered with adverbs in an almost distracting way. A couple of times they even made me laugh, because they were so silly. There's an overabundance of small talk -- it seems to take up half of the page in most scenes. We get every detail of the hellos and goodbyes, and tons of other details that are completely insignificant to plot or characterization. It might have been okay, except that the conversations have none of the source material's cleverness.

I kept reading as long as I did for two reasons. First, I really, really wanted to like this story. As I said above, it has a lot of elements that would indicate it's a perfect book for me. And second, I was curious to see how the various plot elements of Pride and Prejudice would be worked into the modern-day setting. When those failed to impress me . . . well, I had no choice but to put this thing down. It was starting to feel like work to finish it, and I have too many books on my TBR list for that.

RATING: 2 stars, just because I did get a few good giggles out of the beginning.
Private Notes: head hopping, lots of adverbs, don't like the non-Lizzy/Darcy POV, too much small talk without the cleverness of Austen's dialogue

Title: Summers at Castle Auburn
Author: Sharon Shinn
My Review:
As I'm writing this review, I've read 6 other Sharon Shinn books: the 4 primary books in the Twelves Houses series, which starts with [b:Mystic and Rider|97967|Mystic and Rider (Twelve Houses, #1)|Sharon Shinn|https://i.gr-assets.com/images/S/compressed.photo.goodreads.com/books/1388338711l/97967._SY75_.jpg|94425], and the first 2 books in the Elemental Blessings series, which starts with [b:Troubled Waters|7908762|Troubled Waters (Elemental Blessings, #1)|Sharon Shinn|https://i.gr-assets.com/images/S/compressed.photo.goodreads.com/books/1270247367l/7908762._SY75_.jpg|11180191]. If I manage to find her books used somewhere (which is fairly rare in places I shop), then I will grab any I don't own immediately—100% an auto-buy author. Though I'm slow going at it, I plan to read everything she's written.

Summers at Castle Auburn is a weird case, because I picked it up long before I even knew who Sharon Shinn was. I don't remember why I grabbed it, specifically, but I suspect it was based solely on a pretty cover with a story blurb that sounded like it had potential. Regardless, it has been sitting on my shelf for more than 15 years, to the point that I'd pretty much forgotten I had this random standalone Sharon Shinn book. I was excited to dive in when I chose it for my TBR this month, and I can't say I'm disappointed.

The main character of this story, Corie, is the bastard daughter of a major nobleman whose half-sister is betrothed to the crown prince. She got pulled into the court when she was a child (7? 9? I can't remember) after her father's death, and she spends every summer at the castle, making friends and forging a close relationship with her sister. The narrative starts when she's fourteen and getting ready to go on an exciting trip with her uncle. It follows her through age eighteen as she goes back and forth between village life and court life, and is very much a coming of age story.

There are a few places the story focuses, and those I want to mention below.

1) Aliora - The story opens with a trip to hunt these creatures, something that fascinates and excites Corie, because it's been her uncle's (who she admires) life work. What you quickly learn, though, is that these are not creatures at all, but sentient beings—essentially, they are this world's fairies. They have been enslaved by the humans, and that's a normal part of life for Corie. It's fairly subtle how they're woven into the first half of the story, camouflaged by court intrigues and forging friendships, but gradually Corie begins to awaken to how wrong their enslavement is. While I liked the way this storyline played out, I have some complicated feelings about it, and particularly what it has to say about the relationship between slave and captor. It made me think, and that's not a bad thing.

2) Court life and relationships - From page one, the story does what I believe Shinn does best—introducing you to a rather large cast of characters and then spending the book gradually making you care what happens to them. Within the first few pages, you learn that Corie adores her Uncle Jaxon and her sister Elisandra and despises her sister's mother, Greta. She's excited to be traveling with the beautiful young prince, Bryan, who is accompanied by his cousin, Kent, his food taster, Damien, and a new guardsman, Roderick. By page 10, you already have a good idea of who these characters are, their relationships with each other, and what Corie thinks of them. The story follows not only her shifting perspective as she gets older, but how her eyes are opened to the flaws and machinations of those around her. There's a very clear and gradual transition from the young, naive fourteen year old into a young woman who realizes that not everyone is as good as they appear—or as anything as they appear. It's interesting just how naive she is sometimes, and I believe it was intentional that you as a reader can figure some things out before Corie does.

There is somewhat of a romance subplot in this (as there has been in every Sharon Shinn book I've read so far), but I wouldn't say that it's a primary focus of the story. Corie has crushes and considers things like love and marriage, but mostly it's part of her growth as a young woman and a necessity due to her place at court (which is very much as a pawn in the political marriage game). The eventual resolution for her takes place very late in the story (seriously—about 10 pages from the end) and with very few pages dedicated to it. Since I'm someone who enjoys a good romance in my fantasy, I have to say I was a little disappointed in this aspect. There were some hints early on, some bait and switch, and ultimately a satisfying (if unromantic) conclusion. For someone who does not like romance in their fantasy, I think this was probably a good mix.

3) Growth as a witch/healer - In her non-court life, Corie is apprentice to her grandmother in what passes as a (very light) magic system in this world*. It's sort of a combination herbalist/hedge witch sort of thing, where some of it is medicinal and some of it has a hint of the magical. Corie has ambition and drive to learn this skill, and she's very capable in it. It becomes very important to the plot on multiple fronts, and very deftly so.

*The aliora also have magic, of a sort, but it's neither explained nor accessible to the humans.

Overall, I greatly enjoyed this standalone. Shinn's writing style is warm and inviting, and I have no trouble getting absorbed in her characters and her worlds, and this was no exception. Was it as good as the Twelve Houses series? No. Is that a high bar? Yes. There were some minor things I didn't love about this book, enough to take off a star, but I passed some very pleasant hours reading it, and I expect that I will pick it up again sometime when I need a quick read that doesn't draw me into yet another long series.

Title: A Fine Joke
Author: Amy D'Orazio
My Review:
Loved this story just as much as when I read the version that was published online ([b:A Fine Joke|30289530|A Fine Joke|Alex9903|https://s.gr-assets.com/assets/nophoto/book/50x75-a91bf249278a81aabab721ef782c4a74.png|50767645]). No new thoughts, so I'm just pasting over my review from that version, which still stands:

I expected this story to be depressing and frustrating, but it was instead great fun! The joke aspect doesn't stay secret from everyone for so long that it's annoying, and Darcy approaches the news of his fatal disease with such purpose that there's very little dwelling on the sad reality of it all. It's a novella-length story, but there's still a really nice build-up both of Darcy and Elizabeth's relationship and of the solution to the mystery. Yes, Elizabeth softens to Darcy pretty quickly, but this is one of the few scenarios where I found it completely believable. After all, she initially thinks he's dying, and he comes to her so humbly that it would have been harder for her to act otherwise with him. There are a lot of other characters, most of which have fairly passing involvement, but I really enjoyed Wickham's part in it all. Very different, but in a nice way.

Author: Jan Ashton
My Review:
This book had a good concept and potential, but I think it suffered from being a novella and not a full novel. There were several times that events felt like they did not have time for enough build up to feel as important as they should or for any real tension to build. With a story like this that has multiple focus points of both plot and characterization, it really needed to be at least double this length to prevent things from resolving too quickly. I didn't have time to really feel how Darcy and Elizabeth's feelings had progressed from staying together at Netherfield. They really didn't have a lot of interactions, and many things were conveyed by the current POV character reflecting rather than being present in the action.

Certainly not bad, but I was disappointed that it wasn't better.

Title: The Barbed Coil
Author: J.V. Jones
My Review:
This was the first fantasy novel I ever read, a decade ago now. I honestly didn't expect to like it as much as I did, mostly because the story so heavily revolves around war. It does contain one of my favorite types of fantasy plots--the person from our world sucked into a fantasy world--and that's what drew me to it. Strangely, the "our world" piece of the story is so small that it's insignificant. Tessa's connection to where she starts is minuscule very early on, to the point that I almost forget that story element is even there as I'm reading. None of the pieces should have added up to a book I liked, and yet I've read this book half a dozen times. I love it.

It's difficult to express exactly what draws me to the book, because I think there are a lot of small things that add up to the whole. However, my favorite thing is, without a doubt, the magic system. I've never read anything like it elsewhere. The way Tessa and the other (trained) scribes in the book can draw their patterns and find themselves somewhere else, shaping the way things happen, is absolutely brilliant. I became fascinated by every little detail of the pigments and vellum and all the things involved in the process. By extension, Emith is my second favorite character.

My first favorite is Ravis, naturally. I love that he's the coarse mercenary with a mysterious past who ultimately doesn't have some magnificent destiny to become ruler of the land or gain some special magical power. I love that that particular duty fell to Camron and Tessa, respectively, and that Ravis's growth is quieter.

I can see how this book wouldn't be for everyone, but it's one of my favorites.

Title: Midnight Sun (The Twilight Saga, #5)
Author: Stephenie Meyer
Publisher: Little, Brown Books for Young Readers
My Review:
Quick and rough thoughts:

1) I enjoyed it.
2) It was too long.
3) It was exactly what I needed right now.

More detail:

I read Twilight as an adult with an adult's POV, and I liked it. I rated it 4 stars. I recognize the problematic things that people discuss about it and choose not to revise my original assessment of how much I enjoyed it. I still enjoy it. If you don't, that's cool. We can still be friends.

So, given that, and given that I read the 2008 partial draft of Midnight Sun and really liked it (enough that it's my highest rated Stephenie Meyer "book" on goodreads! ha!), I'm honestly not surprised that I enjoyed this one. I've been going through a long reading slump, so having something that was basically a reread with bonus scenes sounded perfect to kick-start my reading brain—and I wasn't wrong. It had its flaws outside of the aforementioned story elements, which I'll get into below, but overall I flew through it as much as someone with a very busy schedule can.

We'll start with a few things I didn't like about it.

— The scenes where Edward and Bella were together, AKA the rehash from the original. It was SO TEDIOUS sometimes. Not enough that I wanted to put it down—I was enjoying the reread aspect of it, after all—but I did some skimming of those after I realized how they were going to be. The best way I can describe it is that it felt like Stephenie Meyer had a checklist of every word, expression, and aspect of body language for every character in those scenes (particularly Bella, obviously), and she was ticking them off as Edward observed each one. It was unnecessary, and cutting some of it would have solved the length issue.

— I didn't enjoy how one-dimensional Bella's "friends" were. There was some real hate for Jessica and Mike, and the complete opposite for Angela. I liked that Edward liked Angela, because obviously she comes across as the most genuine even when you can't see inside her head, but they all had only one note to their personalities. It would have been more interesting to see little hints that they were people and not just cardboard cutouts.

And now for things that I enjoyed.

— Weirdly, I was less disturbed by Edward being in her room at night. (Not that I was particularly disturbed to begin with. I've read far worse trespasses by love interests.) It made total sense that this was how he accustomed himself to her scent so that he wouldn't kill her, and also that he was trying to satisfy his need to be close to her without actually "having" her. Obviously it doesn't work, but I liked seeing that part of his intent.

— Pretty much all of the added scenes were great, but particularly the ones where we get to see more of his family. Alice was always a favorite in the original, and that didn't change here. I found that I hate Rosalie even more (and was surprised at just how nasty she was, honestly), but loved Emmett more, so it balanced out.

— In contrast to the way Bella's school friends are treated, I greatly appreciated Edward's internal monologue where Jacob was concerned. It would have felt incredibly petty if Jacob's thoughts made him even more the loser in this triangle, so having Edward find him nice and pleasant to listen to was a good surprise.

— But my absolute FAVORITE thing was getting to see the Cullens' special abilities in action. Seeing how Edward's mind reading and Alice's precognition interacted was fascinating, and the book was filled with it. Even Jasper (who is honestly often an afterthought for me in this story) proved super interesting, particularly when he used his abilities after the baseball game. My absolute favorite scene of the whole book was near the end when when they were racing to get to Bella (she was at the ballet studio about to be killed). Edward was driving a super fast car they'd stolen, reading Alice's mind as she looked 30 seconds into the future (so he could tell how to swerve in and out of cars) with Emmett and Jasper turned to the back corners so he could read their minds and have a nearly 360 degree view around the car, all while Jasper kept their emotions focused so that panic/fear wouldn't hinder them. As soon as I realized that all those pieces were in play, I actually had to put the book down for a second to marvel over the creative use of those abilities.

4 stars and no shame.

Title: Snow White and Rose Red: The Curse of the Huntsman
Author: Lilly Fang
Publisher: Good From the First Byte eBooks
My Review:
This was an entertaining, pleasant story. It definitely had the feel of a fairy tale, both in atmosphere and the way the plot flowed. I didn't really get Snow White and the Huntsman and how their seemingly intense relationship developed so quickly, but I get the impression that's the way it would have been if this was a fairy tale written in ye olden times. (It's been a long time since I've read any of the originals.) It was far too short to really drum up any serious suspense or drama—but honestly, the length is why I chose it, so I'm not complaining.

Solid 3 stars. It was good, but probably not something I would reread.

Title: The Space Between Worlds (The Space Between Worlds #1)
Author: Micaiah Johnson

Title: Green Rider (Green Rider, #1)
Author: Kristen Britain
My Review:
This book has been on my shelf for a long time, like many other books that have either been recommended to me or just sounded appealing. I tried a couple of times before to read it, but I never got past the first chapter. Not sure why, exactly, except that maybe I wasn't in a fantasy sort of mood. This week, though, I had a couple of flights and need of a non-ebook, and this one was the first that caught my eye. Now I'm super glad that it did.

Karigan is pretty much a perfect heroine for me. She's not perfect as a person. She ran away from school, she thinks about turning away from her promise to deliver her message to the king, she sometimes says things she shouldn't (or when she shouldn't) and she makes plenty of rash decisions. Her path is not smooth, largely because of those rash decisions, but she is persistent and keeps at it, even when things seem hopeless. She truly thinks about her actions, and her view of the world is not black and white. While there are people who are greatly skewed toward either "good" or "evil," no one feels like they've been segregated entirely. The good people have flaws, and even some of the evil ones have little glimpses of good.

The only thing that kept this from being a five-star book was a few characters whose relationships with Karigan I felt weren't developed as well as they should be: Alton D'Yer and King Zachary. These both come in about halfway through the book (or a little later) and play rather significant roles. They were both good characters. I liked them, and I felt like I got to know them a little. My main complaint is that both seem to be hinted romantic interests for Karigan, but there was no build-up to it in her thoughts. Sure, she hasn't gotten together with either of them yet, but I was a little thrown when Karigan was thinking that Alton might be more than a friend, and how she couldn't get the king out of her head. It wasn't horrible. No insta-love here. But I did notice, and it bugged me a little. I'm hopeful that subsequent books will make me forget the minor annoyance completely.

I didn't realize when I bought this book that it was the start of a series. Heck, I've had it long enough that it might not have been a series yet when I bought it. I'm really happy that it is, though, because I fully intend to grab the next book immediately.

Title: For Pemberley: A Pride & Prejudice Novella (Tender Affection)
Author: Christie Capps
My Review: Despite this story starting with a failed sexual assault, it is saccharine sweet—and apparently exactly what I needed to finally get me through a book after a pretty severe reading slump. I think the assault piece was handled well for the type of story that it is, but the romance bordered on melodrama for me. It was a very short time period for so much change and transition, and those types of stories are often hard for me to stomach. However, it was well written, and I could appreciate it for what it was. If you're in the mood for something quick and sappy with very little conflict between Elizabeth and Darcy, then I'd recommend this one.

Title: Compromised by Mr. Darcy: a Pride and Prejudice variation
Author: Valerie Lennox
My Review:
This was a difficult book for me to rate. The sentence-level writing style itself was pretty good (despite some minor editing needs) and the plot was interesting in content (if not purpose/execution), but the characterization left plenty to be desired. Georgiana was a brat, plain and simple, and Darcy wasn't much better. I got incredibly irritated at his unwillingness to talk to Elizabeth, and the back and forth between them was old after about three seconds. The mystery plot brought a lot of new players into the story, most of whom I found either unnecessary or underutilized. Darcy and Elizabeth both jumped to a lot of conclusions to solve the various mysteries and somehow were perfectly spot-on in their guesses. (Really, the whole mystery plot was unnecessary. It didn't have anything to do with the E/D relationship development, so what was the point? I didn't care about Georgiana's plight whatsoever, because she was so unlikable.)

I hate to say it, but this is probably my least favorite version of a compromise plot. At least some of the sillier ones at least had an interesting build of the E/D relationship. This one I just found irritating. 2 stars, and an entire star is really because the writing was decent enough that I couldn't drop it lower.

Title: Ink Magic (Ink Sorceress Chronicles, #1)
Author: Erika Gilbert
My Review:
I picked up this book for free. I'm pretty picky, even with free books, but the concept sounded right up my alley. And it is -- the concept of this book is great. The idea of a tattoo that is somehow the source of one's power, that allows/forces the person to transform to suit the need . . . I really, really like it.

Unfortunately, this book was a mess. It's pretty short, and I think that was part of the problem. It could have benefited a lot from slowing *way* down, elaborating on some of the unclear parts. I like a fast-paced story, but this one was dying for some more detailed explanation and better build-up to climactic moments.

There are too many characters with too little time to get to know them. The result was that I didn't really care about any of them, even when there was danger. Because of this, the love interest was pretty laughable. The Big Bad was equally laughable. A book can't just *tell* me that someone's good, or bad, or a jerk, or a best friend, and then expect me to go along with it. I've got a pretty good suspension of disbelief, but please please PLEASE *show* me something to make me care.

Also, I didn't understand how the main character could have this tattoo and have it not act up for such a long period in her teen years (I believe she points this out to her best friend near the beginning) . . . and yet there's all this supernatural stuff going on right under her nose?

Overall, the writing was decent enough for me to bump it from one star to two. The author has potential, and I'd like to see it properly fulfilled. This, however, doesn't do it.

Title: From Ashes to Heiresses: A Pride & Prejudice Variation Short Story
Author: Renata McMann
My Review: I liked the concept for this story, and I think the characters were enjoyably written. I especially liked the twist at the end <spoiler>where it was revealed that Mr. Phillips is the one who gave the girls money, and not Mr. Bennet. The Phillips' tend to get a bad rep compared to the Gardiners, so it was fun to see him as such a good guy.</spoiler> I would have preferred if this was a bit longer, though. Not for the length itself, but because parts of the story felt rushed, and I thought they could have been more satisfying with a little more build-up and time to execute. Also, it took 10-12% of the story for Elizabeth and Jane to discover what happened to their family, after some rehash of events of the original. It's not a lot of words in general, but given the overall length, I think it could have been put to better use in adding a scene or two that would make Wickham's actions seem less like they came out of the blue.

Title: Valkyrie Symptoms (Valkyrie, #0.5)
Author: Ingrid  Paulson
My Review: This was cute. I'm not big on short stories, because they leave me with this big feeling of, wow, there could have been so much more to that. But since this was set immediately before the book ([b:Valkyrie Rising|13034113|Valkyrie Rising|Ingrid  Paulson|http://d.gr-assets.com/books/1332247199s/13034113.jpg|18197631]), it was fine. It still makes me crave a sequel that I know is going to be a year-plus in the coming, though. *cries*

Title: Murder at Northanger Abbey: Sequel to Jane Austen's Spoof on the Gothic Novel
Author: Shannon Winslow

Title: More Than Admiration: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: Wynne Mabry
My Review: This story was enjoyable enough, though if it hadn't been as short as it was, I might have put it down without finishing it. The middle section (which is an exchange of various letters between Elizabeth, Georgiana, and Mrs. Gardiner) really slowed down the pace by being repetitive and simply too long. It could have been cut in half and served the story far better. That said, despite the flaws of that section, I really like the gradual build of mutual respect between Elizabeth and Darcy and how that was achieved without them even seeing one another. The last third (when Darcy and Bingley finally come to Hertfordshire) was also nice, and while some of the plot points proceeded and resolved in somewhat clunky ways, it was still a good resolution.

Author: C.S. Pacat
My Review:
This book is a little difficult to review and rate, because I read it in two chunks with a pretty significant amount of time between. I had it checked out from the library and just was not diligent enough to get it finished before my time was up...and it turns out that having people socially distancing at home means library ebooks have longer wait times.

Everyone said that this series was worth it for the slow build of the relationship between Damen and Laurent, and I definitely agree with that. The change in how they related to each other, Laurent slowly allowing himself to be honest and vulnerable—it was very well done. However.......all of the politics and troop movements really did drag after awhile. I think the farther I got into the series, the less patience I had for it. When I picked this book back up to finish it, I was pretty much over it. I did a lot of skimming of the last half of this book. I was more than ready to be done with it.

Not sure if I'll ever reread these. It was An Experience. But I'm not mad that I did, and overall I enjoyed them for what they were.

3.5 stars rounded up to 4.

Title: Brightly Burning
Author: Alexa Donne
My Review:
I'm a big fan of Jane Eyre and also of retellings and also of sci-fi, so this was right up my alley. There were some awkward turns of phrase and awkward, clunky ways of revealing the worldbuilding, but I was able to overlook it while I watched for the ways the familiar had been translated. For the most part, I enjoyed the futuristic versions of things and the way it was twisted to fit. There were a few places where it didn't make as much sense or seemed forced (mostly in attempting to make the social conventions fit), but it was still entertaining. I would have finished it all in one night if I hadn't made the mistake of starting it so late.

3.5 stars rounded up.

Title: The Stars We Steal
Author: Alexa Donne
My Review:
This book was fun. I'd forgotten that it was connected to [b:Brightly Burning|35721194|Brightly Burning|Alexa Donne|https://i.gr-assets.com/images/S/compressed.photo.goodreads.com/books/1508951823l/35721194._SY75_.jpg|56358868], which I read for the first time recently, so I enjoyed recognizing familiar ships and general world elements. Like any adaptation, I had fun trying to guess what would change from the original and and how newly-introduced elements would play out. My biggest complaint is that the added elements—namely, the ones that are specific to the world in which it's set—don't get as much development and screen time as they might in a story that was not tied to a specific story arc. Unlike the other book in this world, it remains mostly disconnected from the dystopian elements, which made those plot elements feel a little less impactful. I still liked it, though, and especially the relationship between the characters and how it changed through the story.

3.5 stars, rounded up, because it's Persuasion and how could I not?
