Title: Sense and Sensibility
Author: Jane Austen
My Review:
I first read this book and Pride and Prejudice in high school due to being assigned a paper comparing and contrasting two books by the same author. Jane Austen seemed the least boring author on the list, and such it began. I loved both of them, but I fell in LOVE with P&P...which, sadly, means that I never got around to reading this one again (and high school was a while ago...). UNTIL NOW! I've been steadily working my way through all of Jane Austen's finished works, and this is the last one I needed to read and review to have them all covered on GR.

I've seen the 1995 and 2008 movies so many times that I know the plot by heart. There were no real surprises there (Except that Colonel Brandon doesn't actually carry Marianne in from the rain--what?!??! Way to ruin all my romantic notions, Jane! LOL). The thing that did surprise me was the quiet wit of this story. I was listening to the audiobook (Juliet Stevenson = fabulous), so unfortunately I was not able to highlight favorite passages to quote here. However, there were several times were I just sort of stopped, smiled, and then shook my head at how clever a particular line was. It was unobtrusive and SO well done. Also, Lucy Steele--wow. What a jealous biatch. That was downplayed in the movies, too. I loved that Elinor was onto her the whole time and came out looking all the better in the end.

This is a quieter book than some of the others, but I feel very at home with Elinor. Though her story isn't my favorite, she's definitely the heroine most like me, which makes her easy to appreciate. The heroes of this book, too, are quieter. And I was struck by the difference in fortunes between this and Pride and Prejudice. Darcy's 10,000/year makes Edward's interest on 2,000 sound crazy low. Talk about putting things into perspective.

Loved it, not shockingly. 4.5 stars rounded up to 5.

Title: A Compromise At Rosings Park: A Pride And Prejudice Variation
Author: Isabelle Mayfair
My Review: I always find this sort of compromise stories strange, where Darcy loses his senses and kisses Elizabeth at Rosings, and she kisses him back. It just doesn't fit my mental images of them during that time period. This isn't the first time I've seen it, and it's not my favorite example of the trope, but not the worst either. It was a generically pleasant read, but too short to have a chance to add much of substance to this storyline. I read it less than 24 hours ago, and I've honestly already forgotten almost everything about it.

Title: Obsession
Author: Jann Rowland
My Review:
This was an odd sort of book, in that it wasn't really a variation at all. This was P&P if it had happened several years earlier and some circumstances were different. Darcy is more open and charismatic, Mrs. Bennet is far more of a manipulative harpy, Bingley and Darcy meet under different circumstances, Caroline Bingley is far less subtle, Lady Catherine is far nicer, and Fitzwilliam is only a Major (not sure why that seemed like one of the strangest parts but it did--probably because Colonel feels like it's practically his name...when it's not Richard, anyway). Darcy's courtship of Elizabeth is actually pretty smooth, and most of the conflict comes from exterior sources.

I'm honestly a little surprised that I enjoyed this as much as I did, but I do think some of it was the novelty of such a different story. I almost dropped a star because I didn't really understand Pearce and why he did the things he did. However, since what I really cared about was Elizabeth and Darcy, it was easy enough to ignore his head-scratching logic and just enjoy the rest.
Private Notes: Used to be in Kindle Unlimited, but is not any more. 3/16/17 - Back in KU, yay!

Title: To Be Mistress Of Pemberley: A Pride And Prejudice Variation
Author: Charlotte Elliot
My Review: This was a cute story. Nothing new and exciting, but well written and fun. Caroline was worse than normal, in her desperation, and I loved how Darcy and Elizabeth sort of banded together against her, even before Elizabeth's feelings had changed much. It irked me that Elizabeth so really believed Caroline where Bingley was concerned, but otherwise I have no complaints. Short, but enjoyable.

Title: The Highwayman (Victorian Rebels, #1)
Author: Kerrigan Byrne
My Review:
This was a KDD yesterday and caught my eye. I had no idea that I was in the mood for historical romance, but apparently I was! Within about 10 hours of first noticing it, I'd bought and finished this book. I'm giving it 4 stars for enjoyment, 3 stars for plot, and 3.5 stars for character...all averaging and rounding up to 4 stars.

I'm so used to reading regencies that this later setting surprised me initially, but I enjoyed it all the same. Having Faye working at Scotland Yard was really fun, and especially because she mixed with individuals (criminals, prostitutes) that would rarely been seen in a typical regency. I think I would have enjoyed the plot more if it had stayed in this setting and not spent the last 75% in luxurious homes outside of London. I'm not sure what's common for romances in this time period, since I haven't read much, but I think I would have enjoyed something grittier. (Recommendations welcome!)

Dorian was a tortured hero, and I did think his backstory appropriately explained and justified his actions (or, I can see how they would be justified in his mind, anyway). I liked that unlike the typical rakish hero, he wasn't making sexual advances on Faye. In fact, it was quite the opposite. He only agrees to sleep with her because she wants a child. That level of damage in a hero was appealing and, I think, well done. His inner monologues were sometimes extreme and convoluted, but I was mostly able to accept it. The guy's had a traumatic life, so that's some excuse for an edge of insanity.

However, it bugged me that a good part of the conflict later in the book is based off him being unwilling to accept Faye's love. She knows who he is, accepts him...and he has her move to a different house, because he's afraid he'll corrupt her with his touch, because he can't help himself around her. That was the point where it broke down for me. When Warrington jumps in as a bad guy and catalyst, it felt forced to me. He was such a tiny part of the story that he didn't even really feel like an antagonist. It's one of those sections that probably could have benefited from some of that grittiness I mentioned, because it could have made their estrangement more natural if there were other factors, and then Warrington wouldn't have been necessary.

Even with that, the story was extraordinarily readable and good fun at many parts. Faye was an intelligent, empowered heroine, and Dorian was a tortured, powerful, broken hero. Combine that with the forced marriage aspect of this (can't help it--I'm a sucker for that trope!), and I'm sold.

Edit: Heh. Apparently I had book 4 in this series on my "to read" list already. I must have seen a recommendation for it somewhere. Guess that means I should probably read the whole series, hrm?

Title: The Wager: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: L.K. Rigel

Title: Matchmaker's Guide: Meryton Matchmaker's Confidential Client Questionnaire
Author: Kristi Rose

Title: Poor Mr. Darcy: A Pride & Prejudice Variation Novella
Author: Renata McMann
My Review:
Re-read 1/6/17

Enjoyed this again the second (probably actually third) time around. The part where Wickham tries to kidnap his daughter and instead kidnaps Elizabeth and one of the Bingley twins really sticks with me, and I found myself looking forward to it during the re-read.

Original read 1/25/16

I realized as soon as I started this that I had read it before and apparently forgot to mark it as such. Since I couldn't remember whether I'd actually liked it or not, I decided to give it another go. I loved this variation (Georgiana married Wickham), because it's not one I've seen before. It changes a lot about where the story starts, and yet things took a pleasingly parallel course. Elizabeth and Darcy's courtship was pretty tame and without the snags of the original, but the exterior conflicts that did arise were interesting enough to make up for it. Good story, and I only wished that it was a full novel rather than a novella.

Title: A Conversation Behind the Tapestries: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: Beth     Wood
My Review: While there wasn't anything offensive or awful about this story, it was just plain silly and shallow. Lots of things happened, but character development was minimal, and I didn't really care about what happened, despite there being a few plot elements that I would have liked to see handled well.

Title: Further Down the Road
Author: Aimée Avery
My Review: I'm sorry to say that I did not care for this book. Mr. Wickham was far more evil than normal, which I did not mind, but the scenes that showcased that fact were gratuitously rough. I'm not one that generally minds violence and such, but it wasn't done with enough talent for me to feel it added to his characterization. Much of the story was summarized, also to the detriment of characterization. Characters seemed to be taken to the extreme. Caroline wasn't evil on the same way as Wickham, but her wickedness was equally flat. Louisa and Lydia had a connection, but it felt forced. Georgiana came out of her shell only to be brash and inappropriate. And the revelations about Anne seemed tacked on and unnecessary. I gave it two stars only because I liked the concept of Wickham's obsession with Elizabeth...just not the execution.

Title: Her Final Wish: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: Renata McMann
My Review: Hmm. This book is a bit difficult for me to rate. There were some ridiculous and unbelievable parts, particularly with Lady Catherine, Anne, and Mr Bennet. However, I really enjoyed the backdrop of the illness and quarantine, which was quite unlike anything I've read in this sub-genre before. I liked seeing how the sisters dealt with things in their differing ways and changed because of it all. I liked watching how it changed Elizabeth's perception of Darcy, and seeing her falling in love in a new way. So despite the faults, I greatly enjoyed the story, and it averages out to 4 stars for me.

Title: The Mystic Blessing: An Austen-Inspired Regency Romance (The Gypsy Blessing Book 1)
Author: Wendi Sotis
My Review: This was such a creative concept, and it was executed extremely well. I can't think of a single thing I didn't like about it. I didn't go into it expecting quite such an involved story, so that was a nice surprise. It starts before Georgiana goes to Ramsgate and goes all the way into Bingley coming to Hertfordshire and beyond. I loved the friendship between Georgiana, Elizabeth, and Jane. It was a nice touch that Jane was included, even though she had a smaller part and lesser friendship, and I liked how she was "in the know" about the gypsy blessing from the beginning. Elizabeth and Darcy's friendship was also really nice, and a nice change from the insta-love that I've seen in other stories. There were some fairly intense scenes as Elizabeth uses the drawings she receives to try to prevent events from happening, and both the build up of suspense and the outcomes were well done. Highly recommended!

Title: Servant of the Crown (The Crown of Tremontane, #1)
Author: Melissa McShane

Title: The Best Of Relations
Author: Catherine Bilson
My Review: This story should be called Mrs. Gardiner Fixes Everything. It's a piece of fluff, full of far too many convenient conversations and pieces of information, and very little conflict. Still, it was a fun piece of fluff that did not take long to read, so I don't have any real complaints.

Title: Hope For Mr. Darcy (Hope Trilogy, #1)
Author: Jeanna Ellsworth
My Review:
This book was well-written, but for the most part it simply wasn't to my taste. Elizabeth's opinion of Darcy changes very rapidly. Darcy learns his "lessons" from Elizabeth's refusal very quickly. The religious elements had no subtlety and felt forced into the story. The revelations revealed about Mr. Collins seemed either overdone or simply poorly placed sequel bait. Ditto the attraction between Charlotte and Colonel Fitzwilliam. Once Elizabeth and Darcy come together, they are overly sappy, and there are no real obstacles for them to overcome. Even the recovery of Lydia in London has the gritty edges removed.

It's not a bad story, and for some it might hit all of the right notes, but it simply didn't for me.

Title: Darcy's Voyage
Author: Kara Louise
My Review:
This was a fun story. I think it was too long, and there were whole swaths in the middle that I ended up skimming, but overall I really enjoyed it. The first part of the story, on the ship, was definitely the better part for me. Once the story started to slip into the "normal" plot, it became less interesting. I wish there could have been a bit more variation once Bingley and Darcy came to Hertfordshire, since the tale as a whole started so differently. It was disappointing that it relied largely on the same old plot changes.

Despite those complaints, I did really enjoy the story. The secrecy, the wondering, the furtive walks at Netherfield...very nice.

Title: Pregnesia (The Recovery Men, #3)
Author: Carla Cassidy
My Review:
I first heard about this book from this review at Smart Bitches, Trashy Books (fabulous site). It's not the sort of book I would have chosen on my own, but the review was hilarious, the book was on sale for 99 cents, and I wanted a quick, mindless read.

And wow. This reminded me of the reasons that I tend not to read contemporary romances, and also prefer something longer than the monthly series romances. That's not to say it was all bad--it was certainly as amusing in places as the review promised--but even the parts that were decent smacked heavily of "not for me." At least Lucas wasn't annoyingly alpha male, but I didn't really feel the chemistry. I always have these problems with books where there's a short acquaintance involved.

So meh, but I passed a couple of entertaining hours with it.

Title: Mass Effect, Volume 1: Redemption
Author: Mac Walters
Additional Authors: John Jackson Miller, Omar Francia, Michael Atiyeh, Daryl Mandryk

Title: Upstairs at the White House: My Life with the First Ladies
Author: J.B. West

Title: The Abominable Mr. Darcy (Marrying Mr. Darcy, Regency)
Author: J. Dawn King
My Review:
There were things I liked about this story and things that annoyed me.

On the good side:

-Charlotte and Colonel Fitzwilliam's friendship was a lot of fun, and I loved the way that storyline played out. I'm such a sucker for the Colonel when he's done well.
-I love it when stories bring Georgiana to Hertfordshire, and this was no exception.
-The plotline where Caroline serves spoiled oysters and that is the thing that gets Jane sick (and kills Mr. Hurst!) was a really interesting choice that I hadn't seen anywhere, and I enjoyed that.

On the not-so-good side:

-I realize it's the entire schtick of the story, but I hated all the Mr. Whatever Whatever names that Elizabeth had in her head for Darcy. The repetition of it at the assembly just grated on my nerves.
-The characters logic-ed their way through some problems with far too much ease and awareness of exactly what needed to be reviewed/said, such as when Elizabeth talked to Georgiana about Wickham. Elizabeth is smart, but for the amount of time she'd known the situation and her length of acquaintance with Georgiana, it was just too insightful to be believable.
-I didn't feel like Elizabeth and Darcy's cycles of getting along and then fighting again felt all that natural.

3.5 stars rounded up to 4.

Title: A Will of Iron: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: Linda Beutler
My Review:
Re-read 1/10/17

This book is completely insane, and I stand by the things mentioned below that I find silly...but I do love it all the same. It's the ultimate just deserts for all appearing characters. Unrealistic, but really satisfying.

Original read 7/18/15

I grabbed this book on a whim, because it was a suggested read after I finished another P&P variation, and I'm really glad I did. I loved Anne's diary and how the book alternated between that and the story. In some books, that might have been tedious, but Anne's voice was entertaining, and you could always see how it fit the section you were reading. I loved that some characters got recompense for being awful, even if killing Mr. Collins in such a graphic and humiliating way was a bit disproportionate to the crime. I thought Lizzy having such a strong reaction to Darcy's painting was a little silly, and I wasn't overly fond of Charlotte and Colonel Fitzwilliam appearing to be so much in love after so short a period of time...but those are really minor gripes. The flow of the story, the way the characters were portrayed, and the little bits of mystery around Anne's journal were all really good. I'd have read it all in one sitting if I'd been able.

Title: Magic Study (Soulfinders, #2)
Author: Maria V. Snyder
My Review: Oh, that was fun. These books are easy reads with surprising depth. The characters jump off the page, and you never know someone's true motivations until the very end--and they're often surprising. It was nice to see favorites from book one here (including Valek, obviously), and the new additions were welcome, too. I'm curious to see how Leif factors into book three, how Yelena's magic progresses, how she and Valek end up, how the two countries end up...basically everything. I always have a hard time expressing specifics about books I love, but suffice to say that I loved this one and heartily recommend it.

Title: What A Gentleman Wants (Reece Family Trilogy, #1)
Author: Caroline Linden
My Review:
Whenever I find a trope I really enjoy, I tend to go to all the lists of recommended books with similar plot elements and load up my TBR list. In this case, it was arranged/forced marriages, and specifically this one is because the hero's twin brother puts his name on the marriage registry and sends out announcements. The concept sounded fun, and it was available through overdrive, so I went for it.

The story itself was entertaining. There's the plotline wherein the heroine has to pretend to be a happy duchess, of course, and her developing relationship with the hero and her relationship. Then there's a subplot where the hero is trying to determine if and how his twin is involved in a counterfeiting scheme. Things came together for a logical conclusion with the truth coming out about the marriage and the bad guys doing life-threatening things. And it was all fine. But it was also lacking something.

I'm finding it really difficult to articulate why I can't rate this book higher, despite no specific complaints about the story. I think it's a mix of some minor things that added up. The secondary characters were cute, but ultimately flat. Some of the motivations were a bit silly (like Marcus's reasons for not telling Rosalind and Celia the truth about his marriage). The romance felt a little forced in places. Again, these are more general impressions than things I can really pinpoint within the text, but it's the best I can do. :-)

Solid 3 stars and worth reading if you want something light.

Title: When Beauty Tamed the Beast (Fairy Tales, #2)
Author: Eloisa James

Title: Courting Elizabeth: A Pride & Prejudice Variation
Author: Renata McMann
My Review:
I did not expect to like this book so much. I was assuming, based on the description, that it was going to be full of overdrawn angst while everyone except Elizabeth knows the details of Darcy's letter. Not so! Even though it's intercepted first, Elizabeth still gets to read it in chapter one. Phew! Rather than being an angsty book, this was a fun conspiracy and plotting book, and I loved it so much that I would have stayed up waaaaay too late reading last night if my allergy meds hadn't knocked me out first.

There was definitely a healthy dose of crazy in this book, I'll fully admit. Lady Catherine is at her finest and most insane here, plotting against Elizabeth with the help of Mr. Collins. Nothing I haven't seen before, but she was especially creative in her schemes. The craziest thing, though, is probably the fact that there are four--yes, four--couples by the end of the book. Some I've read before, but not quite in this manner. The secondary characters really shone, both for how entertaining they were, and also the slight variations in their attitudes and personalities that were not contradictory to the source material, but rather enhanced and made them real.

I wouldn't have thought it, but having Colonel Fitzwilliam as Lady's Catherine's closest and most caring nephew, even as devious as she gets, worked so well. (And I'm always fond of him with Jane.) His older brother was a lovely surprise, too. I expected him to be the typical version of the rakish Viscount (though he was already the Earl here...not sure why, because it never mentions their father), but he was a delightful surprise. Even Anne was fun, somehow being a friend to Elizabeth while maintaining that edge of snobbishness and condescension that proves she really is Lady Catherine's daughter.

So much fun. Definitely going on my reread list.

Title: Thursday's Child
Author: Pat Santarsiero
My Review:
This book has been on my TBR list for awhile, but I've been avoiding it due to the subject matter. Which, in short, is that Elizabeth , for money, agrees to bear Pemberley's heir in secret, since Darcy's wife, Anne, is too ill to do it herself. I decided today that it was time to bite the bullet and at least attempt it, because what was the point of having it on my list if I wasn't ever going to read it?

I was surprised to find that I really liked it. This is one of those stories that is a solid 5-star book for me, but that I'm not sure I'll ever be able to reread. Reminds me of [b:A Peculiar Connection|25131977|A Peculiar Connection|Jan Hahn|https://images.gr-assets.com/books/1436992200s/25131977.jpg|44829135] in that way. This one was not quite as emotionally intense, but there was definitely an element of it, in Elizabeth's despair over giving up her child, and her subsequent re-connection with her. I think that's the thing that sold me on this story, despite what it was about: the emotions felt real. Darcy, Elizabeth, Anne, Jane, Bingley...even Caroline. They felt like real people, dealing with real situations. It took some hardship, but everything boiled down to a lovely, happy conclusion, and I'm very glad I read it. For anyone avoiding it the way I did, I'd say this: Take a deep breath and go for it. You're missing some lovely interactions otherwise.

Title: Condescension and Condemnation (The Sweet Regency Romance Series, #7)
Author: Perpetua Langley
My Review:
This story was really well written, and the Merriweathers completely stole the show. They and their misguided beliefs about the British were hilarious and endearing. I also enjoyed the proactive and decisive turn that Bingley took even from the beginning of the story.

However, I did not believe Elizabeth and Darcy's love story. Darcy conspiring to provide dowries to the Bennet daughters just to give Elizabeth a "choice" was high-handed at best and completely silly at worst. Then he barely waited two seconds to propose a second time, which meant that Elizabeth hadn't had any time to get used to her freedom of choice. Brilliant plan, Darcy. No wonder she refused again, because you deserved it. I didn't believe Elizabeth's subsequent doubts of her refusal, because she'd had NO INTERACTION with him (and not even really any new information, before finding out about the dowries) on which to base changing her opinions.

2.5 stars for the Elizabeth/Darcy part of the story; 4.5 stars for the Merriweather and Bingley parts = 3.5 stars overall average, rounded up to 4. AKA the most complicated scoring I've ever used on a story, heh.

Title: A Kiss for Lady Mary (The Marriage Game #6)
Author: Ella Quinn

Title: Open Your Eyes
Author: Jann Rowland
My Review:
I enjoyed this book a great deal, but the point of variation took it past the point of believability a lot of times, so I had to drop a star for that. Elizabeth was just a little too good at reasoning out the motivations behind everything people said or figuring out just the right thing to say to her sisters that would get the best result. Basically, it was the JAFF version of how I play video games: by looking up the dialogue choices online first to see where they lead, so I can get the story I want! It's cheating, but still also enjoyable.

I feel like I've read this before. I don't know if there was an unpublished version online from the days before I tracked that, or if I got this from kindle unlimited awhile back and just forgot to review it. Or maybe didn't finish it. Or maybe it was really similar to another story. I dunno. It was the Collins proposal scene that cemented it, because I remembered well before there was any indication how it would go.

Title: An Encounter at Pemberley: A Pride And Prejudice Variation
Author: Isabelle Mayfair
My Review:
This story was okay. I didn't really buy Elizabeth's injury, given that she was up and walking with a crutch within a day and didn't seem to have any lingering effects from her head injury other than the memory loss. The way she took everything in stride seemed too good to be true, too. Yes, Elizabeth tries to keep a happy attitude, but she's not without normal human reactions and emotions (as evidenced by her strong reactions to all things Darcy). It was weird to not have her express distress, and also to have her thinking about determining whether she's in love (to the point that she asks Aunt Gardiner about it) after a couple of days in Darcy's company and still no memory.

2.5 stars, though rounded up to 3 stars for some generally pleasing scenes between Elizabeth and Darcy.

Title: Love Letters from Mr. Darcy: A Pride and Prejudice Novella (Marrying Mr. Darcy, Regency)
Author: J. Dawn King
My Review:
Hmm...apparently the goodreads page-loading issues today caused my review not to save. Bummer. Let's see if I can remember well enough what I said, given that I've finished another book since then. #notashamed

Short version:

Lady Catherine and Anne were ridiculous, annoying, and pushed the limits of believability. Elizabeth came around too fast. BUT Darcy's letters were lovely, Colonel Fitzwilliam was awesome, and I couldn't help enjoying it.

Title: Captain Wentworth Home from the Sea
Author: Mary Lydon Simonsen
My Review:
I thought I'd let my first JAFF of 2017 be Persuasion-based for a change. Which was a little weird, after so much P&P, but not in a bad way. This was a sweet story, though I think it was a story concept that would have been far better served by a full-length novel. Things moved a little too fast at times. I did like how different Wentworth was due to his injury, and his tendency toward frankness allowed for the story to move in a certain direction while seeming completely natural. Anne was more assertive than her canon self, which at times I thought was a bit of a stretch, but I couldn't help but enjoy her interactions with Wentworth.

3.5 stars rounded up.

Title: Desperate Hearts: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: Anna Kate Suton
My Review: I wanted to like this story, even considering that the twist was spoiled for me by a review on this site...but I found myself bored with the prose barely 10% into it. I speed-read just to see what happened. The plot was pretty ridiculous, but I'll admit the concept was also sort of fun, too. Unfortunately, it was far too short to really have a build-up of tension leading to the reveal of what was really happening, so it was pretty anti-climactic, and it ended before I could even enjoy what little bit of satisfaction I felt at the resolution. I've read worse, but I can't recommend this one.

Title: Nothing but the Deepest Love: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: Madeline Kennet
My Review:
This story was pretty good, but I did find it slow in places. A lot of the focus for the first half is on the friendship between Elizabeth and Georgiana. I always like seeing those two getting closer, but their interactions started to feel a bit tedious and drawn out as the conflict did not really escalate or even change much during the wait for Darcy to join the party and stop the elopement as per the original. Once he's on screen, the focus shifts away from Georgiana almost entirely. It was fine, because I liked his interactions with Elizabeth, but it made the second half feel almost like a completely separate story--which, considering the overall length, means that neither half really felt fully satisfying.

Elizabeth and Darcy's courtship was quick, but there were some quality conversations and interactions in there, so I could forgive its speed. Darcy had practically none of the faults from canon, for no other good reason than his gratitude toward Elizabeth's friendship with Georgiana. I like seeing that side of him, but I think it would have been better if it progressed to that after some effort on his part rather than immediately upon their introduction. There was very little conflict during the second half, as it was just a chronicle of the two falling in love. Nice enough on its own, but it didn't make for a particularly engaging story.

Title: Kushiel's Dart (Phèdre's Trilogy, #1)
Author: Jacqueline Carey
My Review:
I'll be honest...when I started this book, I really wasn't sure I would like it. I have a healthy fear of books this large, both because I'm afraid of them having a lot of fluff/filler and because it's not unheard of for me to get distracted (or in the mood for something different) before I'm finished and stop midway through. The beginning felt slow, with a long path through Phèdre's childhood before it got to the parts I would have considered the "meat" of the story. I considered stopping early on, beceause life's too short to spend a month reading a book that's like a slog through quicksand. However, I'd used an Audible credit to get the book, so I made myself commit for the long haul.

And oh my god I'm glad I did, because this book was FREAKIN' AMAZING.

AMAZING.

My first impressions were straight up flawed. Despite the length of this book (31 hours of audiobook @ 1.8x speed, so about 17 hours of actual listening time for me), there is no fluff, no slog. Even the smallest of actions or details become essential by the end, weaving together seamlessly. The writing style is unlike I've ever encountered, and it is gorgeous. I'm sure some of that was courtesy of the wonderful narrator (Anne Flosnik), but the sentences somehow managed to be rather archaic in construction while sounding completely natural to the characters. The descriptions were so vivid that time and distance passed as if I was truly alongside the characters on their journey, and I knew them all like old friends (or enemies). Yes, there are a lot of characters, and yes, I had a lot of trouble keeping them straight at the beginning...but I soon learned that the book was going to take care of me, and I didn't need to keep up with them all. There were plenty of subtle reminders of who all the players were when they popped back on screen, but without feeling like I was being reminded.

Phèdre's gift/curse is all about the co-mingling of pleasure and pain, and I think the story echoed that amazingly well. She experienced everything from being pampered, educated, and cared for to being kidnapped, exiled, tortured, and heartbroken, often at the same time. Her gifts lie in learning and observing, but somehow she managed to be one of the most active and ingenius characters I've ever read, even when surrounded by strong and powerful people who seem to have far more skill at making war. I was constantly on the edge of my seat, waiting for the hammer to drop. And it did. Again and again. There was death and sorrow, and even all the good things that eventually came Phèdre's way couldn't keep the ending from feeling rather bittersweet...but she's so determined and empowered that I can't help but feel hopeful, too. Even knowing that books 2 and 3 will have to be at the top of their terrible game to live up to this--like Phèdre's assignations, I expect they will be both painful and pleasurable.

This is not what I would consider my "usual" sort of book. It was emotionally sapping at times, and I will definitely need a break before I delve into the next books. But this is most definitely getting added to my list of favorite books of all time, and I expect I'll reread it in a few years. Until then, it will stick with me in ways I didn't expect but am happy to take.

(On a slightly related side note, I just noticed that the second trilogy in this series is narrated by Simon Vance. SOLD! I've been burned by narrators changing mid-series (see [b:Dark Moon Defender|97960|Dark Moon Defender (Twelve Houses, #3)|Sharon Shinn|https://images.gr-assets.com/books/1442211099s/97960.jpg|2727]), so I'm happy that won't be the case here. It just might take me a few years to listen my way there.)

Title: MR DARCY'S CHRISTMAS: "Great characters, great comic moments, great romance"
Author: Elizabeth Aston
My Review:
I don't read a lot of sequels, for the (I'm assuming) obvious reason that I like the build-up of Elizabeth and Darcy's relationship, so the aftermath and relationships of those around them don't interest me as much. I will occasionally read one focusing on Georgiana (though I confess that Colonel Fitzwilliam is my preferred hero of those), so I decided to give this one a try.

This story was well-written and painted a pretty picture of Pemberley at Christmas. Elizabeth and Darcy are in love and happy, so they only have peripheral involvement with the conflict of the story, which centers around Georgiana, her not-yet-announced engagement, and some circulating rumors. It was a fun mix of characters and had some good moments (mostly those featuring Colonel Hawkins), but it was dragged down by Mr. Moresby and Georgiana's seeming confidence in her choices. I get that she was choosing safety over love and the potential of being vulnerable in it, but seeing it all from inside her head made it harder to believe when things changed. Her emotional conflict was pretty short-lived and she was very decisive when her mind changed. I didn't trust her confidence in her new decision, because it didn't seem any different from her first decision. It's hard to explain, but it felt a bit inconsistent to me.

Still, it was enjoyable enough. 3.5 stars.

Title: A Merry Darcy Christmas: A Pride and Prejudice Variation (Jane Austen's Darcy and Elizabeth Holiday Series Book 1)
Author: Emma Dow
My Review: Oh, this story was great! My only disappointment was that Colonel Fitzwilliam wasn't present--not that it detracted from the story in any way, but I'm a softy for him. That "fault" notwithstanding, I loved this little story. Lady Catherine's scheming here actually made sense, and I loved how everyone danced within the confines she created and ultimately managed to best her. I would always like to see more scenes between Elizabeth and Darcy, but for the size of this story, I thought they had just enough time together to believe that Elizabeth's feelings had changed. Very well done.

Title: Mistaking Her Character: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: Maria  Grace
My Review:
This book was a lot darker than I expected. The treatment that Elizabeth faces from nearly every side is despicable, and it makes for a rather different character than the original. However, she retained some of the essential qualities that make her who she is, so I still liked her. Her introduction to Darcy is very different, and so there are none of the misunderstandings from the original. All of the conflict comes from outside sources and somewhat from Elizabeth's reaction to them.

The romance was lovely, but the emotional abuse that Elizabeth suffered made this hard to read much of the time. Dr./Mr. Bennet, Mrs. Bennet, and Lady Catherine are all far worse than normal, and even some of the allied characters (such as Colonel Fitzwilliam and Jane) have much more of an edge to them. It was largely counter-balanced by Darcy's deep affection, but that didn't make it easier to swallow.

The big change for the story was, obviously, that Mr. Bennet is a doctor caring for Anne. However, I think some of the most interesting things were the subtler changes: Elizabeth and Jane are children of their father's first marriage, though it's not immediately evident. The Gardiners are related to their deceased mother, so not directly tied to the current Bennets. Wickham starts out the story in company with Darcy, Fitzwilliam, and Bingley, so their fallout comes later. Georgiana is a little older, having just turned 18 to Elizabeth's 21. They all changed the story in smaller ways, but that I found intriguing.

3 stars for the discomfort of many encounters, but 5 stars for the romance, averaging out to 4 stars.

Title: The Colonel's Timely Bride
Author: Dana R. Lynn
My Review:
This story was adorable. Novellas can be hit or miss for me, because they so often progress too quickly for the plot to be engrossing, or the characters simply don't have time to develop. Not so here. I think this struck the perfect balance of including the good parts and leaving out those things that weren't absolutely necessary.

And, okay, it doesn't hurt that this story touches two of my guilty pleasures: "fish out of water" stories, and anything featuring large doses of Colonel Fitzwilliam. I was a little skeptical about introducing someone original as the main character, but I was pleasantly surprised with Kenzie. Yes, she's from modern day, but it's not as "in your face" as it could have been, since she's trying to fit in. There are just enough modern references to make the point, and then everything else is solidly set in P&P days.

Colonel Fitzwilliam is really well done, with his practical and war-hardened sides warring with his romantic and playful sides. The author even (somehow) managed to fit in glimpses of everyone on Darcy's side of the story (i.e., no Bennets except Jane) without it feeling rushed or cramped. Richard's parents are pushing him to get married, Lady Catherine obviously has her piece to say on that, Anne is protective of Richard (which is a trope I love), Darcy and Elizabeth are fiercely in love, Georgiana is still shy while learning from her new sister how to be impulsive, too....so yeah, beloved characters are well-treated and were nice to see.

Loved it. Only wish it could have been longer, for obvious reasons. 4.5 stars rounded up.

Title: Never So Bewitched By Any Woman: A Pride And Prejudice Variation
Author: Isabelle Mayfair
My Review:
This story was cute. I enjoyed how some of the pieces shifted around, with Wickham missing his opportunity in Ramsgate and pursuing Georgiana in London instead. I enjoyed that Elizabeth was still fooled by Wickham but then started to see his true character on her own, even before she knew he was involved with Georgiana.

The romance was a bit abrupt, though. Darcy and Elizabeth barely said a few sentences to one another before they had this deep connection. I love their battles of wits in the original, so it was disappointing to have something very close to instalove here. It all wrapped up nicely, and I enjoyed it overall, but it really needed some more in the middle for the two to get to know one another for me to go higher than 3 stars.

Title: Darcy's Hope: Beauty from Ashes: A WWI Pride & Prejudice Variation (Great War Romance, #1)
Author: Ginger Monette

Title: Vampire Darcy's Desire: A Pride and Prejudice Adaptation
Author: Regina Jeffers

Title: Lady Bridget's Diary (Keeping Up with the Cavendishes, #1)
Author: Maya Rodale

Title: Christmas Cheer (Holidays With Jane #1)
Author: Jennifer Becton
Additional Authors: Melissa Buell, Rebecca M. Fleming, Cecilia Gray, Jessica  Grey, Kimberly Truesdale

Title: Captain Frederick Wentworth's Persuasion: Jane Austen's Classic Retold Through His Eyes
Author: Regina Jeffers

Title: How I Write: Secrets of a Bestselling Author
Author: Janet Evanovich
My Review: 3.5 stars rounded up. I listened to the audio version, which was read by the authors. The advice offered was nothing special, particularly if you're anything other than a complete beginner. It was more a collection of author FAQs than anything. But the author is a funny lady who writes funny books, and the material was presented in a similar manner. It was entertaining, but not overly educational. Also outdated at this point, but that's not the book's fault.

Title: The Ball At Meryton: A Pride and Prejudice Alternative Novella
Author: Bronwen Chisholm
My Review:
Edit 12/13/16 - Hmm, apparently this was still in "to read" status. Gotta change that so it can count toward my yearly total! :-)

This story was cute and rather fluffy. I liked the way the plot progressed, but it was definitely a shallow version of the tale, in that the character growth and emotions were not explored much. It was hard to believe that Darcy could so easily reconcile himself to the match at this divergence point, but there were some cute and sweet moments that I couldn't resist. Overall not bad, and I would read another by this author.

Title: The Secret Lives of Hoarders: True Stories of Tackling Extreme Clutter
Author: Matt Paxton

Title: Win, Lose, or Darcy
Author: Jennifer Joy
My Review:
3.5 stars rounded down to 3. I always enjoy getting to see the Bennets with improved circumstances, and each author seems to take it a slightly different direction. It was fun to see Elizabeth with her thoughts on making an advantageous marriage even as she tried to reunite Jane and Bingley, but I felt like the story was often lacking in substance. The social situations held no real challenges or ways for Elizabeth to show her wit and the bad things that happened were resolved often within the same scene. Mr. Bennet's faults (particularly in not listening to Elizabeth's warnings) were drawn out to a point that I had a hard time enjoying the drama that resulted. Sophia, likewise, was all over the place as a character, and I didn't entirely buy her actions. Darcy was almost too perfect in the way that he decisively started to change so soon after the Netherfield ball. And why didn't Bingley get Darcy's letters? I really wanted it to be because Caroline had had them intercepted, or something like it, but it simply wasn't addressed.

It was a fast read, and I did like it, but it won't go on my list of favorites.

Title: Infamous Relations: A Pride And Prejudice "What If?" Tale
Author: Catherine Bilson
My Review:
Wow. I've never seen a story where Mr. Collins was quite this bad. Whether or not it's believable for him to suddenly show this despicable side of himself...well, that's debatable. I wanted to see where the story was going and so just went with it. There are quite a few instances of things falling into place a little too conveniently (such as the quick succession in which each person seems to arrive in Hunsford), and often tension was not held quite long enough to make the story as engaging as it could have been (it might have been nice to worry for at least a couple of pages that Darcy might hesitate to marry Elizabeth about what happens), but ultimately I did enjoy this story a great deal.

Jane had more spunk than usual, and likewise Charlotte did an admirable job of standing up to her husband. I liked their supporting force around Elizabeth, and I think I actually enjoyed seeing Jane plotting with Colonel Fitzwilliam even more than I enjoyed seeing Darcy and Elizabeth together. (I think Elizabeth came around to Darcy rather too quickly, given how much power one talk with Jane had versus the letter itself in canon...but I can usually forgive that in stories.) The doctor was a nice addition to the cast of characters and to the investigative plot. Maybe I just need to read more JAFF mysteries....

Title: Starlight Over Pemberley (Jonathan Thomas and Georgiana Darcy Chronicle, #1)
Author: Sebastian Di Mattia

Title: Hope's Folly (Dock Five Universe, #3)
Author: Linnea Sinclair
My Review:
I've never read much sci-fi. Mostly, it's because I don't care about the technical know-how of space travel and things like that, and I get annoyed by all the details of it. I liked and was good at science, but I don't like feeling like I need an advanced degree in it to enjoy a book. However, a not-so-slight (and rather unexpected) obsession with the Mass Effect video game series has made me rethink that position. It's not that I dislike sci-fi--it's that I need a certain type of sci-fi. Thus, a search began.

I can't say this book filled the Mass Effect-sized hole in my heart, but it certainly wasn't a disappointment. I really only have two complaints:

1) I could tell I was reading the third book of the series. Yep, my own fault, but hey, it was the only book in the series available from my library's ebook catalog! I didn't want to spend the money if I wasn't going to enjoy it, so I sucked it up and pushed through all the previous book references. [Note: No stars were deducted for this. Just wanted to point it out.]

2) The romance happened WAY fast. The characters were pulling long hours on the ship, so they were probably in each other's company double what that number of days would have meant under normal circumstances, but still. I think the whole thing took place in something like a week. Maybe less. The romance was otherwise good, which is why I hated being reminded of the timeline so often. (Sure, the whole story was a series of ticking time bombs, so that was important, but it detracted from the romance for me.)

There were enough things that I liked to keep me engaged in the story, though, so most of the time I could ignore it.

1) I loved that the main male character was older (40s), had [prematurely] gray hair, and was injured for the entire book. (Due to something that happened in book 2, I'm pretty sure. Kinda wish I'd gotten to enjoy that carryover, but oh well. I'll wait a few months before reading the others, and I'm sure I'll have mostly forgotten this one anyway. I suck at remembering plots. It's kind of a blessing. :D ) He was definitely an alpha male--I mean, he's a freakin' Admiral--but not in the beat-your-chest and dominate kind of way. He took advantage of the strengths of those around him, and the only time he really put his foot down and was a bit big-headed was when Rya is trying to convince him to let her take his place or go along on his self-sacrificing plan near the end. But he loves her, and I think his refusal was fairly reasonable in that case.

2) Rya was capable and pretty badass, but she makes mistakes and miscalculations. In some ways, she lives up to the perception that people have of her (that she's scary), but you can also see the side of her that's torn up over losing her father, terrified of not measuring up, and desperate to keep up with the big dogs she's been thrown alongside. She was a heroine I truly liked and wouldn't mind spending more time with.

3) The plot was just fun. Again, I can tell it's just a small part of a larger story. (Is there another book after this? Mental note. Must check.) That said, it was still one disaster and tough decision and death-defying event after another, and that made it a fun and super easy read. Too many names and ships and titles and political things, but I'm chalking that up to sci-fi and book 3. I didn't try too hard to memorize it all, and that didn't affect my enjoyment.

4) Captain Folly.

Title: Unwilling: A Pride and Prejudice Vagary
Author: Elizabeth     Adams
My Review:
This story started in a more distant sort of third person that reminded me of the source material but did make me a little afraid that it wouldn't be to my liking. (It's a style that takes talent, so I'm always hesitant.) It softened a bit as it got deeper into Elizabeth and Darcy's story, though, so I did end up enjoying the way it was written. I read most of this in one sitting.

I liked seeing Jane's interactions with London society and with Bingley almost more than I liked Elizabeth and Darcy. She had new and interesting thoughts and conflicts, while the latter couple traversed very familiar ground, but without much of the external conflict that makes their story interesting. Lydia is a brat, but then she's boring. Wickham disappears except for Darcy's information about him. I was hoping at least that he'd run away with Maria Lucas or something, but no luck. Mrs. Bennet has some obnoxious moments, but they don't really cause anything other than momentary embarrassment.

It wasn't bad, and I did enjoy spending a few hours with Elizabeth and Darcy as they sorted out their new lives, but it was fairly lackluster. 3.5 stars rounded up.

Title: An Unwelcome Guest at the Netherfield Ball
Author: E. Bradshaw
My Review:
This is one of those frustrating books that had some potential but could have been so much better. I'm a fan of compromise and forced marriage stories, as a general rule, but there were too many irritating elements to this story to rate it higher. I think if some of the minor things I describe below had been remedied by a good editor, then I could have easily added a star. Other things were larger problems, but also could have been helped with a good editor or beta reader. The combined effect of large and small flaws was not a terrible book, but a disappointing one.

1) There was a lot of repetition in the beginning of things that the average reader isn't going to need to read, like very specific descriptions and identifications of all of the Bennets and their personalities.

2) Related to #1 is that there was an excessive amount of introspection--paragraph upon paragraph of reiteration of the feelings and thoughts of any POV character, including rehashes of things that readers would already know or that had already been communicated in the story itself. It persisted throughout the whole story, and I found myself scanning and skimming for the next piece of action or dialogue.

3) There was a lot of unnecessary and excessive use of italics throughout. It becomes numbing as a means of emphasizing certain words or phrases if every paragraph has six words that are given special emphasis.

4) At around 8%, the phrase "mute point" was used. That is a particular pet peeve that always makes me cringe. (It's "moot point," by the way.)

5) And last, but not least, the plot structure was just kind of a mess. The title implies that Wickham is going to be a big antagonist, but his physical appearances are minimal and he doesn't really do anything other than dance with Elizabeth. Lydia is almost non-existent in the story, and her involvement with Wickham is not explored. It's understandable, due to the divergence point, but it was a missed opportunity in this case, because Wickham's story was pretty dull here. Collins is far more of an antagonist in the early parts of the book, but then he drops off the face of the story once the forced marriage takes place. The last 2/3 of the book centers around only misunderstandings between Elizabeth and Darcy that were, frankly, sometimes difficult to believe. Neither one of them are stupid in canon, but they were often portrayed as oblivious fools in not-entertaining ways. Because of that, I was not nearly as engaged in their delayed courtship as I felt like I should have been at that point.

Title: Lady Elizabeth (Everything Will Change #1)
Author: P.O. Dixon
My Review: I feel kind of cheated that this book has a sequel that I need to get to finished the story. I'm not sure if I didn't notice, had forgotten, or if maybe the sequel wasn't out yet when I got this book. Regardless, this book was a super quick read that left me furrowing my brow in annoyance when my kindle no longer had pages left. I was enjoying the tale (hence my 4-star rating), but now I'm not sure if I want to go on. I hate being forced to buy more than one book when it's really one story. Sure, Elizabeth learned the truth about her heritage, so that was semi-resolved by the end of this--but aren't we all really just here to see her and Darcy get together?

Title: Did Darcy Do It?: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: Sophie Lynbrook
My Review: I can't say this was a great book, but it was decent enough. 3.5 stars rounded up to 4. It was a little jarring how the story started with the announcement of Wickham's murder and then flashed back to the beginning. Even though I understood why it was done that way, I found myself a little annoyed at the delay and wanted things to just hurry up and get back to the aftermath of the murder. It seemed to take forever before it finally got there. The process of trying to prove Darcy innocent was pretty good, and the mystery halfway decent, but I was wishing most of the time that there was more of Darcy and Elizabeth. I did really enjoy Bingley's staunch support of his friend and how their small group banded together, though. Darcy's sadness over his possible fate could have descended into melodrama, but it did not, and I was thankful. The resolution was satisfying enough, and overall I enjoyed this tale.

Title: An Heir for Pemberley: A Pride and Prejudice Variation Short Story
Author: Jane Grix
My Review: This was a nice quick read, but it didn't feel like an actual short story. It was more like a scene from a larger work, which means that we lost the context that more time with the characters would have brought, and we gained only the shorter read time. There was no conflict--only introspection. Well written, but I would have felt cheated if it hadn't been free.

Title: Complicated Attachments: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: Denise O'Hara
My Review:
This one wasn't my favorite. 2.5 stars rounded up to 3. Many of the characters felt inconsistent to canon, which didn't seem to be part of the variation as advertised, but maybe it was. Maybe the intention was to make a lot of small changes to the canon backstory before launching into the new version of the tale, but it sapped some of the fun of the variation for me. Georgiana was the secondary character that struck me the most, probably because she was far more confident (for no particular reason I could see) and close friends with Caroline Bingley. Just weird.

Jane held to her sweet nature for the most part, but having her fall hard for Mr. Wickham's charm made me lose respect for her. It was weird to me that she agreed to let him call on her and give the impression of something heading toward marriage after so short a time period. I did like to get to see her interacting with Bingley while Elizabeth was sick. That role reversal was fun...though, admittedly, the wit flowing between Elizabeth and Darcy was most of the fun of that section of the original, and it was almost entirely gone. Wickham was weird and inconsistent, too. And since Lydia didn't go to Brighton, and Darcy just plainly told Jane the truth about him, all the interesting things with his plot were gone, too.

I didn't care much for Darcy in this book, unfortunately. Some of the things he says to Elizabeth go over the line from unconscious arrogance to cruelty, and especially because he seems to be saying them partly to get a rise out of her and spark a debate. There's an attempt to make up for them afterward, but it doesn't redeem him in my eyes. It takes work to make his Hunsford proposal seem tame and almost romantic in comparison. And since Elizabeth was somewhat passive and sidelined by Jane's part in the story, I just didn't care that much about their romance.

Title: Mr Bennet's Dutiful Daughter
Author: Joana Starnes
My Review:
This book was really well written, so I wanted to start by saying my 4-star rating is more based on personal preference for stories rather than any technical flaws. I read this nearly straight through over a 24-hour period, only breaking due to life needs rather than desire. As the story progressed, I read as fast as my eyes and brain would go, because the plot turned in agonizing directions. It was those directions that dropped my rating--the angst was just too much.

I liked the way Elizabeth and Darcy's marriage was accomplished in this story. I've read others where she accepts him due to her father's death or generally practical reasons, but this is the first where I've seen her marry for her family's protection with Mr. Bennet being on his death bed. It's a subtle difference, but it changed her early interactions with Darcy in a way that was interesting. My favorite thing about this beginning was the way Darcy stepped up for her so protectively, in reaction to Mr. Collins. Yes, all of the reasons why they shouldn't marry still exist, but he doesn't hesitate to throw them aside when she needs him (or he deems that she needs him, really, even though she truthfully does).

I liked that Darcy was so oblivious to Elizabeth's reactions when he made disparaging comments about her family. It's fitting, and also made me feel really bad for him. Sure, it's rude and insulting for him to voice the thoughts aloud...but there's a lot of truth in what he says, and he's expressing it because he thinks that he and Elizabeth are of one mind on it. He's so attentive and romantic otherwise that it almost physically hurt every time Elizabeth was jolted by one of those comments. It's no shock to me that she fell him love with him even before that point of contention was resolved. This was a pretty rare story in that I completely believed that she would fall for him at that stage, and she hadn't even found out the truth about Wickham yet, either.

Then came the real agony. I wasn't surprised that Darcy reacted strongly when he found out that Elizabeth had accepted him for practical reasons only. He'd shown his passionate nature by then, so it was in character. However, the angst was so intense that I was uncomfortable reading it. That says a lot for the writing, I think, but it did detract from my enjoyment. After Darcy rejected Elizabeth's first attempts to reconcile, I started speed-reading/skimming to get to the resolution. It was really difficult for me to stomach that he would jump straight to permanent separation from her without even giving her a chance to speak her side. I can understand a knee-jerk reaction to not want to talk to her, but I felt like he should have given in sooner without needing her to show up at his door after finding out she's pregnant.

But can I just say that Bingley was FABULOUS in this story? I highly recommend this story for anyone who likes to see an assertive Bingley who's still completely in character.

I'm not sure I'd be able to reread this story, but I did enjoy it, and I would recommend it. Just be aware that there's painful angst that (for me) sometimes stretched my suspension of disbelief. This book definitely pulls emotional chords with superior skill. My heart still hurts, even after the happy resolution.

Title: Becoming Elizabeth: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: Ivy May Stuart
My Review:
Unfortunately, I did not care for this one. 2.5 stars rounded down.

I did like the more assertive Bingley, and that Darcy acknowledged to himself that he was dependent on Bingley for his social skills. The general progression of the plot was fine, too, but only at a broad outline level. In the details, there were things inserted that felt very like heavy-handed plot devices rather than seamless parts of the story. With the murders in London, there was unnecessary gruesome detail, too, that seemed out of place in this type of story. Darcy spends multiple paragraphs citing philosophy and considering the text. Elizabeth ran hot and cold in her interactions with Darcy. None of the characters had to earn the changes in their behavior or personality; it was mentioned as being needed, and then in the next scene it was changed.

And where in the world did the whole thing with Lady Catherine accusing Richard of having a liaison with Elizabeth come from? It seemed totally out of the blue to me.

My biggest issue, though, was something I noticed really early in the story and probably could have disregarded, if the rest hadn't bugged me. However, because of the rest being added, this seemed to stand out that much more. The characters spoke far more freely than seemed proper or in character, not hesitating to express their opinions about other people or situations. It seemed to apply to everyone. Elizabeth speaks out about her sisters' behavior at the ball; Mrs. Gardiner gives Elizabeth a set-down about her behavior toward Darcy; Lady Marion (Matlock? Can't remember) pulls a nice Lady Catherine...and plenty more. It all just seemed so weird.

There were some distracting typos and formatting errors, particularly with quotation marks. A couple of times names were spelled incorrectly. Again, things I could have ignored if the story overall had resonated.

Title: Darcy to the Rescue: A Pride & Prejudice Variation Novella
Author: Martine J. Roberts
My Review:
I found this story completely ridiculous. It moved so fast that it gave me whiplash, and consequently there was zero character development. Elizabeth just somehow magically fell in love with Darcy in approximately 24 hours, Darcy realizes he's been proud in not much longer, Charlotte gets angry at Elizabeth for no reason related to the plot, and Caroline makes such a strange claim that I could only roll my eyes when Elizabeth believed her.

So disappointing when I know this concept can be (and has been, by others) done well.

Title: Miss Darcy's Companion: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: Joana Starnes

Title: Elizabeth Bennet's Deception: A Pride and Prejudice Vagary
Author: Regina Jeffers

Title: Her Father's Choice: A Pride and Prejudice Novella (Choices Book 1)
Author: Leenie Brown
My Review:
I found it really interesting that this is a story of compromise brought about by Mr. Bennet. His motivations seemed a little shaky to me, but not so much that I couldn't go with the flow and enjoy the story. Elizabeth was more reasonable throughout than I expected, and I think she came around to loving Darcy a little too quickly for something diverging at the Netherfield ball, but again, I still enjoyed it.

There are a couple of characters added to Colonel Fitzwilliam's family who felt a bit extraneous until I realized they were sequel bait. Mrs. Bennet getting one up on Lady Catherine sort of came out of nowhere, but I'm curious to see if what she brought up becomes important in a sequel...which I think I'll read at some point. Probably. I really liked Mary in this story, and since book 2 is about her, I'm sure I'll probably enjoy it, too.

FYI - The actual story ends around 70% in the kindle version, so just be aware that it's not as long as it seems. There's a pretty hefty sample of book 2 at the end.

Title: The Inconsistency of Caroline Bingley
Author: Renata McMann
My Review: This story was inconsistent. It couldn't decide if it was a story about Elizabeth and Darcy or a story about Caroline, but since Caroline got little-to-no character development, having parts that were largely focused on her felt like a waste. I did like the twist of how Darcy involved himself with Lydia's rescue, given the variation, but the story was so short that it really just glossed over everything that could have made Elizabeth and Darcy's relationship interesting. I didn't feel like anything was gained from their interactions, so Darcy's renewal of his proposal just felt like a thing that was going to happen rather than something about which I was excited.

Title: Deadpool Classic, Vol. 1
Author: Fabian Nicieza
Additional Authors: Rob Liefeld, Mark Waid, Joe Kelly

Title: To Capture Mr. Darcy: A Pride and Prejudice Variation Novel
Author: Elizabeth Ann West
My Review:
Okay, this book came highly recommended, so I hate to say this...but I didn't like it as much as I expected. It wasn't bad or anything--really more like a 3.5 star book for the writing and basic flow of the plot--but the story itself was nothing special, and thus the minor annoyances I had with it seemed that much bigger, when in a more complex or involved story those things might have faded into the background.

My annoyances were as follows:

-Darcy's initial confession of his affection seemed sudden and too strongly worded for being so early in their acquaintance, and especially considering how much more he has to struggle in canon to even admit that he likes her. I get that it was supposed to highlight his awkwardness and inability to express himself to her, but it just made me cringe. Ditto most of their later conversations where she misunderstands his meaning.
-The language seems intentionally complex, using big words where more simplistic phrasing would have sufficed. It was perfectly well done, but felt like it was trying too hard.
-Lizzie and Mariah should have been Lizzy and Maria (this always bugs me in stories).
-Elizabeth retaliates in ways that seem too petty and combative for her. I can see her throwing punch on Mr. Collins (in fact, this should be a plot point in every story from now on!), but not her comments to Lady Catherine and Caroline. It's so much better when she can get them back with witty barbs rather than bare insults.
-I like seeing a slightly more aggressive Jane, but in this case she seemed to take on too much of Elizabeth's personality. She was often teasing and impertinent, and I think there are better ways for Jane to come into her own.
-Elizabeth and Darcy's romance progressed too quickly, and their lessons were either too easily learned or never brought up. Elizabeth getting over her initial dislike and Darcy's behavior for a few days just doesn't have the same punch without adding Wickham and Jane/Bingley as conflict, or adding appropriate conflict substitutes. Caroline might have worked without the aforementioned tendency for Elizabeth's retorts to be rather childish, ruining the effect.
-Wickham's set-down was too easy and distant, and Darcy didn't even have to tell Elizabeth the truth about him and Georgiana.

Regardless of these opinions, thanks so much to Lenora and Debbie for the recommendation, and to Lenora for the loan of the kindle book!

Title: The Red Chrysanthemum: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: Linda Beutler
My Review:
Reread 10/9/16

I enjoyed this the second time around, though I'll admit I skimmed some of the middle bits and skipped most of the epilogue.

Original read 12/16/15

This is the third (and I think last) of this author's P&P based books I've read. I was really impressed with both the style and the depth of story in the first one, and I'm glad to see that quality continued here. I'm always in favor of Darcy and Elizabeth getting more time together in a story, and I liked that the subtle variation here allowed for that. There were times when I thought Darcy was a bit too gushy and romantic, and the things that kept them apart (mostly the mutual disbelief that the other loved him/her) dragged on a bit farther than was probably believable...but the only part that truly dragged (and my only true complaint) was the ending after their marriage. I think this is partially because I only recently read [b:Longbourn to London|22910648|Longbourn to London|Linda Beutler|https://d.gr-assets.com/books/1409115412s/22910648.jpg|42475179] by this author, and there was a lot of repetition. It might not have bugged me if I hadn't had the other fresh on my mind.

Overall, though, I really enjoyed this, and I would read another by this author, if she ever writes one.

Title: The Journey
Author: Jan Hahn
My Review:
Reread 10/8/16

I was in the mood for something sort of fanciful. I still find this one rather over the top, but there are some very nice bits, too. Skipped the epilogue this time.

Original read 7/21/15

The plot of this P&P variation was pretty far-fetched and, many times, silly. Lots of fluff. I admit I started skimming at some point, and I almost didn't read the last 15% or so. It seemed too fluffy by that time. The last chapter + epilogue were excessive. I appreciate as much as the average person getting some closure, but I didn't really need to have a summary of 20+ years of their married life.

But meh. If you go in knowing that it's probably going to be silly and fluffy, it's not awful.

Title: River Marked (Mercy Thompson, #6)
Author: Patricia Briggs
My Review:
This book was very interesting from a character dynamic perspective. Since most of it revolved around Mercy and Adam's honeymoon, most of the other characters who've been in the previous books weren't around much. Even with that, though, they still felt present. They appeared in the beginning, were in Mercy's thoughts, and even checked in on occasion. I love how the author makes the world feel so real like that, where life doesn't stop happening just because the plot of the book is going on.

There was a lot to like about this book, not surprisingly. I loved the bet and Jesse conspiring with Mercy's mom to plan the wedding. I loved that Bran insisted on giving Mercy away. I loved Adam and his gorgeous alpha self, and particularly how he's working on those alpha things he does that get under Mercy's skin sometimes. I loved the revelations about Mercy's father. I loved how the walking stick factored in, but also that Mercy finally managed to get rid of it. It was also a nice change that no one got captured or kidnapped this time around. It was just a straight-up fight to the death.

I'm probably gonna take a break from this series after this one, because I can see the latest book fast approaching, and I don't want to run out! Also, I've managed to run down my audible credits thanks to these addictive things....

The audiobook narrator of this series, Lorelei King, is AWESOME. I avoid reading the text for these just because I like her so much.

Title: Waking to Mr. Darcy (Dash of Darcy #2)
Author: Leenie Brown
My Review:
This was a really interesting version of a marriage-of-compromise story, in that the focus wasn't on the marriage itself, but how it came about. I'm a bit of a sucker for the whole trope of one of them being injured and also for them being trapped together for some reason, so this was a bit of both. Overall, I expected this one to either be a total mess or to have many things I enjoyed, and I'm glad the latter was the case. I liked how it highlighted Darcy's sense of honor and that was the thing that overcame his arrogance to allow him to really see the best things about a marriage to Elizabeth.

I did sort of raise an eyebrow that neither man was willing to risk dark and rain to go get help for Elizabeth. They contemplated it for all of two seconds, and then Darcy's all, "Well, I guess I'll have to undress her." But I liked the rest of it enough that I just sort of laughed and moved on.

Title: Caroline's Fate: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: Elaine Jenkins
My Review:
This story was good fun. I liked Darcy and Elizabeth getting to work together and using that time to figure each other out. It was maybe a bit too convenient that they learned precisely what they needed to learn without the heavy lessons of the original, but it was enough fun that I could overlook it. I greatly enjoyed that this story had a bit of a cozy mystery vibe in places, though it would have been more fun if that had been amplified to its full potential. Caroline's characterization was a bit inconsistent (particularly her one conversation with Elizabeth), and sometimes the scenes where she carried out her "plots" were confusing to follow (particularly the later one). Too many times there were scenes where "something happened" and then you had to follow along for a half chapter before it explained exactly what did happen, which was also confusing.

But even with its faults, I enjoyed the story and that it did something very different with the characters without taking them too far from canon (except perhaps Caroline, but even then only sometimes).

Title: A Walk in Oakham Wood: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: Madeline Kennet
My Review: This was well-written in spite of some typos and formatting issues, but it wasn't really a full story. There's no real conflict. It's just one really long conversation. It's a nice conversation, but I would have liked it better if it had been split into several smaller conversations interspersed with an actual plot. No matter how intense their connection, the ending was rather abrupt and not at all believable based on the Darcy and Elizabeth I know. It was a nice read, though, if ultimately disappointing in its content.

Title: The Fire at Netherfield Park: A Pride & Prejudice Variation
Author: Renata McMann
My Review:
I absolutely adored how this story started off--the first couple of chapters were intense and drew me in immediately--and I was fascinated by some of the subsequent turns in the story. It kept me reading late into the night. However, I deducted a star because I feel like some of those turns could have been carried farther. There's a point later in the book when things start to take the normal plot path, despite the differences, and that was a little disappointing.

Some specifics:

1) I would never, before now, have thought of Mr. Bennet marrying Charlotte. I guess because Mrs. Bennet is such a source of conflict that she rarely gets killed off. Despite that, it somehow makes perfect sense, even if I find it a little weird for Elizabeth's sake.

2) I was disappointed that Darcy and Wickham both have their flaws pointed out very early on, both make efforts to change...and then the exact same things happen. Wickham still runs off with Lydia with no intention to marry her, and Darcy still flubs his proposal with very similar language. I would have loved to see their altered paths give them new flaws to overcome.

3) That said, I did like how things turned out with Wickham and Lydia and the hat shop.

4) However, I didn't feel like Darcy and Elizabeth had the same reasons to react the ways they did. Elizabeth already knew what Wickham was, and Jane and Bingley were married, so it was only his arrogance that sent her running. Similarly, it didn't feel like Darcy had enough feedback from Elizabeth to make big enough changes. The two were left with a pretty short span in the novel to turn their romance around, so it didn't feel as believable.

Title: The Hundred (The Hundred, #1)
Author: Kass Morgan
Publisher: Little, Brown Books for Young Readers

Title: The Annotated Wizard of Oz (Oz, #1)
Author: L. Frank Baum
Additional Authors: Martin Gardner, W.W. Denslow, Michael Patrick Hearn

Title: Iron Kissed (Mercy Thompson, #3)
Author: Patricia Briggs
My Review:
I love this series. Patricia Briggs has some serious talent as a writer. Somehow her characters are vivid and have lives outside the pages of the books, even though they are tightly written with fast-paced plots and no wasted words. I waited a year between reading [b:Blood Bound|285205|Blood Bound (Mercy Thompson, #2)|Patricia Briggs|https://d2arxad8u2l0g7.cloudfront.net/books/1358229157s/285205.jpg|2577848] and reading this one--because I'm a moody reader, not because of anything about the book--and it was so easy to jump back into this world.

I always have the most trouble writing insightful reviews for books that I loved, because I feel like I'd just be regurgitating the plot and then pointing at it as the example of what I loved. It was nice to get more about the fae, and especially because it involved Zee, wonderful curmudgeon that he is. I still loved both Adam and Samuel, and while I'm glad that Mercy decided she wanted to be with Adam, her frank discussion with Samuel about their relationship was one of my favorite parts of the book. Her friendship with Warren and Kyle got screen time for less serious matters, and I loved every minute of it. In short, this book was awesome. Read it.

I've already started [b:Bone Crossed|3128411|Bone Crossed (Mercy Thompson, #4)|Patricia Briggs|https://d2arxad8u2l0g7.cloudfront.net/books/1305738183s/3128411.jpg|3159812] and was SUPER happy to find that it picked up immediately after this one. Like, in the same scene.

Note: I listened to the audiobook and, like always, Lorelei King was fabulous.

Title: Second Impressions: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: Amy  George
My Review: I liked this book, but it lacked depth in places that I would have really liked it. Darcy and Elizabeth start out the book already in love, and they have to do very little to clear up a couple of things and get together. All the obstacles seem like they should be difficult, but they really aren't. And a large part of the book focuses on Elizabeth's American cousin, Emmeline, and while she was fine, I would have preferred to just have more time with Elizabeth and Darcy. In general, I really enjoy the trope where Darcy is a widower (usually from Anne) with a kid, but there were so many missed opportunities there. So yeah. It was well-written, but rather shallow at times or reliant on quick tells rather than shows.

Title: Beyond Heaving Bosoms: The Smart Bitches' Guide to Romance Novels
Author: Sarah Wendell

Title: Pride and Prejudice and Zombies: The Graphic Novel
Author: Seth Grahame-Smith

Title: Let Me Be the One (San Francisco Sullivans, #6; The Sullivans, #6)
Author: Bella Andre

Title: A Fine Joke
Author: Alex9903
Publisher: Meryton Literary Society ( A Happy Assembly* )
My Review: I expected this story to be depressing and frustrating, but it was instead great fun! The joke aspect doesn't stay secret from everyone for so long that it's annoying, and Darcy approaches the news of his fatal disease with such purpose that there's very little dwelling on the sad reality of it all. It's a novella-length story, but there's still a really nice build-up both of Darcy and Elizabeth's relationship and of the solution to the mystery. Yes, Elizabeth softens to Darcy pretty quickly, but this is one of the few scenarios where I found it completely believable. After all, she initially thinks he's dying, and he comes to her so humbly that it would have been harder for her to act otherwise with him. There are a lot of other characters, most of which have fairly passing involvement, but I really enjoyed Wickham's part in it all. Very different, but in a nice way.

Title: Pride, Prejudice & Secrets
Author: C.P. Odom
My Review:
I enjoyed this book a great deal, despite its faults, so this is 3.5 stars rounded up to 4. I liked that Darcy and Elizabeth's engagement was neither compromise nor forced necessity (I like those, too, but it's always nice to see something different). I liked how all of the people who knew about it piled up around Elizabeth, forcing her to think seriously about it and not break off the engagement on a knee-jerk reaction. It was also interesting to see Elizabeth deal with the Wickham situation in a mature fashion. However, both of those circumstances meant that there was almost no conflict whatsoever in the story. I had fun along the ride, but it probably could have been a 5-star book if the characters had had to work at anything to find happiness.
