I also would have preferred if things had focused solely on Darcy and Elizabeth, because most of the secondary plots (Jane and Captain Fitzwilliam--heck, even that man's existence seemed superfluous--Caroline and Colonel Fitzwilliam, Wickham and Mary King and his military success) were wholly unconnected with the main story (other than being about characters we know) and could easily have been deleted without affecting anything. The epilogue, too, did that thing I hate where it chronicled the next multitude of years for every character.

But again, I did really enjoy the story, and especially seeing how Elizabeth came around to Darcy in a rational, sensible way. Not the most exciting story--I definitely can't call it gripping--but quietly entertaining.

Title: Darcy By Any Other Name: A lighthearted Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: Laura Hile
My Review:
Hmm...how to describe the way I feel about this book? It was very well written, and I loved the way it portrayed Darcy--which is a good thing, since the majority of the story is from his perspective. He handles his existence as Collins with even more grace than I might have expected from him. Is it any wonder that Elizabeth took notice?

But that's the main reason I had to deduct a star--that Elizabeth fell in love with him as Mr. Collins, and particularly in only a couple of weeks. Obviously, it's great that she could see past his physical self and come to love the person that he was. It's nice that it proves her love has nothing to do with his fortune (one of the things I liked about books like [b:Poor Mr. Darcy: A Pride & Prejudice Variation Novella|25886806|Poor Mr. Darcy A Pride & Prejudice Variation Novella|Renata McMann|https://d.gr-assets.com/books/1439611157s/25886806.jpg|45766389]). I like the idea that Darcy was willing to live his life as Collins with Elizabeth at his side. But I couldn't get over the fact that it was Mr. Collins. Maybe I'm just superficial that way, but it was creepy to me.

I liked the twist on Wickham's storyline. I didn't care for Colonel Fitzwilliam's part. He's a particular favorite, so I was really counting on him coming around. I understand why he couldn't for story purposes, but his role seemed so much less than it could have been. It would have been nice to see Bingley more present, but I guess his excuse was being cowed by Lady Catherine--or maybe that it would expose Collins-as-Darcy too soon?

In short, I liked it a lot, but there were a few things that bugged me.

Title: Mr. Darcy Came to Dinner
Author: Jack Caldwell
My Review:
Second read 5/27/16:

The audiobook is very well done. Still fluffy, but still fun.

First read 9/13/14:

This was cute. I didn't love the way the prose directly spoke to the reader sometimes, and the whole thing was rather...idealistic and unrealistic...but I still enjoyed it. Beloved characters got what I wanted. Minor characters got moments in the spotlight. Disliked characters were either redeemed or got deserved comeuppance. It was a lot of fun to have an excuse to have all these characters under the same roof for an extended period of time. Basically, it's a Pride and Prejudice where everyone communicates instead of keeping secrets (90% of the time, anyway) and where everyone gets what's best for them. Fluffy, for sure, but fun enough for a quick read.

Title: The Scent of Metal (Argonauts of Space, #1)
Author: Sabrina Chase

Title: Lady Susan
Author: Jane Austen
My Review:
Definitely not my favorite Jane Austen. It was quick and engaging, and the characters were vibrant and well done, but I just plain didn't like anyone enough to really care how it all turned out. I think I was waiting for some big twist to get thrown at me, but all we really got was the realization that Lady Susan was just as bad--and sometimes worse--as people said.

I do have to give kudos to the audiobook version (http://www.audible.com/pd/Classics/Lady-Susan-Audiobook/B01E7RSWHY) voiced by Elizabeth Klett and others, as it was very well done.

Title: Pride, Prejudice, and Cheese Grits (Jane Austen Takes the South, #1)
Author: Mary Jane Hathaway

Title: Darcy's Journey: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: M.A. Sandiford
My Review:
I loved this book! There were times when I was almost overwhelmed by the names, places, and the sheer history of it all, given that I'm not a history buff. (Okay, who am I kidding? I'm, like, the anti-history buff.) But even the healthy dose of historical information wasn't enough to dim my enjoyment of it. It was all woven in seamlessly, and I loved seeing Elizabeth and Darcy integrated into it all.

This is one of those stories where the progression of Darcy and Elizabeth's relationship is pretty low in conflict and angst. They approach things in a very grown-up and mature manner and have some very honest conversations on their future--which I loved. There was so much going on, though, that it was never boring...especially because they were both active participants in what was happening. I never felt like they were being forced along a path, but rather made choices and had to deal with the consequences.

Highly recommended. (And probably especially if you enjoy history and all things continental Europe...but don't let it scare you away if you don't.)

Title: Passage (The Sharing Knife, #3)
Author: Lois McMaster Bujold
My Review:
Second read 5/12/16

The thing that struck me the most on my reread (listen) of this book is just how complex Dag is. He's learning to deal with his past, learning about himself as he slowly changes into a new Dag (yet is still somehow himself), and learning both about and how to deal with others in ways he never needed before. I'm not so much sucked into the plot as I am sucked into Dag's progression. Even the tiniest things, like him ground-ripping oats, seem fascinating and intense.

Once again, the audio narration is fabulous. Mental note: need to seek out other books read by Bernadette Dunne.

First read 6/19/13

This was a really enjoyable installment of the series, possibly my favorite so far--though they're all pretty equal, so it's hard to say that for sure. The new characters who were introduced were vibrant and enjoyable, the familiar characters grew and learned from new experiences, and the challenges they all faced made for a good read.

I really enjoy the progression of these books, and how they all tell a complete tale (or at least a relatively complete one--book one did feel a bit chopped off) and yet take place one after another. It's like getting a nice breaking spot before starting on the next chapter. I've already bought book 4 (sadly, the last). I can't remember the last time I went straight through a series like this. Good stuff.

Title: Legacy (The Sharing Knife, #2)
Author: Lois McMaster Bujold
My Review:
First read 5/31/13; second read 5/10/16

Somehow I didn't review this book the first time I read it. I'm assuming that's mostly because I breezed through it and immediately started #3, but I think it's also because this book was so tense that I just had a hard time figuring out how to comment.

I enjoyed this as much as the rest of the series. Dag is just...great. I loved his long, troubled past and how it shapes his present so profoundly, even as he's feeling his way into a different sort of future. I liked Fawn alright, but I wasn't nearly as interested in her solo scenes as in Dag's. Every discovery he made about his ground and groundwork was fascinating, and the same was true for his personal, emotional discoveries. I wanted to slap his brother and mother for not being able to see him past their prejudices.

I also really liked the secondary characters here. Mari and Sean made reappearances, and then there were other Lakewalkers who somehow managed to be very vivid without even really having a lot of scenes. Fairbolt is probably my favorite, with Mari's husband being a close second (I know his name, but don't ask me to spell it...audiobooks are awful for that). I really appreciate these books for showing both sides of the misunderstandings and prejudices and making neither entirely right or entirely wrong.

Once again, the audiobook was well done. The narrator does an excellent job of showing emotions through the dialogue. I laughed aloud a couple of times because of the way the lines were expressed even more than just for the words themselves.

And yeah, I've already downloaded and started book 3, heh.

Title: Beguilement (The Sharing Knife, #1)
Author: Lois McMaster Bujold
My Review:
Second read 5/6/16:

I enjoyed this book even more the second time around--I think because I knew what to expect, but it had been long enough that I'd forgotten a lot of the details. The audiobook was available from Overdrive through my library, so I figured it was worth a try. The narrator was really good, and I found myself sitting in my car after getting home from work, just to finish the section before I had to turn it off again. Really good. I immediately downloaded book two (since it's essentially the second half of this one).

First read 5/28/13:

I was looking for a fantasy novel with romance elements, and this book was definitely that--though way more romance than I expected. It wasn't done in the romance novel style, but the story focused more on the relationship between Fawn and Dag than it did on the fantasy elements. In a way, this was really only a portion of the story. It wasn't a cliffhanger in the way that I hate, but the mystery of the fantasy goings-on was left open for the sequel.

I really enjoyed the world that was setup here, particularly the peek into Lakewalker culture. The magic isn't exactly revolutionary in its capabilities, but the way it's described is really interesting and different. Dag was rough around the edges but also a really likeable character, and I thought he was a worthy love interest. Fawn was fine, too, though I didn't like her quite as much.

It's hard to go into further detail without getting into potentially spoiler-y territory, because this story is straightforward and pretty uncomplicated. Long story short is that I could have stood to have a heftier adventure plot--a further extension of the things that happened in the first 1/3--but I enjoyed the story and the characters, and I plan to read the next one.

Title: Love Never Fails
Author: Jennifer Joy
My Review:
More like 3.5 stars, but there was something about it that kept me from being able to give it 4 stars. There were a lot of things I liked a great deal, like the way that Darcy and Elizabeth slowly got to know one another, but I felt like there were a lot of little things that were never developed up to their full potential.

Elizabeth and Darcy's relationship lacked almost all of the fire of the original. They simply got along from the beginning, and that never changed. I didn't believe that Colonel Fitzwilliam could fall in love with Caroline, nor that Caroline would suddenly be genuinely nice to Elizabeth based on one final conversation with her aunt. Aunt Lavinia herself was interesting, but her supposed meddling and helping Elizabeth seemed pointless, because Darcy and Elizabeth were getting there on their own anyway. I had a hard time believing Mrs. Bennet's sudden turnaround from silly to responsible, and her snagging a rich, kind, loving husband in a couple of months based on a newspaper ad. And Lydia's suicide attempt was sudden and barely got any attention beyond Darcy playing the hero. I wish there had been a little less going on and a little more attention given to fewer plotlines.

But it wasn't all bad. Even though I think Elizabeth and Darcy had it easy, I did like their scenes together. The prose itself was nice, and I was easily sucked into the story. I enjoyed Aunt Lavinia's antics (and especially the reactions from Bingley, Darcy, and Fitzwilliam), even though I was disappointed ultimately in her impact on the story. I liked the fun, easygoing relationship between the aforementioned gentlemen and really would have liked to see more of them. I liked how the author portrayed Elizabeth's grief for her father and also her strong sense of family--even her mother and Lydia, which was nice.

This isn't a reread for me, but it was enjoyable enough.

Title: On Oakham Mount: A Pride & Prejudice Variation (Pemberley Departures, #1)
Author: Sophia Meredith
My Review: This was pretty good, but I didn't like it as much as I expected I would based on the blurb. I expect that's more a stylistic and story subject matter preference than anything, but *shrug*. There was a lot of introspection that bordered on excessive for me, and Darcy and Elizabeth both came to love the other without really having to do much to earn it. Darcy, especially, seemed to magically learn his lessons about his pride and behavior without the harsh lessons he receives in the original. I did enjoy Colonel Fitzwilliam and his advice (and meddling) about Darcy and Elizabeth, and I enjoyed that <spoiler>Mary married Mr. Collins...it seems like that's a relationship that's favored by many but not actually written a lot (that I've read). Though there wasn't much depth in their characterizations, so it was a passing enjoyment.</spoiler> The rest, overall, just didn't do it for me. I was looking forward to tension and growing understanding between two people thrown together by circumstances, but that wasn't what this was about. Oh well.

Title: Just to Hear 'I Love You': An Alternate Tale of Jane Austen's 'Pride & Prejudice'
Author: Sarah   Johnson
My Review:
I feel like this book tried really hard but ultimately struggled to deliver what it promises. There are so many storylines that all but Mary and Fitzwilliam's felt shallow and excessive. I would have been fine if the sole focus had been on that couple, but it diverted too often to the other characters with their own plots. Elizabeth, Mary, and Georgiana, and even Fitzwilliam's backstory were so different from their canon selves that this might as well have not been a Pride and Prejudice story at all--which makes it feel like this is only JAFF so that the characters don't need as much developing. I'm sure that wasn't the intent, but rather the way the result felt.

Some things seemed to come out of nowhere (the way Meryton society blamed Mary) or were forced upon me in a way that I couldn't really enjoy how they came about (Bingley and Maria Lucas). Lots of telling rather than showing, and the plot felt jumpy and inconsistently paced. And now that I think about it, there weren't really obstacles that the characters had to overcome to achieve their happily ever after. Sure, there was disapproval from multiple fronts, but none of it really ever threatened to stop the characters from getting what they wanted. That took a lot of the thrill out of it.

I did really like the concept behind the changes to Mary, and it's always nice to see a story where she gets her time to shine. Colonel Fitzwilliam was very well done, too. Darcy's sneaky way of helping Fitzwilliam by giving Mary a dowry that would allow them to marry was a fun twist. Darcy and Elizabeth's story was sort of meh, but it was fine. I liked getting to see Bingley when there's no Jane to keep his attention, and the story did a good job of showing that fickle sort of love that Darcy claims Bingley often feels but that we never get to see in the original.

I didn't hate it, and I wasn't bored, but I felt like it lacked some essentials that could have made it a truly good story. Solid 3 stars.

Title: Pride and Persistence
Author: Jeanna Ellsworth
My Review:
Second read (4/22/16):

The audiobook was well done. The voice for Mr. Collins was far worse than the man himself (I was seriously tempted to fast forward), but otherwise I liked it. I noticed some repetition in description (particularly of the aforementioned Mr. Collins) and some other things that were mentioned enough (how much Elizabeth liked the various food items at Rosings) that I rolled my eyes. Overall, though, it was still enjoyable. Sure, Elizabeth was quick to come around to Darcy, but most of the appeal of this story is Darcy and his memory issues, and the rest is sort of scenery.

First read (4/12/14):

What can I say? I'm a sucker for Jane Austen adaptations and variations. They can be pretty hit or miss, but this was a rather good one (3.5 stars). My only real complaint is that any conflict was pretty light, and the heavier conflict (mostly perpetuated by Lady Catherine, not shockingly) was short-lived. I enjoyed the amnesia aspect of it the most. The first half of the story was definitely the best for me. I wouldn't say the second half was dull (because it wasn't), but it was definitely less exciting.

Title: Ready Player One (Ready Player One, #1)
Author: Ernest Cline
My Review:
I had all sorts of brilliant things to say about this book when I first finished it. Unfortunately, that was late in the evening last week and I didn't have the energy to write it up. Now, all cleverness has fled...but I'll do my best.

Overall, looking back from the ending, I loved this book. I dropped a star from my rating because the beginning was rather slow, and I'm honestly not sure if I would have continued without the superb narration pulling me along. It picked up around 50%, and from there I was hooked. I was still young in the eighties, so many of the (abundant) references were over my head, but there were enough that resonated to let me appreciate the awesome geekiness of it all. The main character was naive, unassuming, and relatable--if a bit too lucky at times to be altogether believable. The secondary characters were alternately fun and chilling. Though the world was sometimes drawn in excruciating detail, it was still vivid and fun.

A geeky friend pointed me at this book, and I would point fellow uber geeks toward it...with the caveat/warning to resign yourself to the amount of info dump headed your way and to just sit back and wait for the good stuff.

Title: Death Comes to Pemberley
Author: P.D. James
My Review:
This is one of those infrequent cases where the movie is much better than the book. I had avoided this due to the less-than-stellar reviews (and because I have plenty of other things to read), but I decided to give it a try because I enjoyed the miniseries enough to watch it three times within two weeks. (I know. Sometimes I can't help myself.) What I found wasn't awful, by any means, but I think a large part of my enjoyment was based on filling in the blanks based on what I already knew from the movie. This book's largest fault is that, due to the way the story unfolds...the last half is pretty dry and boring.

Be forewarned. I can't help comparing and contrasting against the movie as I examine the book's faults.

The one thing that I found disappointing about the movie was its portrayal of Colonel Fitzwilliam. It's no secret that a fan of him with Georgiana, when it's done right, but in the movie he was selfish, arrogant, and essentially talked Darcy into convincing Georgiana it was her duty to marry Colonel Fitzwilliam rather than Alveston. It was heartbreaking to see her do it, and I hated the Colonel, which made me sad. Despite the fact that this was my least favorite thing about the movie, I was even more disappointed when almost none of that storyline was in the book. Colonel Fitzwilliam was a bit stuffy and snooty, but he was never engaged to Georgiana, and he and Darcy never had a falling out about it. He was an ancillary character in the book, while in the movie he was a primary antagonist--and the movie was stronger for it.

My other complaint about the book, and the reason that I found the last half of the book so boring, was that Elizabeth was sorely underused. She's almost non-existent after they go to London and start the trial proceedings. It's all Darcy sitting in the courtroom, listening to the testimony, and getting advice/commentary from Alveston. In the movie, rather than having the Colonel and Wickham tell Darcy the truth about what happened, Elizabeth slowly uncovers it herself. She sees Louisa Bidwell with her baby, gets from her the information about her lover, and later realizes Will's involvement and obtains his confession. The movie is so much more dramatic. Her interaction with Lydia, too, added heart to the storyline that is completely absent from the book.

In short, whoever wrote the screenplay for that miniseries was a genius for the way he/she was inspired by the storyline of the book but made it vastly better. I liked the book alright (the first half better than the second), but for anyone who's thinking about reading it...I would honestly just recommend watching the movie.

The audiobook was decent, but nothing special. While the narrator did a passable male voice, almost all of the men sounded the same. It was difficult to tell Darcy and the Colonel apart in scenes without dialogue tags.

Title: Rainy Days - An Alternative Journey From Pride And Prejudice To Passion And Love
Author: Lory Lilian
My Review:
This book started out on the right foot, but somewhere in the middle things went off track for me. I'd rate the first half 4 stars and the second half 1-2 stars, so I averaged it out at 2.5 and rounded up to 3 stars, even though I'm not sure I'd ultimately recommend it--which makes me sad, because there were things I did like. By the time I got to the last 20%, though, I started skimming and wishing I was done.

The good:

-The point of variation, where Elizabeth and Darcy get caught in a storm and seek refuge in a cabin together, was nice. And though I genuinely love forced marriage stories, it was a refreshing thing that all involved agreed they could just keep the circumstances secret and not do anything rash.

-Bingley had some truly spirited moments, and I think my favorite was when he was angry with Darcy for getting engaged before him.

-I loved little Becky Gardiner. Often precocious little children in stories are obnoxious, but I couldn't help but be charmed right along with Darcy.

-I liked the new reason for Bingley and Darcy leaving Hertfordshire.

-I liked that Lord and Lady Matlock took some convincing to approve of Elizabeth. So often in stories they are instant approvers despite their position in society. I don't mind it--though, really, it doesn't make sense for Darcy to be so worried about his family's approval if they have always been like that--but it was nice to see a different take here.

The bad:

-The last 20-30% of the story draaaaagged. There were plenty of things happening, but the main storyline was really done already. All of the meaningful conflict was done, so it felt like meaningless (and largely uninteresting) random events. There were tons of details about the ball and wedding plans and other miscellaneous things, but none of it mattered.

-Once Elizabeth and Darcy finally admitted they loved each other, the scenes where the two of them found time alone became more and more frequent, more and more improper, and more and more soppy. They went on and on about how much they were in love, how their souls were intertwined and crap. *barf* I could have lived with it if they weren't so LONG. But at least there was no premarital sex. I do have to give the author credit for that, even if I think many of the scenes were still pushing the bounds of propriety a bit more than I could believe for Elizabeth and Darcy.

-This probably relates to the end of the story dragging, but after awhile the misunderstandings and arguments that Elizabeth and Darcy were having became repetitive and silly. I started to lose respect for both of them because they weren't learning the lessons taught from the last misunderstanding they had. By the time I got to Darcy's story about Miss Alton, I no longer cared.

Title: A Walk in the Meadows at Rosings Park: Three Stories Inspired by Pride and Prejudice
Author: Mary Lydon Simonsen
My Review: This just moved way too fast, even for a short/novella. It seemed silly for Darcy to move as fast as he did, and there really wasn't any conflict. This story is pretty benign but also pretty silly. Not for me.

Title: When Mary Met the Colonel: A Pride and Prejudice Novella
Author: Victoria Kincaid
My Review: This was cute and rather fluffy. I'm not sure what I was expecting, given the length, but I do wish there had been space to let the courtship develop slower. The bits that we get to witness are very nice but just too few. It would have been far more satisfying if they'd fought through more obstacles. I do like the idea of Mary with Colonel Fitzwilliam, though, and I'm a sucker for <spoiler>a wounded hero who's tended by a heroine.</spoiler> More like 3.5 stars, but worth the rounding up for concept and cuteness.

Title: A Peculiar Connection: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: Jan Hahn
My Review:
Wow. This book was intense and beautiful. Elizabeth and Darcy's pain and separation upon learning that they are brother and sister (erroneously, of course) is palpable and felt like a punch to the gut every time they were alone together and came close to voicing their deepest emotions. I loved the journey of this story and the intrigue as various people add pieces to the tale they're unraveling. I think it would be difficult to reread this, because Elizabeth's despair felt so genuine. Even as things resolved nicely, it still felt bittersweet. So many people in the story lost so much--but it was a lovely and well-written story that I'm glad I read.

Now I just need something fluffy and happy for awhile.

Title: Assumed Engagement
Author: Kara Louise
My Review: This was a cute story and an easy read. I would have liked it better if Elizabeth and Darcy had had more actual interaction with one another, but seeing Darcy make strides to improve himself was nice. This is one of the few stories I've read where Darcy's interactions with his "lessers" were influenced by a true thoughts of superiority rather than just shyness. I liked Elizabeth's friendship with Georgiana, but I did think that their familiarity and Elizabeth's change of feelings about Darcy came a bit too quickly. The conflict level was pretty low, with both Lady Catherine and Wickham being barely used at all, which was somewhat disappointing. <spoiler>Also, having Darcy call Elizabeth "Lizbeth" was just silly.</spoiler> But even with the story's faults, I did enjoy my reading.

Title: A Sense of Obligation: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: Rose Fairbanks
My Review: Oh my god, this book was just awful. I honestly can't find anything nice to say about it. I had hoped, when I found the first chapter ridiculous and eye-rolling worthy, that it would at least prove to be humorous, but I only found it to be stupid. All of the characters acted completely unlike themselves (except Mrs. Bennet, but since she's already often ridiculous, that wasn't a stretch) and Darcy's misconceptions about seducing Elizabeth get stretched well beyond belief the farther the story goes. Given that the story starts before they've met Wickham in Meryton, and Elizabeth magically knows to distrust him, the only conflict in the story is wrapped up in Darcy's beliefs and his resultant treatment of Elizabeth. Everyone in Hertfordshire is overcome with lust, <spoiler>even Jane (*eye roll*) and Caroline Bingley...who is lusty for Mr. Collins???? </spoiler>*triple eye roll* I skimmed as quickly as I could to the end, just in case things improved, but what a waste of time and $7.99 + tax. I can't believe I paid full price for this.

Title: The Longbourn Will: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: Carolyn Whyte
My Review:
I really, really enjoyed this one. I have no idea how accurate the legalities are, but pft. It was an intriguing concept to not only change the circumstances around the entail of Longbourn and also to reverse the choices made by characters in several key places. I found it was most interesting that even with altering those decisions, many of the key plot points stayed much the same--only slightly out of order or with a twist. There were plenty of changes, too, though--particularly with Wickham's plotline--and I had fun seeing them play out.

I don't have any serious complaints, but having Elizabeth receive four separate proposals seemed a little outlandish. The tension achieved between Darcy and his cousin with Colonel Fitzwilliam declared he wanted Elizabeth was decent, but I felt it was still a little bit of a stretch. I can see the Colonel liking her, but for him to pursue her so doggedly when Darcy had prior claim seemed like a bit much.

I liked how Mary's plotline resolved, the changes to the Lydia/Wickham plotline, and definitely Elizabeth and Darcy. They had a few little improper moments together, but it was never too much for me. Maybe Elizabeth decided she was in love with him a little faster than I might have expected, but it was nothing egregious. If anything, I think I would have liked to see more of the two of them alone together.

Title: Emma
Author: Jane Austen
My Review:
I'm actually surprised at the strong feelings I had about this book as I read it. I've seen all three of the Emma movies of the last twenty years or so, plus Clueless, so I'm plenty familiar with the story. I can't really say that the book added much as far as the plot itself, but characters gained depth that I didn't expect--some for the better and some for the worse.

I hated Emma (the character) for the first 1/3 of the book. I'd always found her a bit annoying in the movies, but her arrogance and snobbish tendencies were magnified in the text. I think if I wasn't familiar with the story and her path of reformation, I would have put the book down early on. I couldn't identify with her on any level, and that made me not care what happened to her. Those feelings did shift, though, as Emma herself matured. By the time Mr. Elton proposed and she realized the folly of pushing Harriet toward him, I had started to come around to her. I still didn't love her, but I could appreciate her more.

I liked Mr. Knightley, but it's hard to say whether that was due to liking him in the movies--and thus imputing that feeling on the book--or if the text actually made him likeable. I enjoyed his conversations with Emma, both playful and serious. He's a worthy sort of hero, though there was a point where I wondered why in the world he would want Emma at all. I wonder if there's any fan fiction where he hooks up with someone from Pride and Prejudice.... *innocent smile*

The big thing that the movies couldn't do that I loved in the book was provide some sly hints around Frank Churchill and Jane Fairfax's attachment. The movies either skip it so much that I was flabbergasted at the storyline or they hit you over the head with it. It was fascinating to see the dropped hints and understand the motivation behind Frank's actions. It did make me dislike Frank a great deal more than I did in watching the movies. He's a jerk, straight up, and I felt really bad for Jane--both for Frank's treatment of her and Emma's automatic dislike. I can't even blame her for forgiving him, because it's still a preferable situation to being a governess, but I wish she had a third option. (Maybe she could join Mr. Knightley in his fan fic. Though not paired up with him, because I'm not sure they would be suitable.)

I loved Mr. Woodhouse's eccentricities, and WOW the Gwyneth Paltrow version wasn't exaggerating Miss Bates. If anything, she was still downplayed for the sake of screen time. John Knightley gets skimmed over in most of the movies, and seeing his interaction with the family and contrasting him with George was interesting. Likewise, I liked the contrast of Isabella with Emma. The two brothers and two sisters make pretty fitting pairs.

I listened to the audio version narrated by Juliet Stevenson, and it was wonderful. Highly recommended.

Title: Graded French Reader: Première Etape (French Edition)
Author: Camille Bauer

Title: The Ghost and the Goth (The Ghost and the Goth, #1)
Author: Stacey Kade

Title: Falling for Mr. Darcy
Author: KaraLynne Mackrory
My Review: There were parts of this that were a bit silly, sometimes people seemed out of character (particularly Darcy), and the plot went some fairly outlandish directions (particularly with Wickham's plot). It was a fun read, though, once I accepted its idiosyncrasies. Not my favorite by this author, but worth reading.

Title: Engaged to Mr Darcy
Author: E. Bradshaw
My Review: This probably would have been four stars, except there was a lot of rehashing what happened in the original and also getting exhaustive amounts of backstory from various characters. I enjoyed getting a peek into the continued courtship if Elizabeth and Darcy, and I always love getting to see more of Colonel Fitzwilliam, Georgiana, and Bingley, but sometimes the sidebars were excessive. I skimmed through most of those parts and it was worth sticking with for the good bits.

Title: Heiress to Longbourn: A Pride & Prejudice Variation Short Story
Author: Renata McMann
My Review: I enjoyed the concept of this story, and the way the Lydia/Wickham story came out was interesting. However, I would have liked a bit more substance to Elizabeth and Darcy's story, and there were some distracting typos. I would have been annoyed if I paid for it rather than getting it with kindle unlimited.

Title: Suddenly Mrs. Darcy: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: Jenetta James
My Review: This was a nice take on P&P. My biggest complaint was that a lot of it felt summarized rather than shown. Not all of it, certainly, but I liked the interactions between the characters enough that I wish there had been more of it. I enjoyed seeing Elizabeth take control of her duties as the mistress of Pemberley and yet be rather insecure about her relationship. I liked her relationship with her maid. And of course the build of the romance was nice.

Title: A Pemberley Bride
Author: Helen Stone
My Review: This was sometimes overly fluffy and often felt rushed, but it was cute nonetheless. Caroline Bingley is always enjoyable as the main antagonist, even if she was a bit over the top.

Title: Expecting His Proposal
Author: P.O. Dixon
My Review: I'm not overly fond of short stories in general, but this was decent enough. It was jarring to start the story with Elizabeth being favorable to Darcy's proposal due to things that changed at a much earlier point in the tale, all of screen. I did enjoy seeing how Elizabeth overcame the revelation about Darcy's interference with Jane and Bingley, given that her feelings were already engaged...but overall it wasn't enough that I loved the story. It was okay, though.

Title: Snow Storms & Kissing Games: A Pride & Prejudice Novella Variation
Author: Sarah   Johnson
My Review: This was a silly bit of fluffery with overly modern language and sensibilities. Nothing overly awful or offensive, but mostly just made me roll my eyes.

Title: Rumour and Reprisal (A Pride and Prejudice)
Author: Joanna Ramsay
My Review: This book was disappointing. The story felt rushed and summarized. The dialogue was often stilted and awkward. Favorite characters were present, but they lacked soul. The situation that Elizabeth and Darcy faced felt contrived, and neither of them had to do any real work or grow as characters to solve the problem, so I found I didn't much care about their resolution.

Title: The Last Waltz: . . . another pride and prejudice journey of love
Author: Pat Santarsiero
My Review:
This is more like 3.5 stars, but I rounded up because the things I liked ultimately outweighed the the things that were mildly off putting.

There was a severe overuse of ellipses, even in the book title itself, and also some strange uses of italics in dialogue that seemed to emphasize words oddly. There were a few places where the story skipped more time than felt natural, particularly after Elizabeth left Lambton to return to Longbourn--the transition was so abrupt that I had to reread to make sure I hadn't missed anything. And some of the liberties that Darcy takes with Elizabeth seemed out of character and were hard for me to stomach. Especially when he was kissing the scars on her leg fairly early in their acquaintance.

Even given all of that, though, I enjoyed this story a great deal. I was intrigued by this version of Elizabeth with somewhat crushed spirits, and it made for a partial role reversal with Darcy being the one to draw her out. I do find it rather far-fetched how it came about in the first place, but the concept made that worth overlooking. I enjoyed how many of the situations and conversations from the original still took place, but with alterations. I especially loved the change to her conversation with Colonel Fitzwilliam that originally revealed Darcy's separation of Jane and Bingley. I wish Elizabeth's friendship with Georgiana had gotten some more development (it felt already fully formed before it came onscreen), but it was nice to see anyway.

Overall a nice story that I would recommend.

Title: Steady to His Purpose: A Variation of Pride and Prejudice
Author: Cassandra B. Leigh
My Review:
I enjoyed the concept of this story, where the forced marriage through compromise was achieved largely through the gossip of servants and townspeople. It didn't really affect Elizabeth and Darcy's relationship, though, so other than misunderstandings about their true feelings for one another, there wasn't much conflict in the story. After a while, it felt like a series of interconnected scenes about their married life, but there wasn't any real progress. Sure, Elizabeth came to understand that Darcy truly loved her, but given how content she was 90% of the time, it didn't feel like any big change. The last 25%, in particular, was excessive and unnecessary--particularly the Twelfth Night party.

There was some weird formatting in this story that could have used a good editor to clean up, with spacing and centering and italics that weren't always properly placed. There was also an excessive amount of quoting and footnoting, which was distracting. I find that sort of thing, frankly, boring, and I skimmed it or skipped it whenever it occurred, especially after I realized it was going to be frequently repeated.

Title: Apprehension and Desire: A Tale of Pride and Prejudice
Author: Ola Wegner
My Review:
More like 2.5 stars, but I rounded down because of the premarital sex and for how lewd and graphic the sex and not-quite-sex parts were. I've read plenty of books with Elizabeth and Darcy sexytimes (post marriage, preferably, because I can't imagine either of them departing from propriety so much), and even the more descriptive ones at least try to keep with an appropriate tone for these characters. Darcy, to me, was completely out of character here.

But I did like the concept for this story. I liked Elizabeth seeing/hearing about people she knew in reduced circumstances and that pushing her to accept Mr. Darcy's proposal. I liked that Darcy was truly arrogant in many circumstances and had to overcome it. At a certain point, though, there wasn't really much conflict and story left. There were some interconnected events related to Darcy's cousin, the viscount, and to Jane, but Elizabeth and Darcy's plot was pretty much resolved. It would have been nice if those storylines could have offered something to the main plot rather than being tacked on.

This was an easy enough read, but overall I didn't come out of it completely satisfied.

Title: Mr. Darcy's Struggle: A Pride & Prejudice Variation
Author: Martine J. Roberts
My Review:
Mr. Darcy's Struggle was my struggle...to finish reading it. If I hadn't been listening to read-aloud on my kindle while working, I probably would have DNF'ed it and picked something else. But no wi-fi, so I turned the reading speed faster and soldiered on. I hate being this harsh on a book, but I honestly can't find anything that I liked about it, other than the fact that Elizabeth and Darcy were characters.

1) I normally love forced marriage books, and I'm even willing to forgive some of the totally ridiculous setups I've seen to make this happen. But really? I can't imagine Elizabeth refusing Darcy's proposal and then suddenly deciding she wants to get into some serious kissing. And that's assuming I can even accept that Darcy would kiss her to begin with. And then Darcy tells Mr. Collins they're engaged and Elizabeth just...goes with it?

2) Elizabeth gives in way too easily to the whole thing. I can understand her acquiescing because it's what makes sense, but I wouldn't expect her to be so content about it, especially before any of her issues with Darcy have been resolved. It's like she just forgets that all the things that made her refuse him were even important. Her opinion of Darcy softens way too much before she knows the truth about Wickham.

3) And the lusty kissing just keeps happening! This is not the Elizabeth and Darcy I know. I kept rolling my eyes every time it happened, which is probably not the reaction best for enjoyment of the story.

4) The story is non-linear in quite a few places, with frequent, unnecessary flashbacks to scenes that happened only recently. Why not just show the scenes in order?

5) She could recognize the sparks of sexual chemistry between them. "She" being Georgiana...on the day she meets Elizabeth. Just no.

6) Darcy at one point thinks of Lydia as a "foolish chit." Now, I don't deny that's what she is, but Darcy is far too refined to use that kind of language, even in his head. It was such a small thing, but it really bugged me.

7) What was the point of Mr. Bennet suddenly revealing that he's actually richer than he lets everyone know and that now Elizabeth has 3000 pounds if she needs to leave Darcy? That whole scene meant nothing in the context of the story, so why?

8) Colonel Fitzwilliam proposing to Elizabeth at the ball came out of freaking nowhere. Given that it's teased in the book description, I would have expected it to have some significant build-up (and happen much earlier) and for it to do something other than put an artificial stumbling block between Elizabeth and Darcy. Instead, it caused Darcy to act like an idiot and made me quickly skim the last 15% of the story. But no worries! Elizabeth can just read Darcy's diary, plot with the servants, and then seduce him and it's all better. *eye roll*

Title: Haunting Mr. Darcy - A Spirited Courtship
Author: KaraLynne Mackrory
My Review: This book was cute and fluffy. Sometimes a little too fluffy, but that's probably a matter of preference. The concept was interesting and fun. I would have liked for Elizabeth to have taken a little longer to come around to her love for Darcy, but the circumstances of the story didn't really allow for it. The conflict was fairly low, and even the Wickham storyline got almost entirely brushed over, so I admit I was doing a quasi-skim at times to get to the next major events. Enjoyable for a few hours entertainment, but not something I imagine myself ever rereading.

Title: Mr. Darcy's Persistent Pursuit: A Pride and Prejudice Variation (Longbourn Unexpected Book 1)
Author: Elaine Owen
My Review: 3.5 stars, rounded up. This was one of those fairly low conflict variations, but I found the circumstances and plot interesting enough that I didn't mind. I was happy that at least it took Elizabeth longer than a week to decide she loved Darcy. I think my favorite thing was the variation on the Lydia and Wickham plot, and now I remember this is the book with the Lydia sequel. I never thought I'd be tempted to read a book about Lydia, but I plan to give this one a try.

Title: The Resolute Suitor: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: Don H. Miller
My Review:
I started out with high hopes for this book, and the general progression of the plot wherein Elizabeth decides she loves Darcy was fine. The addition of the Gardiners' book and tea shop was interesting, though I'm not sure how accurate. However, I had three major problems that made me rate this book rather low.

1) It was very repetitive. There were long (loooooong) sections of dialogue where the characters discussed scenes that we'd already witnessed, going once again through all of the particulars. Sometimes there were even discussions where they recapped other discussions that had recapped other scenes. I've complained about books that summarize too much, but this book could have benefited from losing huge chunks of dialogue.

2) There were too many new characters--primarily due to Elizabeth's introduction into the ton--that had very little to really do in the story. It was hard to keep track of them, and after awhile I stopped trying. I can't remember which was Colonel Fitzwilliam's sister and which was one of the women Lady Matlock was encouraging Darcy to court, because their roles were pretty much inconsequential. (And, as a side complaint, I don't even really see the point of Elizabeth coming out in the ton anyway. It didn't really change anything about the story other than giving her an opportunity to introduce her sisters to that society. See #3.).

3) The second half of the story wasn't really about Elizabeth and Darcy at all. They were only background players while we witnessed the pursuits of Elizabeth's sisters, particularly Mary. I don't mind stories where we get to see more of them, but not at the expense of the couple I really care about. It either needed to be worked into the story about Elizabeth and Darcy, or it should have been its own novel.

Title: Sketching Mr. Darcy: A Pride and Prejudice Alternative Journey
Author: Lory Lilian
My Review: I probably would have put this as a 4-star book except for the fight that Elizabeth and Darcy have that I assume was the substitute--given the variation--for the proposal argument. The things they said to each other were so distasteful that I lost too much respect for Darcy to continue loving him as much as I wanted. Lady Stafford also bordered on ridiculous many times, but I could have overlooked her if not for the other. Otherwise,  this variation was fine. I really liked the way they ended up married. It was probably the most reasonable and least silly version of Elizabeth being compromised that I've seen. I liked how even Darcy didn't rush into it. Too many times he's shown as a besotted fool very early on, and this was a good example of realistic restraint on his part.

Title: Even More Ardently
Author: Caitlin  Williams
My Review: This was a cute short story. I liked getting to live in that particular P&P world for a little longer, and seeing how Elizabeth and Darcy interact after a year of marriage was nice.

Title: Earning Darcy's Trust
Author: Jennifer Joy
My Review:
I'm not sure that I've ever read a variation that had Georgiana come along to Netherfield at the beginning--only a few where she came to visit later--so I really enjoyed that aspect of this story. Anytime Georgiana can be done well and have more screen time, I'm game. I was a little afraid at the beginning that she was going to be a brat and that I wouldn't like her, but I found that it was realistic of a 15-year-old girl who fancied herself in love, and she became a much more likeable character quickly enough.

The point of divergence did require some pretty extensive backstory (or it had it, anyway...not sure it actually required so much), and I felt like it bogged down the beginning of the story. The Bingley-Darcy party didn't show up in Hertfordshire until 29%, and I found myself watching my progress bar and wondering if the book would have enough time left to do the rest of the needed story justice. Darcy and Elizabeth didn't start off on terms quite so contentious as in the original, so that helped pave the way for a quicker resolution--but it also means the story lost some of the best tension. The blackmail plot was decent, but it resolved far too quickly (especially the part involving Caroline and Louisa). I did appreciate, though, that much of the resolution was accomplished because of Darcy learning to trust the right people (especially Georgiana) rather than taking all of the responsibility on himself, so it would have been that much better if there had been a few more twists and turns before it was solved.

Title: The Second Mrs. Darcy: A Pride & Prejudice Variation Novella
Author: Renata McMann
My Review: This wasn't a novella so much as it was a summary of a novel. There were interesting concepts with uninteresting execution. The characters had no growth. I felt like the story tried to manipulate my emotions the way Anne manipulated Darcy, but I wasn't having it. I did like the idea of Elizabeth being invited to Pemberley by Anne and much of the circumstances that followed, but the story had no heart.  Too many things were rushed even beyond summary level, like <spoiler>Elizabeth's insistence on Kate's impromptu wet nurse, Elizabeth's acceptance of Darcy's second proposal, Bingley's proposal, Georgiana's husband's death</spoiler>--they all pushed the plot along a path, but in a way that felt contrived. Elizabeth and Darcy's love didn't feel earned.

Title: Mr Darcy's Reluctant Bride: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: Freya Scott
My Review:
I actually paid full price for this on amazon, and now I wish I hadn't. I was suckered in by my favorite point of variation--where Elizabeth and Darcy are forced to marry before they know one another well enough--but this version was ridiculous. My complaints are as follows:

1) There was no mention whatsoever of Wickham and Elizabeth's opinion of Mr. Darcy being swayed by Wickham's lies, despite the hasty engagement happening during the Netherfield ball. I did a search for "Wickham," and he's literally never mentioned. What's the point of throwing the two of them together at that point if there's no Wickham to deal with? Was that some unmentioned point of variation? Maybe he never existed? If so, it only served to make the story boring.

2) The romance between Charlotte Lucas and Colonel Fitzwilliam was completely forced. They spoke all of two sentences to one another, and suddenly Elizabeth is wanting to set them up. And then, conveniently, he comes into an inheritance so that he can marry her. It was just dumb...which is a shame, because that pairing could be interesting given good storytelling.

3) Elizabeth has no interaction with Georgiana as she starts her life at Pemberley. It's glossed over with some comment that Georgiana was always occupied with Mrs. Annesley and they barely saw one another. Talk about a missed opportunity to improve the story!

4) I can't imagine why Elizabeth fell in love with Darcy. They had almost no interaction with one another, and when they did it was largely negative. I felt like Elizabeth fancied herself in love with him because that's what was supposed to happen in the story and not because it was earned.

5) It was far too short. Maybe it was intended to be short, but if that was the case, it should have been a more focused story and tried to do far less than was promised here. Almost half of it was dedicated to the period up until they got married, when the part that anyone's going to care about is relegated to the other half and barely summarized there. So many opportunities for conflict and character development were lost. The characters didn't have to work for anything.

6) Caroline Bingley just made me roll my eyes, and then right after I would roll my eyes at Elizabeth for falling for her lies. After Caroline lured her upstairs at the ball and locked her in a bedroom, why in the world would Elizabeth automatically believe anything that woman would say? It just made me lose respect for Elizabeth.

7) And okay, REALLY? Elizabeth is hurt and Darcy's first reaction was to loosen her freaking corset? What about the ballroom, oh, ten feet behind him? Couldn't he call for assistance? The Darcy I know would NEVER even partially undress her unless there was no other recourse. There are much better, more believable ways to compromise them and force them to get married.

I would have given it one star, but the writing wasn't awful--just the story itself.

Title: Mr. Darcy to the Rescue: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: Victoria Kincaid
My Review: This variation was decent, but the last 1/3 felt more like a summary, which was disappointing. I would have liked to see a bit more of Darcy and Elizabeth getting to know one another, but at least they did have some decent interactions before she decided she was in love with him, too. I would have liked to see more of Georgiana and Colonel Fitzwilliam, but I did like that Bingley got a little screentime.

Title: One False Step: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: Elaine Owen
My Review: More like 3.5 stars. This was a fairly benign story and entertaining in a quiet way. Elizabeth fell for Darcy way too quickly, so I would have liked to see a bit more struggle there (because that's the best part!). I did enjoy the plotting against them and the way Jane and Bingley's courtship progressed. I also liked the trust that Elizabeth and Darcy built with one another, even if I felt it needed more time to get to that point. Elizabeth had an appropriate level of impertinence, teasing, and bravery, all of which sometimes get forgotten in this sort of story, so bravo there.

Title: A Constant Love: A Pride & Prejudice Continuation
Author: Sophie Turner
My Review:
I decided to give the Pride and Prejudice variations a rest...and read a continuation instead. ;-) Of all the Jane Austen-based books I've read, I've got to say that the style of the prose in this one came as close to the original as I've ever seen. The only thing that was really lacking was a heavy dose of wit and commentary. There were moments, but definitely nothing compared to the source material.

I did struggle a bit on rating this book. I settled on 4 stars mostly because I did enjoy the read, there was nothing offensive about it, and the writing was good. However, my biggest complaint is that the characters didn't really have much growth. There was a tiny bit with Elizabeth and Darcy learning to communicate with one another better, but even that was a relatively small thing. All of the other "conflict" in the story was exterior to the characters and completely out of their control, so they spent the majority of the book being extremely passive. Even given that, though, following their lives for a year-plus was pleasant. Not fun and exciting, exactly, but kind of like watching the rain while wrapped up in a quilt with some hot chocolate. Cozy.

I did highlight a few sections I enjoyed. There were two lines of dialogue that unexpectedly made me laugh aloud, both voiced by Darcy.

First, as Georgiana is asking Darcy not to be angry about her getting an improper letter from her suitor, he says:

"Why does everyone assume I shall always be stern?"

And second, in response to Elizabeth asking him about the painting of her that he commissioned, about which he has refrained commenting to that point:

"It is tolerable, but not so handsome as the original."

She cracks up, of course, as did I.

Title: Fitzwilliam Darcy: An Honourable Man
Author: Brenda J. Webb
My Review:
This book was insane. There were tons of references of people being sent to Bedlam, and I think this book should have gone right along with them. That's not to say it wasn't an entertaining read--because it was--but phew, I had no idea what I was in for. It was well-written, but it could have used a judicious editor, because it was far too long and delved into the personal motivations of far too many characters (especially all the Darcy-chasers of the ton). Weeding it down to only the essentials would have made a stronger book. I'm also pretty sure this could have just as easily been a non-P&P based regency romance novel. At the time the story starts, the characters are so altered from the ones we saw in the novel that it wouldn't have been difficult for them to be different characters altogether (albeit ones with similar backgrounds and personalities). Elizabeth and Darcy were a bit too melodramatic with their declarations of love for my taste, and the villains were rather mustache-twirly...but I still stuck around till the end to see how the train wreck would play out.

I would recommend this as a fun and silly read for anyone who goes in aware of what they're getting.

Title: When They Fall in Love
Author: Mary Lydon Simonsen
My Review: This wasn't an Austen variation about Elizabeth and Darcy--it was an art/history/politics/geography lesson. There was plenty of information about various cities in France and Italy, but almost no story. What story it had was more like a summary of a short period in someone's life rather than an engaging tale that I wanted to experience. Pretty much Darcy shows Elizabeth the sights around Florence and she gives him advice on how to help him keep his daughter from being so clingy. The only conflict was a fairly easily resolved misunderstanding. I skimmed just because I wanted to see if it found some story eventually, but it didn't. Boring. I would have given it 1 star, but the actual prose was well-written, and the info about Florence would have been cool if used elsewhere.

Title: Pirates and Prejudice
Author: Kara Louise
My Review: The concept for this variation was so silly that I didn't know whether I could reasonably expect to like it, and yet so intriguing that I couldn't resist. Sure, Darcy being so devastated by the refusal of his proposal that he'd let himself go and be mistaken as a pirate was far-fetched...but it was just so much fun that I couldn't help enjoying it! For obvious reasons, the story was much different from the original and the usual variations, so it was a little disappointing to miss out seeing more of favorite characters like Georgiana. Still, I enjoyed the story a great deal and would recommend it to anyone who can get past (or get into!) the concept itself.

Title: A Most Civil Proposal
Author: C.P. Odom
My Review:
I debated a bit on whether to rate this book 3 stars or 4 stars. Though I ultimately choose the lower rating, there wasn't anything about this book that was awful, and it's likely that the things that annoyed me probably won't annoy everyone.

First, I'm not fond of variations that make Mr. Bennet the bad guy. Sure, Wickham still featured--to a lesser degree--but the main conflict for Elizabeth is the fact that her father does not give his blessing to her marriage because of willfully believing rumors about how her relationship with Darcy came about. I could have forgiven that if he'd relented after the millionth explanation from someone trustworthy, but he doesn't. He even blatantly ignores the information they give him about Wickham. I get that Mr. Bennet is not the best father in the world, and that his pride and prejudice is somewhat a stand in for what we don't get in Elizabeth and Darcy due to the variation...but it felt forced.

The other annoyance was the epilogue. It wasn't bad, but the melancholy descriptions of bad fates for certain characters and Elizabeth and Darcy thinking on their age and the eventual end of their time together--it just wasn't necessary. Totally ruined the tone of the book for me and left me feeling sort of depressed.

I did like the divergence point, though, with Darcy's rather romantic proposal, and I liked that Elizabeth still didn't give in immediately. I liked seeing more of the Gardiners. I liked how Bingley dealt with Caroline.

Overall, I'd probably read this again and just skip the epilogue.

Title: A Death at Rosings: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: Renata McMann
My Review:
While the concept is a bit far-fetched for me (that Anne would enlist Elizabeth to help manage Rosings, particularly when they've rarely spoken prior), the overall execution and story was enjoyable. I liked that Elizabeth and Darcy didn't immediately resolve their differences just by being in close proximity, even though it was obvious they were both trying. I liked that Bingley got to show some initiative. The resolution of the Wickham storyline was perhaps a bit too neat, but it did go rather differently from the original, and it was nice to see a true variation there.

Really 3.5 stars.

Title: Compromised!: A Pride & Prejudice Variation
Author: J. Dawn King
My Review: This book was enjoyable, though not one of my favorites. Elizabeth and Darcy fell in love faster than I thought was believable, given how early in the story it diverged. The various conflicts came up and were resolved with equal speed and at times too conveniently. Still, there were many bits I enjoyed that I hadn't seen elsewhere before, such as the connection between Mary and Anne. Worth reading if you're looking for quick and light, particularly if you can get it through kindle unlimited (like me) or cheap.

Title: Remembrance of the Past: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: Lory Lilian
My Review:
This was a nice, well-written story, and I enjoyed it a great deal. It was longer than I expected, to the point that it almost felt like multiple stories that were welded together--though not in a discordant way. It almost felt like a book and its immediate sequel that simply hadn't been separated. Just as I thought the story was wrapping up, I'd look down and see that I was only at 40%...or 50%...or 60%. There were so many major conflicts that I kept expecting to be annoyed. I kept expecting the silly misunderstandings to start and drag on just to add to the word count. But they didn't. I think I was most impressed that the conflicts felt like those happening between reasonable, intelligent, clever adults.

Lady Cassandra was nice. I wasn't entirely expecting to like her as much as I did. Colonel Fitzwilliam is always a favorite, and I enjoyed him here, too. Georgiana was handled equally well, and so was Mr. Bennet. In short, all of my favorite secondary characters got the treatment they deserved. Also, I appreciate that someone managed to write a solid variation where Wickham and Lydia actually factored in very little. It was refreshing in a way I hadn't expected.

Overall, highly recommended. I only deducted a star because I'd prefer a slightly edited down version. It could have had a few less conflicts and been a tighter book--and also easier to add to the reread list without serious time commitment!

Title: Oxford Cottage: A Pride and Prejudice Variation (Darcy and... A Pride and Prejudice Variations Collection Book 1)
Author: Leenie Brown

Title: The Subsequent Proposal: A Tale of Pride, Prejudice & Persuasion
Author: Joana Starnes

Title: Shades of Milk and Honey (Glamourist Histories, #1)
Author: Mary Robinette Kowal

Title: Moon Called (Mercy Thompson, #1)
Author: Patricia Briggs
My Review:
Second read - 9/4/15

This is one of those books that I have a hard time reviewing because I like it so much that I can't single out specific pieces to talk about. There's a lot going on in this story and a lot of characters introduced, but they're so distinct and vibrant that I have no trouble keeping up with it all. Also, I'm a sucker for stories where a strong woman is taking care of an injured man. And Mercy gets to do it TWICE.

First read - 4/13/12

Title: Yours Forevermore, Darcy: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: KaraLynne Mackrory
My Review: I liked the concept that Darcy wrote letters to help him deal with his feelings, and also that Elizabeth could accidentally receive them. The story was overall well written, but not a lot happened. I would have liked it better if Elizabeth had been around Darcy more. Her falling in love with him isn't quite so satisfying when they're apart the whole story, and especially without the Lydia conflict.

Title: Longbourn to London: A Pride and Prejudice Variation
Author: Linda Beutler
My Review:
This is really more like 3.5 stars for me, but I rounded up because it's well written and I like what I've read of this author.

I'm not generally put off by racy content, but it always strikes me as a little odd in Jane Austen-based fiction. Mostly personal preference, but I prefer it lighter or not included. I knew going in that this book would have it, though, based on the reviews I read, so I can't complain. My main annoyance was with Darcy's heavy-handed manipulations related to information being passed to Elizabeth. I expected Elizabeth to be a lot angrier when she found out that Darcy had written to her father and purposely kept it from her. The other was that there were a lot of things from the original summarized, I suppose to get the unfamiliar up to speed, but come on. Anyone reading stuff like this who hasn't read the original needs to be dragged outside and beaten with my well-worn copies (yes, plural) of Pride and Prejudice...then given a cup of tea and allowed to take said volume back to the couch for a read.

I liked Lizzy's interactions with Jane, Georgiana, and Aunt Gardiner--they're always among my favorites, and they didn't disappoint here. Oh, and I was so happy that Caroline got set down like she deserved. Even if I was mentally yelling at Elizabeth for not paying more attention to where that letter ended up. Overall, it was entertaining, and not a bad way to pass a few hours when I wasn't looking to be caught up too much by a story. There was a story, but since it mostly boiled down to Elizabeth's expectations for her wedding night, it wasn't too difficult to break my reading into several nights. Sometimes it's nice to find a book I can actually put down--even though that sounds negative, it's really not.

Title: Northanger Abbey
Author: Jane Austen
My Review:
While this book was very different from Pride & Prejudice and Persuasion, and it's never going to overtake those two as my favorite Austen book, I really enjoyed this book. The tone of the narration was entertaining, and I loved how well it played off of Catherine's naivete so well. I think I've heard this referred to as a YA novel before, and I can definitely see why. Catherine grows up before your eyes, making mistakes and teaching herself and being taught until she's shaped into the heroine she was destined to be.

Even as much as I liked it, I was so disappointed that there wasn't more detail given during the proposal (okay, for an Austen novel, I suppose that's not really much of a spoiler...but still) scene. I'm pretty sure there wasn't even one pretty piece of dialogue during the whole description, and given how adorable Henry was every other time he talked, I really wanted it! I guess that's why I have the movie version. (Though, speaking of the movie version...I liked the way the plot flowed in the book better--shocker, I know. Catherine had more time to grow and mature in the book, so she seemed more deserving at the end.)

Highly recommended for fans of the more popular Austen works who want to explore the rest.

PS - My audiobook version was narrated by Nadia May. She was decent. Not as fabulous as the Pride & Prejudice and Persuasion audiobooks that would have long been worn out if they were still in cassette form, but pretty good. Her male voices weren't awful, but Henry was a bit too smarmy and not heroic sounding for me. John Thorpe, on the other hand, is exactly how I would have imagined him. But her version didn't detract from the story, so I'd recommend it.

Title: Partials (Partials, #1)
Author: Dan Wells
My Review:
Wow. This book was a workout. Maybe a triathlon, because it felt like several different parts that flowed seamlessly into the whole. I was listening to the audiobook without much sense of how far into it I was, and I kept thinking, "Okay, now we're at the end." ...and I was always wrong. The story was far more complex than I expected and covered a lot of ground and time.

I liked Kira's attitude, her intelligence, and the fact that she thought decisions through before acting...even if the decisions were ultimately bad or had tough consequences. Even though I'm not always into the science-y bits of sci-fi (I'm totally okay with hand-waving stuff), I really enjoyed all the medical research. It appealed to my Michael Crichton-loving side. It was a lot more in depth than I expected--but well, that was sort of the name of the game with this book.

I only deducted a star because it was pretty slow to start. For the first 1/4 of the book, I wasn't sure if I was going to like it. It wasn't bad. It just hadn't shown me the direction it was going yet, so I couldn't figure out whether to keep going. Thankfully, I had more reason to continue than to stop.

Am I the only one who wanted/wants Kira to hook up with Samm? I remember Dan Wells saying something on Writing Excuses about people not liking Marcus (I'm pretty sure it was him, anyway), and I can see why. I'm curious to see whether he pulls off making him more likable in the next one. I'm really sad that Jayden had to die. It made sense to lose someone from the core group, given all the times they skirted death, but still sad. I liked him. He trusted Kira even somewhat against his own beliefs, and that made him one of the most heroic characters, in my book.

Solid four stars, and I will definitely read the next one.

On the audio narrator: Not my favorite, but decent enough. She managed to make the voices distinctive, even though I found some of them mildly annoying. Her New York accent was good, though.

Title: The Kiss of Deception (The Remnant Chronicles, #1)
Author: Mary E. Pearson

Title: Someone in the House: A Gothic Novel of Desire, Fear, and Unearthly Passion
Author: Barbara Michaels
My Review: Well that was another disappointment. The book was really interesting up until the ending, which I hated. *sigh*

Title: Patriot's Dream
Author: Barbara Michaels
My Review:
Meh. I stuck it out with this book in the hopes that it would turn around and ultimately surprise me with an ending I liked, but no such luck. I wasn't particularly fond of the overabundance of history from the start. I felt like I'd gone back to the boring-est of boring American history lessons from high school or college. Toward the middle, I started to be able to ignore that, because I liked Jonathan's story so much. I, like the main character, wanted to help him and see him be happy and all that. I was intrigued by the twists and turns of his life, and most especially by his friendship with Charles.

But then. Jan chose that asshole Alan (Like she had to choose a husband after two weeks??? I mean, I realize this book was written in the seventies, but damn.), and Jonathan married Mary Beth (Charles' betrothed, that neither of them actually wanted) only to be poisoned by her when he got older to keep him from freeing their slaves on his deathbed. *sigh* That ending made the whole thing not even worth it. It's getting two stars instead of one just because I love Barbara Michaels, and because there were enjoyable bits in the middle.

Title: Blood Bound (Mercy Thompson, #2)
Author: Patricia Briggs
My Review:
GAH I loved this book so much. Like the first in this series, I find it difficult to articulate exactly what I loved about it, because I'd have to pretty much summarize the entire text here. Still, I feel it deserves an attempt.

Mercy has guts, but she's not stupid. Even when she's doing things that might get her killed, she does reasonable things like leaving messages to tell others where she's going, just in case. She's not all-powerful (or even close), but she plays to her strengths and is smart enough to recognize the strengths of others. I love how she knows so much about werewolves, but she can't actually use those advantages herself because she isn't one. I love that she has normal friendships, acquaintances, co-workers, love life issues, and just generally feels like she has a life outside of the main plot of the story. She feels REAL. She has a conscience, but she's willing to make tough decisions to protect people she loves.

Adam and Samuel, of course, deserve mentions here. I'm definitely Team Adam, but I've got a solid soft spot for Samuel, too. His tale about the girl he dated and his aborted child was heart-wrenching, and I wished I could offer up a hug right along with Mercy. I think I'd get annoyed with his depressed attitude if he had too much more book space, though, so the balance in this book was just right. I appreciated that Mercy and Adam didn't jump right into bed together...though it could have happened if Mercy had chosen differently. I like that she truly thinks about those sorts of decisions rather than just being carried away by emotion or hormones. Their conversation at the end was just about as good as any straightforward confession of attraction/love, but it had a lot more character. Mercy isn't playing hard to get--she's just being careful, because there's a lot at stake.

Secondary characters abounded in this book, but they're written in such a way (and narrated in such a way--this audiobook was so well done) that they feel distinctive, and I never had trouble keeping them straight. Even now, I don't have any problems remembering names...which is actually pretty unusual, especially when listening to an audiobook. I love Stefan, and getting to see how different he is through the eyes of his menagerie was really cool. I would have been really peeved if he died. I was also, surprisingly, upset when I thought Ben had died. He was set up starting in book 1 as someone we're not supposed to like, but again, he's so REAL that pulling back the layers reveals a person and not a caricature. I truly felt for him when he was refusing to leave his wolf form at the end, because of eating Daniel. I truly didn't expect the whole additional ending with Mercy going after Andre, but like Stefan indicated, it's the sort of thing I should have expected of her. It was truly satisfying. Even some of the characters who got less screen time (Jesse, Gabriel, Zee, Warren, Kyle, Bran, Darryl) were vibrant and awesome.

The plot was good, too, but it felt so tightly wrapped up in the characters that I almost forgot to mention it separately. The bad guy was suitably creepy and his acts truly horrific. I knew Mercy and friends would figure out how to best him--because duh--but I was dumbfounded about how they would do it all the way up to the end. There were so many pieces to the puzzle that it would have been impossible for me to guess, but everything made perfect sense. Also, Mercy got to save Adam's ass again, which I greatly appreciated. Does that make me a bad person? Hehe.

I've already checked out the audiobook for [b:Iron Kissed|1412138|Iron Kissed (Mercy Thompson, #3)|Patricia Briggs|https://d.gr-assets.com/books/1358229529s/1412138.jpg|1178926] from the library and will be starting it tomorrow morning. My only consolation for the sadness of finishing this book (like all such good books) is that there are plenty more in the series to come.

Title: Grimspace (Sirantha Jax, #1)
Author: Ann Aguirre
My Review:
​I wanted to like this book more than I did. It wasn't bad, but I'm also not sure whether or not I want to continue the series. There were two things that kept me reading: I loved the c​​oncept of grimspace and the connection between the pilot and navigator. I also loved the interaction between Jax and March was fun, and I liked the gradual way their relationship built.

But unfortunately, there was also quite a bit that bugged me.

The characters seemed to connect too quickly. I felt like they'd barely met before they were teasing each other or throwing jabs or whatever. It was too...intimate for strangers. Even after a good deal of time had passed, I didn't really feel like I had enough insight into anyone other than March to get their connection to Jax. I mean, the bounty hunter? They hang out for a few days and suddenly he's the answer to all her problems. Honestly, though, I think a lot of this feeling was due to the first person present tense narration. I pretty much despise that POV, and while I'm willing to give it a chance every once in awhile, it made everything feel too immediate to be the best choice here, IMHO.

The plot was pretty scattered and it was littered with too many characters that weren't around long enough to be as significant as they were probably supposed to be. Loras was underused, especially considering the heavy implications of his situation. Hon had potential, but then he functioned as little more than an inconsistent, mustache twirling baddie. Jax's husband's role was similarly short and only really a plot device. The two clans near the beginning get dropped in as a feeder for what I'm assuming is a series-spanning plot, but none of the interesting bits got probed. Jax spent six weeks babysitting on one planet. What was the point of that? *yawn*

If I hadn't wanted to see what happened with Jax and March, I probably wouldn't have finished it. I almost stopped reading a couple of times, especially in the first half. It really wasn't awful. It was just a combination of the POV and the other things mentioned above that sort of grated. But meh. I reserve the right to give book 2 a try, if I'm in the mood someday.

Title: Touch of Frost (Mythos Academy, #1)
Author: Jennifer Estep
My Review:
9/1/15

Wow. I can't believe it's been almost exactly 3 years since I read this the first time. I was in the mood for something fast-paced and fun, and I also need to catch up on some of the later books in this series, so I figured I'd give it a go again. I enjoyed it just as much and had much of the same impressions. Good series.

9/4/12

I've had my eye on this series for awhile, so when this ebook became available from the library, I jumped on it. It wasn't a perfect book, but I was overall very pleased with it.

I'll start with the things I found annoying:

First, despite being a boarding school where students are apparently constantly in danger of being killed by the Reaper bad guys, the staff does a terrible job of supervising them! This was applicable in several places, both plot-driving and not, but the one that grated on my nerves the most was the constant mention of students having sex in every corner of the school. I saw it mentioned in some of the amazon reviews, but I chalked it up to people being a bit prudish and having different views on what belonged in YA books than I did. But honestly, I didn't like that part either. It wasn't graphic or overly vulgar, just...unnecessary. I mean, Gwen finds used condoms in the library stacks often enough that's important to mention it multiple times? Really?

Second, there was a heavy emphasis on the stereotypical "mean girls" in this book. I mean, I get it. High school stereotypes exist because there's a lot of truth in them. And I did appreciate that not everyone labeled in this category ultimately ended up staying there. But the description of Mythos Academy makes it seem like every. single. girl. is a mean girl, because their families are rich and they look perfect and wear designer clothes--and there's no way a high school could exist where 90% of the girls fit into that clique. It's an elitist clique! Even if all the girls are rich and beautiful, they will find a way to divide even further. Maybe this is just a case of being biased by the protagonist's POV, but it still stuck out to me in not a good way.

Lastly, the mythology is pretty convoluted. I'm no stickler for history, and I'm totally okay with taking both historical and literary concepts/figures and playing with them for the sake of fiction. The concept of throwing all the gods from various cultures/religions into the same Pantheon is a cool one, though not particularly well explained here. It just sort of is.

So now for the good things:
