Gwen's power (psychometry) is subtle and cool. She's sort of a lower class citizen, so to speak, because of it, but I find it way more interesting than her being a regular super warrior. It gives her interesting challenges while also letting her learn things in a way that no one else at the school can. Perfect choice for the main character.

Daphne. Her transition from the opening scene bitch to Gwen's best friend at the end of the story is fun, interesting and actually feels really natural. She offers a great contrast to Gwen, and I definitely will not mind seeing more of her in the sequels.

Mostly, though, I gave this book four stars instead of three because the story had a fast-paced, fun, and logical flow that made for a good read. I didn't want to put it down--and only did because it was after midnight and I had to get up for work at 5:30 the following morning. It was actually a pretty simplistic sort of plot, once you got to the end and saw how it played out, but I don't think it needed too many bells and whistles to be successful. It relied more on fun characters and less on complex plot twists, which is a refreshing change sometimes.

So yeah. Good read. I'm already working on the sequel.

Title: Magic Bites (Kate Daniels, #1)
Author: Ilona Andrews
My Review:
It feels a little unfair to rate this at 3 stars, because I have a feeling my enjoyment of it lost one star because I was listening to the audiobook. The narrator read everything in a breathy and dramatic tone, as if reading a report would be just as intense as being cornered by the scary Big Bad. There was no variation in tone at all, and it was already driving me crazy about 2 minutes into the book. I probably would have stopped listening, except I was dying to read and had no time other than my daily commute.

But the story itself wasn't bad. Kate grated on my nerves a lot of the time, but I was curious about her parentage and her powers. The story dropped a lot of hints and answered almost none of them, and that alone was almost enough for me to want to read the next book. It was really interesting that she used blood magic quite a bit, because that's something that's frowned upon in a lot of stories I've read. The bad guy was sufficiently creepy, but it felt a little out of the blue to me. The clues were all there, but for some reason it felt choppy...or, something. I can't really put it into words.

Crest was lame. Derek was adorable. Curran was sexy, especially when he rescued Kate despite the hefty cost of getting burned really badly. *swoon* I liked that they had a lot of chemistry without any sort of insta-love. All we got was that one kiss before the climactic battle, but I was totally okay with that.

I enjoyed the setting a lot. I loved immersing myself in a world where the characters have to wonder if they're taking the right car across town or if they're going to be stranded when the magic or tech hits. It's sort of post-apocalyptic, but it's also sort of current day, because many things about the world haven't changed.

I will most likely read book 2, though I wasn't into this one enough to actually pay for any more of them yet, so I put in a hold at the library...for the kindle book. No more audiobooks in this series for me, because the same narrator does them all. Bleh. Hopefully Kate's voice will be more appealing when it's made up by my own imagination.

Title: 2,000 to 10,000: How to Write Faster, Write Better, and Write More of What You Love
Author: Rachel Aaron
My Review: I looked at this book awhile ago and put it down, but I decided to pick it back up tonight to see if I could get some external inspiration to get my butt in the chair and writing. Overall, I don't think there's anything revolutionary offered here, but it's not bad information. If you want a short book that really condenses the writing process (or one version of it) into distinct action items, then this is a good place to start. The kindle price (currently 99 cents) is worth that much. If you've gotten to the point where you can put together a workable story outline, but you haven't figured out how to raise your daily word output, then this may help you find areas where you should focus. Probably more like 3.5 stars.

Title: Spell Bound (Hex Hall, #3)
Author: Rachel Hawkins
My Review:
Second read - 9/3/15

Yep. I just reread the whole trilogy in one day. (Not to mention it also being a *work* day....) I'm officially a crazy person, but I couldn't heeeelp it. I love these books!

First read - 3/24/12

Title: Avalon High
Author: Meg Cabot
My Review:
I've read this book before, but I wanted something quick to pass the evening, and this sufficed nicely. It's definitely very fluffy in the typical Meg Cabot way (which I love, by the way, so that's not an insult in the least), and it seems even less substantial compared to some of the books I've read in the last year. Still, it's hard to find fault with a fun, light read like this. It's cute, and it's King Arthur. What can I say? I'll probably read it yet again sometime.

Side note: The worst part is that I've seen the awful Disney adaptation that completely changed the direction of the plot, and that had me all mixed up about what was going to happen. (Though I actually kind of wanted Marco to be a good guy. I felt kind of bad for him. *shrug*)

Title: Mass Effect, Volume 2
Author: Mac Walters
Additional Authors: Jeremy Barlow, Tony  Parker, Matthew Clark, Drew Geraci, Sean Parsons, Garry Brown, Omar Francia, Michael Atiyeh, Michael Heisler, Benjamin Carré, Dave Marshall

Title: Mass Effect: Library Edition, Volume 1
Author: Mac Walters
Additional Authors: Omar Francia, John Jackson Miller, Garry Brown, Jean Diaz, Dave Marshall, Jeremy Barlow, Patrick Weekes, John Dombrow, Chris Staggs, Eduardo Francisco

Title: The Bitter Kingdom (Fire and Thorns, #3)
Author: Rae Carson
My Review:
Oh my goodness. That was fabulous. I was at about the 40% mark for the longest time--not because it was a bad book, but because I desperately didn't want it to be over! I finally sucked it up, and *happy sigh*. Such a good book.

My favorites things were:

1) Hector. He was already swoon-worthy, but there's something about a man who can be kidnapped and mistreated and still kick ass. Not to mention his fierce love and protectiveness for Elisa. Gah, he's lovely.

2) Storm. I didn't think I'd like him, back when he was introduced, but I adore him. His approach and different attitude made for a nice contrast to the others, and yet he was just as loyal and awesome as the others in the end.

3) Do WHAT? Did Elisa REALLY just declare herself EMPRESS?? My jaw might have dropped, but I loved that show of power from her. How far she's come since the first book!

The only thing that seemed a little...underwhelming to me was the way Elisa finished her service. I get that the whole point was she did all the rest on her own, like Hector said, but it was still sort of "..." and I probably wouldn't have cared except for Elisa losing her godstone. Which BOO, but I get that, too. It's fitting, while still sort of sad. I guess it's a good thing the trilogy ends there, because it wouldn't be as interesting to read if she didn't have the magic. The magic system in this world was completely fascinating from start to finish.

Anyway. LOVED this book. LOVED this series.

*On the audiobook - Jennifer Ikeda read the previous two and all of the Elisa POV chapters in this book, and her performance was great. Luis Moreno was decent on Hector's POV chapters, but I didn't love him. Since that was only a small piece of the series, though, I'd still recommend the audiobooks.

Title: Charlotte Collins: A Continuation of Jane Austen's Pride and Prejudice
Author: Jennifer Becton
My Review:
Aug 1, 2015

Second reading was just as enjoyable as the first. I especially loved the proposal scene.

Aug 13, 2012

I really enjoyed this book, especially considering that a) P&P sequels can be pretty hit or miss, and b) Charlotte is not really one of my favorite characters from the original. While it wasn't as engaging as the original, and the misunderstandings and twists weren't as serious or as surprising, it was well-written and entertaining. I would recommend this one for anyone who wants to live in this world just a little longer.

Title: Reader and Raelynx (Twelve Houses, #4)
Author: Sharon Shinn
My Review:
CAMMON RAPPENGRASS!! *giggle*

I freakin' love these books. LOVE THEM. I'm pretty sure that, at this point, the only thing the author could have done to make me love them less is kill some of my favorite characters - and thank god she didn't do that. I love the style, the character voice, the plot...I just can't praise it highly enough. If I could give this series 10 stars, I would.

This was a fitting conclusion to the series. The ending battle was epic and showed off the skills of the various characters appropriately. The villains got what they deserved. I especially loved Tayse getting to chop Halcon Gisseltess in half after the bastard captured Senneth. So satisfying. Senneth pouring so much magic into things at the end that she lost her magic altogether was super intense, and though I hate seeing a beloved character suffer, it was perfect--and particularly that ending with her little puff of flame. Gah. Why can't I have a four more books about these characters?!?!?!

I will probably eventually read [b:Fortune and Fate|2880573|Fortune and Fate (Twelve Houses, #5)|Sharon Shinn|https://d.gr-assets.com/books/1348522956s/2880573.jpg|2906784], the companion book to this series, but I'll have to wait awhile to get Senneth and the gang out of my head first. I don't want that one to be ruined simply because it can't live up to the others without having the same character set.

Title: Romancing the Duke (Castles Ever After, #1)
Author: Tessa Dare
My Review:
This was pure fluff and wish fulfillment, and I enjoyed every minute of it. I'm a sucker for a disfigured/disabled Beauty and the Beast sort of hero, and Ransom was just the right mix of alpha male, sad history, and unexpected sweetness. Izzy was a lot of fun. I liked her spunk, and the whole thing with her father's famous stories and her entourage of fans made her very different from any heroine I've read before. Even the secondary characters were interesting. (Am I the only one who pictured Duncan from Robin Hood: Prince of Thieves?)

The conflict was a bit light, and even the heavier conflict resolved pretty quickly, but I didn't care because the fluffy bits were fun without being saccharine. I have to be in the mood for this sort of read, but this week it really hit the spot.

I think this is the fourth Tessa Dare I've read, and it was another success. I plan to slowly and surely make my way through her entire catalog, because she hasn't failed me yet.

Title: For Peace of Mind: A Pride and Prejudice Variation (Darcy and... A Pride and Prejudice Variations Collection Book 2)
Author: Leenie Brown

Title: Lost in Austen: Create Your Own Jane Austen Adventure
Author: Emma Campbell Webster

Title: The Madness of Lord Ian Mackenzie (Highland Pleasures, #1)
Author: Jennifer Ashley
My Review: I can see why this book came highly recommended, and I definitely enjoyed it. I stayed up way too late to read it all in one sitting. Honestly, though, I didn't read this for the romance. It kept me glued to the page simply because Ian was a fascinating character, and I really liked seeing his development. His brothers were fun in their own ways, but sometimes everyone other than Ian felt larger than life and almost like caricatures rather than characters. Beth was fine, but I felt like she could have been replaced with another woman and the story would have been much the same. Even with those minor criticisms, I really did like the book, and I would recommend it to anyone who wants a good historical romance with just enough of an outlandish story to be fun and different.

Title: The Riverside Shakespeare
Author: William Shakespeare
Additional Authors: Harry Levin, Herschel Baker, Anne Barton, Frank Kermode, Hallett Smith, Marie Edel, Charles H. Shattuck, G. Blakemore Evans

Title: Miss Peregrine's Home for Peculiar Children (Miss Peregrine's Peculiar Children, #1)
Author: Ransom Riggs

Title: Space Junque (Apocalypto, #1)
Author: L.K. Rigel
My Review:
This book was weird. There were tons of characters, lots of acronyms to learn, and a plot that went by at almost whiplash speed.

And yet, somehow, I still really, really enjoyed it.

I picked this up awhile ago. I don't read a lot of sci-fi, but the cover art on its sequel, [b:Spiderwork|9854729|Spiderwork (Apocalypto, #2)|L.K. Rigel|http://photo.goodreads.com/books/1333306382s/9854729.jpg|14745853], drew me in. (Yeah, sad, I know. But it's the truth.) Today I was looking for a shorter read (this is a novella), so I decided to finally give it a try.

And yeah. Wow. It's actually difficult for me to express in words how I felt about this story. It was weird, like I said, but in an interesting and wow-I-wanna-know-what's-going-on sort of way. Just try it. You'll see what I mean.

Title: Jane
Author: April Lindner
My Review: This was a fun modern adaptation of Jane Eyre. It follows the plot of the original almost exactly. While that's all well and good (because I love the original), I still think I would have liked to have seen even just a slight twist somewhere, just to make things interesting. I recommend it so long as you go in knowing you'll get exactly what you expect.

Title: Under the Never Sky (Under the Never Sky, #1)
Author: Veronica Rossi
My Review:
I really enjoyed this book. The characters were different from what you find in a lot of YA, particularly Perry. He had what would be considered adult responsibilities in current day and time, which made for an interesting contrast to Aria's relatively self-centered and superficial youth. Their connection was gradual and very believable.

I liked the Senses a lot. As far as magic systems go, it's pretty subtle, and that made it even more interesting.

My only real complaint was that the end of the book felt rushed. Considering the huge chunk of chapters they spent in other locations (at Delphi, especially), it seemed a little crazy how all of the major revelations at the end were crammed into the last four chapters. It didn't ruin the book for me or anything, but it was noticeable.

Overall rating 3.5 stars but rounded up for kick ass characters.

Title: The Shadow at the Gate  (The Tormay Trilogy, #2)
Author: Christopher Bunn

Title: All Men Are Rogues
Author: Sari Robins
My Review: This book was pretty good. More like 3.5 stars. The hero and heroine were likeable, their challenges were believable, and the resolution was satisfying. I thought the bad guy's goal was a bit silly and out of left field, but the spy intrigue was fun. Nothing that would make me run out and buy all of this author's books, but it was an enjoyable few hours.

Title: Déjà Dead (Temperance Brennan, #1)
Author: Kathy Reichs
My Review:
Not the first time I've read this book, but I still liked it the second (maybe third?) go 'round. The thing that strikes me the most with this series is the personality. Tempe, of course, is very vibrant and real, but so are the other characters--even the ones who are only on the page for a very short period of time. I may start working my way back through these books so I can pick up any I might have missed. I can't remember which I've read, but it definitely won't be a chore to see.

And Andrew Ryan is lovely...but was there ever a question on that? :-)

Title: In Bed with a Highlander (McCabe Trilogy, #1)
Author: Maya Banks
My Review:
I've hit a milestone today in my romance reading: I've officially read a highlander book. There are about a gazillion of them, and this one was highly rated, so I figure it's a good enough place to start. Conclusion? I stand by my pre-reading assumption that this sub-genre is not really my thing. My overall feeling was sort of...meh.

1) The book seemed like it was striving for something complex and interesting, plot-wise, but it never really got there. The bad guy wants Mairin's inheritance, and he'll do some dastardly villain things to get it. I was hoping that at least the murder-attempt plot was going to have an interesting resolution, but it turned out it was just one of the random guards working for the bad guy. *yawn* I wish it had been someone shocking and that his reasons had been interesting, but no.

2) Some of Mairin's attempts to fit in with her new clan were just silly. How in the world did she think that firing the horsemaster was going to please her new husband or the clan? I'm all about female empowerment, but sometimes she was borderline stupid in the things about which she chose to act out.

3) Conflicts resolved too quickly. Each time Mairin and Ewan argued or had a misunderstanding, they made up within a few pages. Now, artificially inflating and extending conflicts is one of my big pet peeves, so I appreciate that it didn't do that...but, like #1 above, it just made the book seem less substantial. Even when the bad guy makes off with Mairin during a convoluted plot to convince people that she's really married to him, I'm pretty sure that Ewan rescued her within a chapter or two.

4) And speaking of that--why in the world did he need to go through said convoluted plot, which involved slowly poisoning the king? If the king was about to be dead, and the co-conspirator cousin was going to take over, then why did he even need Mairin and her dowry? They were already taking over the throne!

Despite the above gripes, there was honestly nothing particularly offensive or annoying about this book. The writing was decent, the characters were decent, and the plot resolved itself in a satisfactory manner. I read it all in one sitting (though mostly because I was simply in the mood to read.) I just expected more.

Title: The Curse Girl
Author: Kate Avery Ellison
My Review:
Reread 3/28/15

I was in the mood for a quick read, and this hit the spot. My only complaint about this story (contradictorily) is that it's not longer!

Originally read 9/1/11

I love a good fairy tale retelling, but they can be hard to find. I tend to read them over and over again to compensate. This one is going on my reread list right next to my collection of Robin McKinley retellings -- which is to say I loved it. I stayed up far too late finishing it.

This is the same tale we all know, but there are just enough twists to it to make it original and interesting. The characters are lovely, from Bee all the way down to the house staff, and it's easy to imagine yourself sitting there with them, trying to figure out how to break that pesky curse.

Highly recommended.

Title: Death Before Swine (Ben Hart Mystery #1)
Author: V.K. Scott
My Review: I won a free copy of this, but it's definitely worth far more than free! I loved this book and especially the main character. Ben Hart is just what I like in an amateur detective and in a narrator. He's an all-around nice guy, well-meaning, and he stumbles into his "job" before he even realizes what's happening. He's a regular guy with regular guy problems...who just happens to be wrapped up in a murder investigation. Combine him with good secondary characters and a well-crafted mystery, and I'm sold! Recommending this one to all my mystery-reading friends, and I can't wait to read the next one.

Title: From the Corner of His Eye
Author: Dean Koontz

Title: Between the Lines (Between the Lines, #1)
Author: Tammara Webber
My Review:
I read this book awhile back and was really surprised by how much I enjoyed it. It's alternating first person and present tense, and at the time I hadn't read much of that--and didn't like it. I'm a little more used to it now, so it rankles a little less, but it's still not my favorite POV. I do enjoy how it's employed here, and it gives the story a nice feeling of immediacy.

This story is a bit about fantasy and wish fulfillment. The main character is a semi-regular girl (a small-time actor) who wins a role in a major motion picture to star next to one of the hottest young actors in the world. What teen girl hasn't fantasized about something at least close to that? Reading about Emma's journey into that world, it's very easy to put yourself in her shoes. She's not an overly engrossing character on her own, but I think it works. Being blander helps makes her more identifiable for the reader.

The other POV character, Reid, is the opposite of bland. He's a bad boy who doesn't have any qualms about being a bad boy. I normally balk at sympathizing at a character like this, but Reid has it tough, despite the outwardly glamorous appearance of his life, and he's just so damned consistent that it makes him feel real. I think if he'd softened too much through the book that I would have liked him less, as crazy as that sounds.

The basic plot is a fairly standard romance sort of thing (and especially the teen-level misunderstandings that drive things), but the framework and the excellent characters made this book feel one step above standard. I really liked it.

This time around, I specifically listened to the audiobook, and the narration was okay. It took me awhile to get used to the narrators, and I didn't feel like the voice acting fell into the background and let me be absorbed by the story. The male narrator, in particular, was pretty horrible at doing opposite-gender voices, and that was annoying. I did eventually stop paying as much attention to the voices and get back into the story, but I think I'll stick with the text next time unless I'm driving or something.

Title: The Girl of Fire and Thorns (Fire and Thorns, #1)
Author: Rae Carson
My Review:
7/22/12

I LOVED this book. It's first person, present tense, which usually sets my teeth on edge, but I forgot about that little detail very quickly. Elisa is a very real sort of heroine with real faults and insecurities, but a desire to be the best she can be. Her desire to be *useful* was the thing that resonated the most with me. She didn't care about being the hero (though she was), but rather about not standing by and watching while others took care of business. She managed to be a princess and a revolutionary without the two roles seeming incongruous at all. The secondary characters were great as well, and I hope to see more of them in the sequel. (Why isn't it out yet????)

6/18/13

Listening to the audio book this time around. The final book of the trilogy is out in August, so it's the perfect time to start my way back through the first two. I'm not sure how it's possible, but I'm enjoying myself even more this time around. I can't speak highly enough of this book.

Finished again, and it still gave me chills. Bravo, Ms. Carson.

Title: Silver Lining
Author: Maggie Osborne
My Review:
I'd read this book before, but it's been over a decade, so I didn't really remember a lot of it. This was actually the first historical romance I ever read, because my mom's best friend challenged me to try it. Not sure what prompted me to want to reread it (since I never really developed a taste for historical western romance, despite now liking other historicals), but I did enjoy it the second time around. Probably more like a 3.5, rounded up.

The concept is a bit far-fetched, but I didn't mind that too much. The characters are people I wouldn't mind knowing in real life, with the exception of our main antagonists (naturally). I think the best part, for me, wasn't even the romance itself. The extended family unit presented here is very strong, and I liked that they had their conflicts, but they obviously loved and supported one another anyway. Like when Max and Wally get into a fist fight over something silly (though obviously about deeper issues), but they work it out like true, loving brothers, and it's fine.

Not really much else to say about it. If you're looking to suspend your disbelief and pass a pleasant evening living with gold miners and ranchers and a spoiled banker's daughter, then I'd recommend it.

Title: Dragonflight (Dragonriders of Pern, #1)
Author: Anne McCaffrey
My Review:
I honestly wasn't sure what to expect from this book. I've heard good things for a long time, but on my first attempt to read it, I was scared away by the prologue. It was too history-book science-y for me...which I'm sure is probably not an unknown thing for science fiction readers, but I tend to stick on the fantasy side. Thankfully, I gave it another try, and after the prologue this book was far more like fantasy than like science fiction.

I liked Lessa and F'lar and their dragons, and I had a moment of genuine surprise and excitement the first moment I realized that Lessa and Ramoth had jumped back in time. The story did move really fast, though. There were times when it jumped months and years. Necessarily, but it was jarring at first. When Lessa makes her big, bold move at the end, it's over and done in a chapter, despite the enormity of it. I wouldn't call it a negative of the story, exactly, but it did make me feel a bit more distant from the story and characters than I think I would have with a more contained, intimate story.

All that said, I really did enjoy this story, and I plan to read the others in the series.

Title: When Rose Wakes
Author: Christopher Golden
My Review:
This book was alright. The concept was good, and the characters were decent, but it lacked that little extra zing that would have made it something more. It covered a span of only a couple of weeks for the characters to get to know one another, and I just didn't know most of them well enough to care. Rose's dreams were the best part, honestly; I think I would have liked to have read a regular Sleeping Beauty story done with this set rather than the modern day transposition of it.

Not an awful way to spend a couple of hours if you're really into fairy tale retellings, but it wasn't my favorite.

Title: Thorn
Author: Intisar Khanani
My Review: I just noticed that this book was still on my "to read" shelf...but I totally read it a few months ago. Huh. It's been long enough that I don't remember it particularly well. I do remember that I enjoyed it, but it wasn't my favorite. Not something I'm dying to reread, but I passed an enjoyable evening with it.

Title: Fairy Tale Flirts 5 Romantic Short Stories
Author: Lisa  Scott
My Review:
I will start by saying that occasionally I like to read short fiction, just for a change of pace, but I don't prefer it. As such, I'm probably not the target audience for these.

These stories were just okay. I bought it because I'm a sucker for fairy tale retellings, but I was disappointed in the result here. Some of the stories felt forced into the confines of their inspiration tales. My favorites, by far, were Belle and Snow, and I enjoyed those a great deal. I didn't care at all for Cindi or Red, and Goldie was just okay. The five together averaged out to the 3-star rating.

Title: The Door to Canellin (Gatehouse #1)
Author: E.H. Jones
My Review:
THE DOOR TO CANELLIN is one of my favorite sorts of fantasy novels--where someone from the "real" world gets sucked into a fantasy world for his/her own set of adventures. In that regard, this novel doesn't disappoint. Both Wes and his dad, Ryan, have some exciting adventures and learn a lot along the way. The narrative and ending were fun and satisfying. There's just enough magic and sword-fighting for my taste, not so much that it gets old. Those things are important, but they don't overshadow the interpersonal elements.

My only real "complaint" (and I use the word rather loosely) was the changing in point of view between Wes and Ryan. I understood why it was needed, and I liked each individually, but in some ways it was like jumping between a young adult novel and a "grown-up" novel. It couldn't quite be either, which was jarring. The feeling eased up about halfway through, when it was obvious that they were each on their own journeys (and probably because I got used to it), but it was enough that I almost thought about putting it down. I'm glad now that I didn't.

Looking forward to seeing what the sequel will have to offer.

My Review: I finished this in one night. It kept me on the edge of my seat in the way that any good ghost story should. It felt like the teen version of one of those old Betty Ren Wright stories ([b:The Dollhouse Murders|750035|The Dollhouse Murders|Betty Ren Wright|http://photo.goodreads.com/books/1178019676s/750035.jpg|736173] springs to mind) I used to read, which is definitely a good thing. I was creeped out enough that I had a hard time going to bed with everyone else in the house already asleep, and the lights all out!

Title: Venetia
Author: Georgette Heyer
My Review:
I've been meaning to get around to reading a Georgette Heyer novel for awhile, and I'm pleased to say that my first (carefully researched) foray was a very good one. I loved how self-sufficient Venetia was and how those who loved her rallied around her. I loved Aubrey in all his selfish glory, and particularly at the end when he in no uncertain terms said he thought Venetia and Damerel should get married and that he'd been saying so for a long time, like everyone was completely ridiculous for not seeing it also--and indeed, they were! And Damerel himself was all the more admirable for the way he protected Venetia, even from himself. It was adorable how he freaked out when she showed up at his house at the end, and was determined that he would go stay at an inn to protect her propriety.

I will definitely be reading another Heyer novel.

Title: Everything I Know about Love I Learned from Romance Novels
Author: Sarah Wendell

Title: Sleepless
Author: Thomas Fahy
Publisher: Simon & Schuster Books for Young Readers
My Review:
2.5 stars rounded up because the actual writing was pretty good. This was a fast read (about 3 hours), and I can't say I was bored or terribly annoyed reading it. It was just a little too difficult for me to buy into the concept and some of the things that the characters did. Maybe if it had been a book twice this length, with a lot more time to make me understand and believe the whys of it all, but meh.

The concept was neat: somehow people are being made to sleepwalk and "dream" terrible things, and somehow people are dying because of it. I also liked the characters well enough. There were a few times when I rolled my eyes at the typical teen stereotypes being used (cheerleader, druggie, popular kids, etc.), but I think it did a decent enough job showing characters who broke those stereotypes that I could forgive it. The connection between Emma and Jake was well done, too. It covered a pretty short period of time (a week, I think?), so they were already on the cusp of a relationship when it started, but the build-up was still good.

But reeeeally? When was the last time you went on a school-related summer trip to New Orleans to build homes for Habit for Humanity after Hurricane Katrina, witnessed the aftermath of a murder, and then DECIDED IT WAS OKAY TO PROMISE NOT TO TELL ANYONE????? And what kind of teacher would ask that of his students? I don't care how justified the murder might or might not have been (And sure, they're told things that indicated the guy deserved it, but what happened to proof? You're going to assume this random lady is telling you the truth just because you feel sorry that she lost her house in a hurricane?)...a responsible adult wouldn't put that kind of burden on teenagers. I also felt that the climax was pretty weak. Sure, it was nice that the person behind it all wasn't as easily identified as the facts made it seem, but come on. Making everyone sleepwalk and kill people seems pretty extreme for a girl who's trying to prove to her teacher-lover that he's right about the things that are tearing society apart (a list which, apparently, does not include teachers sleeping with their high school students). I did like that Jake was able to prove his worth by refusing to hurt Emma, but the rest of the scene was pretty weak.

Glad I checked this one off the list of books-I-own-but-haven't-gotten-around-to-reading, but it's not going on the keeper shelf.

Author: J.K. Rowling

Title: Slightly Married (Bedwyn Saga, #1)
Author: Mary Balogh
My Review:
I really enjoyed this book. There was just enough intrigue with Cousin Cecil's plotline to shake up the general relationship stuff and make it a lot of fun. The book alternated between locations several times, and that made things more interesting, too. It was nice to see Eve and Aidan in different places and how those places changed their actions and how they interacted.

The prose was lovely, and I felt really drawn into the world. I also really enjoyed that Eve was not your typical regency heroine, but she didn't feel unrealistic, either. (Maybe she was unrealistic, but I don't claim to be a history scholar of any sort, so whatever.)

I would definitely recommend this to regency historical romance fans, and I am going to add this series to my "to read" list for sure.

Title: Exclusively Yours (Kowalski Family, #1)
Author: Shannon Stacey
My Review: I've had this in my kindle library for awhile (I think I got it cheap or free), so I decided to give it a read. It was pretty good, just not quite substantial enough for me. I keep trying these quieter stories that have no real conflict outside of the relationship itself, and I've yet to find one that has satisfied me. I need some suspense subplots or something. But again, it was good. Well-written, the characters were nice, and I'd recommend it to anyone who wanted a plain ole contemporary romance.

Title: A Lady by Midnight (Spindle Cove, #3)
Author: Tessa Dare
My Review: I think this might be my favorite of the series so far. Granted, they're all some subtle variance of 4 stars, but hey. It's been a little while since I read the others, so it's not easy for me to articulate the difference. Maybe I'm just a sucker for a strong, quiet hero. Thorne is definitely that. And there's a bit of a fairy tale sort of element to it all, which is a nice touch. I'm very pleased that I'm 3 out of 3 with Tessa Dare and this series, and I definitely plan to continue.

Title: Blaze of Winter (Star Harbor, #2)
Author: Elisabeth Barrett
My Review: I can't say this book was awful, but mostly it was just silly. The hero and heroine had some serious insta-love going on. Even by the end, I didn't feel like they knew each other or had reason to be in love. Both of their so-called issues were way over played, the quasi-mystery was lame, and I swear I was going to laugh myself to death if he thought of her hair as "titian" one more time. The building blocks of a good story were there, but it just didn't come together for me.

Title: The Shambling Guide to New York City (The Shambling Guides Book 1)
Author: Mur Lafferty

Title: Flat-Out Matt (Flat-Out Love, #1.5)
Author: Jessica Park
My Review: There's a bit too much rehash in this novella-ish thing for me to rate it much higher than three stars, because I feel a bit cheated for paying $3 for it. Damn [b:Flat-Out Love|11096647|Flat-Out Love (Flat-Out Love, #1)|Jessica Park|https://d202m5krfqbpi5.cloudfront.net/books/1361307724s/11096647.jpg|16018629] for drawing me to it. There were two chapters of fully new material, and they were good, but not earth shattering. Worth reading if you really enjoyed the first one, but I would recommend getting a friend to loan it to you rather than paying for a few thousand words.

Title: Flat-Out Love (Flat-Out Love, #1)
Author: Jessica Park
My Review: I'm trying to digest this book and come up with a thoughtful review, but I've found that I don't have a lot to say about it. I enjoyed it a great deal, but I have to admit that the whole underlying concept was a bit...heavy-handed. I definitely had a *gasp* moment when I realized what the family's secret was, and I was totally on board with the chapters that followed. However, in thinking back, the whole setup just seems a little ridiculous. Mostly, it's the fact that the whole family <spoiler>kept up the ridiculous charade that Finn was alive. I'm sure there are families out there that are just that messed up...but I guess this is a case where truth is stranger than fiction.</spoiler> I liked it, though, and if you buy into the setup (I'm pretty good at suspending my disbelief, even when my brain resists.), the relationships between Julie and the various characters is superb.

Title: Suddenly You
Author: Lisa Kleypas
My Review: I'm beginning to notice a trend, now that I've read enough historical romance to be able to see such things, and it's that I tend to enjoy the stories that have a hefty non-fluff plotline to balance out the fluff. This book was well-written, and I enjoyed the main characters, but the story was only about the characters' relationship. The only real conflict is when they'll finally admit their feelings for one another. Now, granted, it also avoided some of the things that I don't like in romance novels, and I appreciate that there was no attempt to fill the plot with artificially plumped up conflict where there really was none. This was a decent enough story, but not quite industrious enough for my taste.

Title: Making Faces
Author: Amy Harmon
My Review:
Egads that was emotionally wrenching. I knew it would be, just from reading the description. I usually avoid books like this, because tear jerkers are not really my thing. For some reason, though, this one drew my notice, and I started reading it without really thinking about the consequences.

I managed not to cry. Barely. And probably only because my husband was in the room, and I didn't want to have to break the story tension while I explained myself. I resisted pretty easily until Fern and Ambrose were in Bailey's room, after he died, and reading Bailey's list. That very nearly got me.

Overall, this was a great book. It was very easy to be absorbed by the characters and their hardships and triumphs and sadness and heartache and happiness. It felt very real, as if I was just peeking through a window and watching life unfold in the town. I usually prefer something a bit more fantastic (in the make-believe sense), but it was a nice change to exist in a world like my own for awhile. In reality, I haven't experienced the same things as the characters (and hopefully never will), but for the four hours or so that it took for me to finish this book, I felt like I did.

And as far as the new adult genre goes, I found it very refreshing that there was no sex at all. I have no issues with graphic sex in books, but so often it seems like this genre is too hyper-focused on the physical elements. This book was about bared souls rather than bared bodies, and I think I liked it all the more for it. Also, I liked that there was a respectful treatment of Christianity without it being preachy. Fern's father is a pastor, and that is not glossed over, but neither is it used as something to manipulate the plot, one way or the other.

I'm not sure I'll ever be able to reread this, but I'm glad I did this once.

Title: The Temporary Wife
Author: Mary Balogh
My Review: This was a fluffy book that took me only a few hours to finish. I enjoyed the setup, the main characters were decent enough, and then ending was good--but things resolved with a nice pretty bow a bit too easily. I mean, I expect a happily ever after, of course, but there were some pretty serious familial issues involved here, and they really needed double the number of pages for them to work out the way they did and have me buy it. Not bad, just perhaps overreaching on the suspension of disbelief.

Title: A Week to Be Wicked (Spindle Cove, #2)
Author: Tessa Dare
My Review: This was a wonderful second book in this series. I was glad that we got a few scenes back in Spindle Cove so that it didn't feel like we'd completely left it behind. Honestly, though, I don't think I would have missed it too badly--because I was so damn entranced by Minerva and Colin's story that I didn't have time to! Colin was somehow rakish and damaged, and still a guy that I wanted to see succeed and get what he wanted. Minerva was nerdy and inquisitive, and I love how there was a definite give and take between them. They taught each other plenty, and it felt like a trade between equals. Like book one, this book did not rely on petty disagreements and misunderstandings to push the story along, and I love Tessa Dare all the more for it. I will definitely be reading the next book in the series.

Title: Lord Dragoner's Wife
Author: Lynn Kerstan
My Review:
3.5 stars

This book was very heavy on the spy business and very light on romance, which is my real reason for putting it in the 3-star range instead of the 4-star range. It was good--well written, likeable characters, interesting plot--but the interaction between Delilah and Dragoner was so minimal that I felt kind of cheated. I didn't believe they were in love by the end. I wanted them to be in love, because I thought they both deserved it, but it was a stretch for me. It was enjoyable, but I'm glad it I got it from the library instead of buying it for $8.

Title: Mr. Darcy, Vampyre
Author: Amanda Grange
My Review: I was drawn in by the idea of this book, but the execution was, frankly, boring. Elizabeth and Darcy's honeymoon is a series of moving to a new place, meeting strange people that Darcy knows (who are, to us, obviously vampires), Elizabeth wondering what's going on and why he's acting weird, Darcy refusing to talk to her...repeat. After about two repetitions, I started skimming more than reading. I was curious to see how things resolved, but that's pretty much the only reason I didn't put it down altogether. Very disappointing.

Title: The Trouble with Mr. Darcy (Darcy Saga #5)
Author: Sharon Lathan

Title: The Perfect Bride for Mr. Darcy
Author: Mary Lydon Simonsen

Title: Offside (The Barker Triplets, #1)
Author: Juliana Stone
My Review: I wasn't all that impressed with this book, mostly because not a lot really happened. All of the conflict and tension felt rather flimsy. The only thing really keeping Billie and Logan apart is the lame little secret they're keeping from one another (which, essentially, is the same secret). All of the drama was a constant repetition of the "girl on the guys' team" crap (which was fine to start, but it never built into anything else), the same old arguing between the sisters, and the same lame bully attacking Billie. After awhile, I found myself skimming. It just wasn't that interesting. I did finish it, but mostly because it was short enough that it didn't take long enough that I regret the time.

Title: Heart of the Dragon's Realm
Author: Karalynn Lee
My Review:
This story was okay. My biggest complaint is that it felt rushed, not much happened for the first half, and the world lacked detail. I ended up skimming a lot, because so much of what went on during the early parts of the story felt unnecessary. It may have been a symptom of the length, which was too short to really support a decent subplot that I think it really could have used. Some of the details that were revealed through heavy telling (about the main character's wild tendencies, about the mountain city, about dragons, about the war, etc.) could have been better shown if there was space for supporting story lines. With subplots, the extraneous characters who were introduced could have had purpose, and there would have been greater opportunity to feel the developing relationships rather than being told it was happening.

This probably would have been a 2-star book, because unfulfilled potential tends to get under my skin worse than a genuinely bad book. However, this wasn't poorly written, and the core plot was fine, so I can't in good conscience rate it lower than 3 stars. Still, it could have been fleshed out into a much better book.

Title: Wildefire (Wildefire #1)
Author: Karsten Knight
Publisher: Simon & Schuster Books for Young Readers

Title: Practical Paleo: A Customized Approach to Health and a Whole-Foods Lifestyle
Author: Diane Sanfilippo

Title: Larceny and Old Lace (Den of Antiquity, #1)
Author: Tamar Myers

Title: His Good Opinion: A Mr. Darcy Novel (Brides of Pemberley, #1)
Author: Nancy Kelley
My Review: I was actually really surprised at how much I liked this book. Austen-based fiction can be hit or miss, and especially one that tries to retell one of my favorite novels of all time in such a way that it's not just re-hashing from a different POV. This one succeeded very well. The insight into Mr. Darcy's thought processes and how things played out on his side were entertaining and engrossing. I read well past bedtime two nights in a row! I recommend this one for fans of Pride & Prejudice.

Title: Awake (A Fairytale Trilogy #1)
Author: Jessica  Grey
My Review:
Very cute and fun story. Essentially, it's what would happen if Sleeping Beauty didn't wake up as the original stories said...along with a few other details they got wrong. The characters were solid and I was really rooting for them. My only [minor] complaint was with Nicholas, who was rather underdeveloped and inconsistent. Thankfully, the focus of the story is on the others, and they're great.

It appears that this is the first in a series, and I'm looking forward to reading what comes next for these girls. Grey has introduced a very interesting world that I look forward to spending more time in it.

Title: Epic Fail
Author: Claire LaZebnik
My Review:
Now THIS is what Pride and Prejudice retellings should be. It's endearing, witty, adorable, intelligent . . . I can't sing its praises enough. The characters are great. I love that their names are similar (same first letter), but not the same, thus giving them a more modern feel. I love the way the modern plot was fitted to the old one. Derek is swoon-worthy, and Elise is worthy of her role and lives up to her Austen predecessor. Jules and Chase are cute without being overbearing.

If you love P&P and love teen romances, then you'll love this.

Title: Everneath (Everneath, #1)
Author: Brodi Ashton
My Review:
I struggled with how to rate this book. I can't say that I really enjoyed it. I'd like to qualify that by saying that I read books strictly to be entertained and to escape for awhile, and it's very rare I read something for its literary value, emotion-evoking abilities, or anything else. And overall, this book was pretty depressing. There were no moments where I grinned madly (because things were going so well), only to have it ripped away and then returned by the end.

But, ultimately, I settled on 4 stars, because this book was really well done, even if it wasn't something I would choose to read again. It had 4 things working for it:

1) The writing was nearly flawless. The prose was nice, and the plot flowed like a plot should. There were hints at what was happening, but the author never hits you over the head with it. Information is dribbled in gradually, and sometimes it surprises you. There's a definite build-up, and there's resolution--there's plenty of room for that inevitable sequel, but the "for now" is wrapped up.

2) The *mood* is brilliantly done. You can really feel how Becks feels. You can tell that she's been through some awful things, and her gradual reawakening is palpable. That said, this is probably what killed the book for me. I felt somber the whole time I was reading. Even though it was realistic and well-done, I needed some ups to balance the downs.

3) I cared about all of the characters (Becks and her family, Jack, Jules), or was at least curious to find out what made them tick (Cole).

4) I loved the twist on traditional mythology. Other than the fact that Never too old for YA Books was reading it for May, the mythology was the main reason I kept going. I was super curious to find out more. We still don't have all that information by the end of the book, but it was enough for me to be satisfied with this book.

Will I read the sequel, once it's out? I'm not sure. The end of this one had a more hopeful tone than 99% of the book, so I might be willing to give it a try. I'd probably drop it again if the overbearing depression continues, but this one had enough good elements that it would be worth a couple of chapters to see.

Title: Lola and the Boy Next Door (Anna and the French Kiss, #2)
Author: Stephanie Perkins
My Review:
I already feel guilty for this review and my three stars, and I haven't even written it yet. This book was good, but considering how much I adored [b:Anna and the French Kiss|6936382|Anna and the French Kiss (Anna and the French Kiss, #1)|Stephanie Perkins|https://d202m5krfqbpi5.cloudfront.net/books/1358271931s/6936382.jpg|7168450], it had big shoes to fill, and well.... Let me see if I can break it down.

Things it had working against it:

1) It's not about Anna and Etienne. Now, I realize that they've got their happiness, and a story wouldn't be interesting without more drama--and they totally deserve to stay happy. They actually guest starred in this book a lot more than I expected, but it wasn't the same. It's really difficult to recapture that kind of magic with a new narrator.

2) Even though it's not Anna and Etienne's story, it tries way too hard to be like it. Rather than the guy having a girlfriend, this time it's the girl. *yawn* Max was annoying and boring. I understood why Etienne was with Ellie, but I couldn't understand why Lola was with Max...other than stubbornness and immaturity, which does not make for a good impression of the main character. And in another parallel, the romance is built on a solid friendship--but we don't get to experience the build-up, which was the best part with Anna.

3) The setting. I have nothing against San Francisco, either in general, or as the setting for a story like this. However, when compared to Paris, it just couldn't hold a candle. The descriptions of Paris in [b:Anna and the French Kiss|6936382|Anna and the French Kiss (Anna and the French Kiss, #1)|Stephanie Perkins|https://d202m5krfqbpi5.cloudfront.net/books/1358271931s/6936382.jpg|7168450] were lyrical and dizzying and beautiful, and it really lent itself to the story in a way that SF does not--at least, not in the way it was used here.

Things I generally didn't like:

1) The secondary characters lacked depth. Lola's best friend, for instance. I've even forgotten her name now! The most memorable thing about her is her obsession with Nancy Drew and all things detective, but I didn't feel like the quirk was part of a full person. Calliope was a tad better, but only because we get to see a few different sides of her throughout the story.

2) I liked that Lola and Cricket were friends. I found it incredibly endearing that he not only loved her, but he was man enough to tell her up front. But honestly? Knowing that he loved her and she loved him...it sort of robbed me of the suspense. Obviously, in this sort of story, I know they're going to get together, but the whole fun is seeing how they figure out that they're perfect for one another. There was no challenge to it, and it made the plot roadblocks feel a little artificial and forced.

3) And okay, I hate to have to say this...but I found Lola a little annoying, too. It's hard to articulate why, but sometimes her (purposely) over-the-top personality was just a little much for me. Maybe I just didn't feel like I related at all.

What I did like:

1) Cricket. As I mentioned above, despite the story consequences of it, I liked that he was super honest with her. He was sweet and thoughtful and creative. (Though, now that I think about it...maybe just a little too perfect. *sigh*)

2) Regardless of all my complaints, the story is very well written. It pulled me straight through the plot over two evenings of reading, and I would have finished it the first night if I hadn't had to get up early for work the next morning. As it was, I lost sleep due to reading late. Stephanie Perkins has a talent for making a very personal, small-scope tale come to life.

3) The setup for the 3rd book, [b:Isla and the Happily Ever After|9627755|Isla and the Happily Ever After (Anna and the French Kiss, #3)|Stephanie Perkins|https://d202m5krfqbpi5.cloudfront.net/books/1362064564s/9627755.jpg|14515040]. Anna and Etienne and Lola and Cricket in Paris for the Olympics! Paris! And it's Isla and Josh, both of whom I really liked.

So okay. Overall, it wasn't wasted time, and I did enjoy the story. It wasn't anything spectacular, though. It had its good points and its faults. As a bridge to book three (which I'm praying will be back up to book one level), it's acceptable. I'll even be happy to see Lola and Cricket again...but I'll be happier to see the others....

Title: Kiss of Frost (Mythos Academy, #2)
Author: Jennifer Estep
My Review:
I'm not sure what it was about this book that kept me reading. There were a lot of things that bugged me pretty continuously throughout, but overall it was an enjoyable story. Downgraded from 4 stars to 3 stars just because of the annoyance factor. I can only guess that it's the characters that keep me glued. I really, really enjoy them, despite the other flaws in this book and the series.

The first, lesser thing that bugged me was the constant repetition. The rehash of book 1 was, in my humble opinion, overdone. I, of all people, really need a reminder about what happened in previous books. I have a terrible memory for books, so I find it very helpful--but this was too much. The key is that I need a reminder, not a complete summary. This was written in such a way that I suppose you could have skipped book 1 entirely, even though it's distinctly a continuing story through each book, not just standalone stories connected by common characters. In addition to the plot rehash, the book is filled with constant reminders about the characters--reminding us multiple times that Gwen's power is called psychometry (yeah, I know), that Logan is notorious for sleeping around (yeah, I know), that Daphne is really strong because of being a Valkyrie (yeah, I know)...I could go on. I might expect this stuff in middle grade, but for an older YA book (I categorize it here simply because of its uncensored references to sex), it was a bit much.

The main thing, though, is that Gwen Frost was very nearly Too Stupid To Live. I swear, I wanted to strangle her. I can forgive her for not telling anyone about the SUV, because she has genuine reason--not wanting anyone to know she's sneaking off campus to see her grandma. Okay, valid. But the arrow in the library? Even after she promises Daphne she'll tell Professor Metis, she doesn't. And for what? Some idiotic idea that she needs to handle things herself? And she doesn't for a second consider the danger to the other students if there's another Reaper on campus. Yep. TSTL. I won't even go into the wolf and Preston and Oliver and her reckless disregard for common sense in those instances. If you read it/have read it, you'll know what I mean.

And to be honest, the plot would have been almost nonexistent if Gwen hadn't been so dumb, which made the conflict fall a bit flat for me. I would rather have conflict built up for valid reasons rather than a main character who simply didn't speak up for the stupidest of reasons.

But I'm being harsh here. Even with all of the above, the book was enjoyable. I was eager to see how Gwen used her powers next, to watch her learn to fight, to see her friendships with Daphne and Carson develop, to see if Logan would finally get his head out of his ass. Not to see how the plot turned out, because I honestly guessed the two major "twists" long before they were revealed, and the main plot was pretty unexceptional. It wasn't what I was reading for.

The REAL story came after the bad guy was caught. No joke. I enjoyed the reading, but the few chapters after we find out who was trying to kill her were the best of the book. There's real conflict. We learn real things. And yeah, Logan gives her that necklace, ohmigodadorable. If those chapters hadn't been there, I might not have gone straight to book 3, but they spurred me onward, and I'm hopeful that Gwen has kicked her amateur ways and is ready to play with the big girls.

Title: Dark Frost (Mythos Academy, #3)
Author: Jennifer Estep
My Review:
This was my favorite of the series so far, because there was a solid, interesting, and suspenseful plot built on actual conflict. Loki's followers want this dagger that will allow them to free him, and Gwen's got to find it before they do. It's simple and direct, and it worked really well. Unlike book 2, she's actually communicating with her friends and the adults around her, following all the proper channels, and yet she still gets embroiled in some exciting stuff. Loved it.

The characters, as always, were my favorite part. Finally getting to see Daphne's magic quicken, Nott, waiting anxiously to see what would happen with Gwen and Logan this time around, new or continuing or unlikely friendships with others...good stuff. And I'd just like to say that I totally knew that Nickamedes had this Snape thing going on. The author put way too much emphasis on the fact that he seemed to hate Gwen for there to not be something like that in the backstory. At least he finally fessed up and wasn't a total coward about it like Snape.

The developments with Gwen's powers are also really interesting. I like that she understands her psychometry and uses it well, but that there are other, related powers that she doesn't know or understand. And I particularly like that there's a potential dark side to it all, even the psychometry. I appreciate that the author delves into Gwen's feelings about prying into other people's thoughts, given the fact that she can't control it, but she is also often eager to use it to find out people's secrets.

Overall? I'd definitely recommend this series. Of course, the sucky thing is that now I've got to wait for book 4.

Title: Annie's Song
Author: Catherine Anderson
My Review: I enjoyed this book, and the characters were great, but the conflict was a little too light for my taste. After about half of the book, I knew that all problems would be solved, so I stopped worrying. Everything was a little too easily accomplished. Decent, but not my favorite.

Title: Yours Until Dawn
Author: Teresa Medeiros
My Review:
I debated a little bit on whether to rate this book with 3 or 4 stars, and ultimately I decided on 3 because, while I enjoyed it, it just didn't have the lasting appeal for books I would stick in my 4 star (really, really liked) and 5 star (LOVED) categories.

The characters in this book were great. I felt both of them truly deserved their roles as the hero and heroine, and there was never a time when I just wanted to strangle them because they were being so unbelievably stupid that I couldn't stand it. There were misunderstandings, but never because of radically terrible assumptions on both parts. They were worthy of one another's love.

The part that didn't quite work for me involved the revelation of the heroine's secrets. It wasn't terrible, but there were a few places where, looking back, I felt a little cheated by the point of view. Even outside of that, it just wasn't anything special, and it ultimately didn't present any real challenge to resolving the romance.

This book won't go on my favorites shelf or anything, and I'm not sure I'd ever reread it, but I passed an enjoyable evening reading it, which is always an acceptable outcome for a book!

Title: Embrace (The Embrace Series, #1)
Author: Jessica Shirvington
My Review:
There were things I liked about this book. There were things that annoyed me about this book. And there were things that were so "meh" about this book that I just didn't care.

Overall, the "meh" won over.

I read this book in one sitting, but there were several times I almost put it down. It was only sheer force of determination to finish it that kept me from setting it aside--and I can almost guarantee I wouldn't have come back to it if I had. That said, it wasn't an awful book. I didn't want to throw it across the room at any point, nor did I immediately add Ms. Shirvington to my DO NOT READ list. I think I can chalk my ambivalence up to a few key things:

1) I felt like the author was good at the initial tug on my heart strings, but not so good with the follow-through. Just when I'd start to get interested and invested in one of Violet's challenges, the focus would shift elsewhere. Am I supposed to focus on the mystery of her dead mother's note? The bad stuff that happened to her at her old school? Her anger at Lincoln? Her confusion about Phoenix? The decision she has to make about embracing? The dead acquaintance? The dead Grigori? There was simply too much going on for each element to get its due, and it did not seem to blend into a seamless story.

2) I don't think the stakes were high enough or clear enough. Okay, sure, she wants to live a "normal" life, and being half angel might get in the way of that...but Violet doesn't really have a normal life to start. Her family is well off, her dad is never around and pretty much gives her the run of things, and she's spending most of her free time either doing artwork or hanging out with her crush, who lives alone in a warehouse flat and is many years older than her (more on that in a bit). So I don't buy that she's clinging to normalcy, even with the issues lingering from her past. And as far as what she has to do if and when she turns Grigori, well, they never get much into the specifics beyond some vague ideas that she'll have to send the exiled back to the angel realm. Considering how much time Lincoln has to spend with her (including not only training, but a couple of parties), I think it's fair to say that she wouldn't be leaving behind her life entirely.

A couple of people and a couple of Grigori die, but they're sort of faceless, and you have no real reason to care beyond Lincoln and Griffin caring. I can imagine why it's all such a big deal, but I don't feel like the book showed me why it was.

3) This sort of goes hand-in-hand with #1, but there were a ton of characters and barely any of them got enough screen time to make me care. Even the ones who did get screen time seemed almost excessive. Like Griffin's partner, whose name I even forget...she had no real function other than not liking Violet.

4) I know I was supposed to like Phoenix, but I just didn't. Even before I knew who/what he was, what he was doing, why he was like he was, etc., I still didn't like him. I felt a little sorry for him, and I don't mind him as a character. In fact, if I were planning to read the sequels, I would want to see more of him--as a character, but not as a love interest. The fact that Violet did like him made me like her less.

If you adore angel-centric books, then I won’t say you shouldn’t give this a try. It might be your thing…it just wasn’t mine.

Full review is here.

Title: Georgiana Darcy's Diary: Jane Austen's Pride and Prejudice Continued (Pride and Prejudice Chronicles, #1)
Author: Anna Elliott
My Review: I really enjoyed this P&P sequel. Georgiana and Colonel Fitzwilliam are some of my favorites from the original, and getting their story was very satisfying. And there's a sequel! I also really enjoyed Georgiana's relationship with Anne de Bourgh and how both of them really grew and changed over the course of the story. Very nice.

Title: Beautiful Creatures (Beautiful Creatures, #1)
Author: Kami Garcia
Publisher: Little, Brown Books for Young Readers
My Review:
I honestly didn't know what to expect from this book. Until I saw the trailer for the movie, I didn't even really know what it was about--and the first trailer didn't clarify that much. Still, it's been on my list for a couple of years, ever since a friend recommended it, and I'm glad I finally started it.

I loved that this book was told from a guy's point of view. You don't see that much in YA books, especially YA paranormal with a healthy dose of romance. It was really refreshing in a way that I never expected it would be. Being set in the South was nice, too. Even though it's not the South that I know, it had enough in common with my childhood experiences and general knowledge of my homeland that I felt like I was going home to visit family every time I started reading again.

I couldn't put this book down. I would pick it up to just read a few pages, and then end up reading for hours. I didn't expect to finish it today, and yet here I am! Overall, it was a good read, and I was happy to see that there were some bits and pieces that I didn't see coming to balance out the things I saw from a million miles away.

Ethan and Lena were great characters, and they showed realistic doses of strength, teenage irrational behavior, and vulnerability. Ethan dealing with the death of his mother and his reclusive father, Lena and her upcoming birthday, the interesting dynamic between Lena and Ridley, Mason's wolf dog, Amma and her mystical protection charms--it all added up to a charming, engrossing and entertaining story.

I don't think I'll go immediately into the sequel, just because I don't want my time to be sucked away again just yet! I bought all of the rest of them, though, so they're on deck and waiting for me.

Title: Beautiful Darkness (Caster Chronicles, #2)
Author: Kami Garcia
Publisher: Little, Brown Books for Young Readers

Title: Contract for Marriage
Author: Barbara DeLeo
My Review: This was a pretty good little book. Short, fairly simplistic, and fluffy, but a decent read. It wasn't hard to see where every scene was going, but since I didn't go in expecting anything more than that, I wasn't disappointed. Worth reading to pass a pleasant hour or two.

Title: Having My Baby
Author: Theresa Ragan
My Review: Cute story. It was fluffy and just complex enough to be satisfying, though certainly not groundbreaking. The secondary characters were nice, too. I'd read another by this author if I was in the mood for something quick and easy. 3.5 stars.

Author: Alison Arngrim

Title: Murder on a Girls' Night Out (Southern Sisters Mystery, #1)
Author: Anne George

Title: Dream Chaser
Author: Angie Stanton
My Review: I picked up this book again in a fit of insomnia, needing an easy read that I knew would be satisfying. It didn't disappoint on the second go-round, either. I like Willow's drive to succeed and her supportive (and yet weird) family. I like that, while there's some confict with Eli that's driven by the past, most of the story is pulled along by Willow's own fears and insecurities. It's all very natural and relatable.
Private Notes: First read date March 26, 2012

Title: Initiation (The Warrior, #1)
Author: Rebecca Royce
My Review:
I read the sample for this book ages ago, when I went on a sampling rampage, and it's been on my TBR list ever since. I can't say that it didn't live up to the sampled bit, because the writing and characters were consistently good.

The plot didn't do quite what I expected (which is not necessarily a bad thing). There were almost three separate sections based on where Rachel was located at a given time, and while it wasn't bad, it introduced a lot more in the way of subplots than perhaps were appropriate for a story of this length. And when I say "subplots," I really mean romantic interests. They had varying degrees of acknowledgement by Rachel, but I think there were 4 potential guys by the end. That's awfully ambitious for a girl who's apparently never had that kind of attention before!

Overall, I liked this book. Not as much as I was expecting to like it, but enough that I'm planning to buy the sequel.

Title: Divergent (Divergent, #1)
Author: Veronica Roth
My Review:
My one complaint about this book is that the premise is a bit far-fetched. In some dystopian worlds, you can picture how the world got to the point it's at, but this one is...well, almost ridiculous at times. I find it hard to believe that things hadn't broken down sooner.

But overall, it was a good read, and I enjoyed the characters. Some of them, especially villains, were of the mustache-twirling, evil-laughing type, but I can can suspend my disbelief on their characterization a lot better than I can on the world...and I managed that, too. Tris was great, ditto Four, and I liked how characters like Christina, Will and Al were friends but truly not perfect. And Uriah...and his brother, whose name I forget--I'd love to see more of them. I'm curious to see where everyone ends up for the second one, since not all of the characters were with Tris at the end.

I will be picking up the second book tout suite.

Title: Insurgent (Divergent, #2)
Author: Veronica Roth
My Review:
I enjoyed this one enough for 4 stars, but I didn't like it quite as much as the first one. I think the first one was more coherent, with its focus around Tris's initiation, and this one jumped around quite a bit more. Also, I had to yell at Tris a bunch for being an idiot. Why do so many YA heroines insist on repeatedly doing things like lying to their friends/loved ones before rushing off to do stupid things that most often do not work???

But it was enjoyable, and a fast read. The ending leaves a big fat opening for a sequel (which, with the way things were wrapping up, I wasn't sure was gonna be there), but it resolves enough that I'm satisfied with the ending. I'll be happy to pick up #3 next year.

Title: Lure (Siren's Call, #1)
Author: Stephanie Jenkins
My Review:
I love reading new twists on old tales, whether that be fairy tales, mythology, classic novels, etc. Unfortunately, many of those new stories fall short. I'm not sure if the writers rely too much on the source material to pull the story along, or what, but I've read some disappointing ones, even by seasoned and favorite authors.

This is not one of those poorly-executed attempts.

I bought this book today and devoured it within a few hours. Couldn't put it down. I love that the major characters are Sirens--they have dealings with some of the major Greek mythology players, but the story isn't about the majors. We get to read about the "little guys," so to speak. Char's handling of her new "job" and how she juggles it with friends, not-boyfriends, her waitressing job and her slightly overbearing father is entertaining and engrossing.

The romance builds steadily, but at no point did I want to roll my eyes at Char's obsession (because there wasn't one), and I never wanted to flat-out kick the boys involved. (These are big pluses, because I can't say the same for every YA I've read.) What can I say? I loved the boy plenty right along with her. Her friends were interesting, but they didn't take over the story. Her mentor didn't info-dump the whole of the explanation/backstory, but her holding of information was believable and not annoying.

I'm only left with one question: when's the sequel going to be out?

Title: Fallen (Fallen, #1)
Author: Lauren Kate
My Review:
This book wasn't bad. I can't say that I was super impressed with it, but it wasn't boring, and I didn't want to throw my kindle across the room at the end. (I guess it's pretty sad when those are my current criteria for a decent book, but what can ya do?)

I enjoyed the setup and the concept, and the characters were pretty good. My only real complaint, and my reason for deducting two stars, is that I didn't really feel any building of tension. I know that Luce sees these black shapes, and that she isn't sure if she's crazy, and she has to choose between these two boys...but I didn't feel the weight of what would happen should she choose wrong. Even at the end, there were scenes that *should* have had an impact, but they sort of fell flat for me. They weren't boring, but they weren't tense.

I won't be recommending this one to anyone, but if it's already on your TBR list, give it a try. It wasn't a terrible way to pass a few hours.

Title: Rippler (Ripple #1)
Author: Cidney Swanson
My Review: I felt like this book left a lot unfinished. It wasn't a cliffhanger, exactly, but I don't feel like there was any plot thread that it followed. There was no real rising action, no climax, no conclusion. It was just a bunch of scenes about the characters, and they did some things that were loosely related. Granted, I liked the characters, and they did interesting things, but it loses a star for the lack of any real cohesive, complete story. I will, however, be reading the sequel, because the characters and the bits and pieces of the world building that were revealed in this book were intriguing. The writing (aside from the plotting) was great, and I'm hoping that book 2 will get that extra star.

Author: Susan Bischoff
My Review: Characters were great, story flowed nicely, and it had a satisfying ending. My only real issue is that it felt too short. I was really surprised when it ended, because I thought it would have at least a few more chapters. It wasn't unfinished--thank goodness for no cliffhanger--but the plot wasn't particularly complex. I'm interested to see what's done with the sequel.

Title: Beautiful Disaster (Beautiful, #1)
Author: Jamie McGuire
My Review:
I enjoyed this, particularly because I like a healthy dose of angst before my HEA. However, I think it could have been cut down a bit, and I would have liked it better. Not because it was too long--because I blew through it, despite the length--but because I felt like it reached a natural sort of resolution around 50% through, and then it almost started another story. I understood and appreciated that the story was designed to take us through the entirety of their courtship, so to speak, but the last half felt like too much crammed into one book.

Still, good book. Well-written, good characters, interesting plot. I won't be actively recommending it, but if you think it sounds like your kind of thing, I say go for it.
